showed in Kaci and Bouabana-Tebibel (2014) that the search
time is independent of the size of the whole collection. So,
we evaluate the search time variation according to thesize of lists of documents containing the searchedkeywords.
8.2.1. xSE-ACAS performance based on KP-ABE
In this experiment, we study the search time variation ac-cording to the number of occurrences of searched keywords inthe data collection, as shown in Table 8 . We executed search
queries for the two models SE-ACAS (without parallelization)and xSE-ACAS (on a cluster formed of 32 nodes) according to
the scenarios of Fig. 11 . The results of the experiments are
presented in Fig. 13 . They show a considerable decrease in the
search time for xSE-ACAS thanks to the parallelization pro-cess. This time is of about 4.7 s for keywords distributed over12000 documents. It decreases linearly with the increase ofthe cluster nodes.
8.2.2. xSE-ACAS performance based on CP-ABE
We conducted for this experiment the same tests as thosecarried out in section 8.2.1 for KP-ABE, but considering, thistime, the CP-ABE variant. The experiment results are presented in Fig. 14 . They are similar to those obtained with
the KP-ABE variant regarding the considerable improvementin the search time using parallelization. However, thesearch time for SE-ACAS is better under CP-ABE relative toKP-ABE.
8.3. xSE-ACAS performance based on KP-ABE and CP-
ABE
In this experiment, we analyze the performance of xSE-ACAS
under KP-ABE and CP-ABE variants. To deal with this test, weaugment the size of the search space. The used scenarios arepresented in Table 9 . We consider queries composed of five
keywords.
The search time curves for xSE-ACAS according to the two
ABE variants are shown in Fig. 15 . Their progression is similar,except that the search time with CP-ABE is shorter than that
with KP-ABE, thus electing xSE-ACAS based CP-ABE as a moreefficient technique.

9. Conclusion
We proposed, in this article, a model of search on encrypteddata for the Cloud Computing, whose main purpose is toenhance the access control on data. Integration of SearchableEncryption with access control mechanisms ensures, on onehand, confidentiality of data at both Cloud and user sides andlimits, on the other hand, data access only to authorized
users. However, this hybridization failed to satisfy the ACAS
property.
To remedy this, we proposed to move the access control
mechanism to the side of the Cloud, thus providing a newxSE-ACAS model, which reduces, furthermore, the decryp-tion time by parallelization over the HPC cluster. xSE-ACAShas also been conceived to take advantage of HPC in orderto speed up users 'requests. To achieve this, the SSE-1 search
method has been revised to support parallelization. Theperformance study showed a considerable improvement ofthe search time.
xSE-ACAS also integrates SSE-1 with two ABE techniques,
namely, Key-Policy Attribute Based Encryption (KP-ABE) and
Ciphertext-Policy Attribute Based Encryption (CP-ABE). A so-lution analysis highlighted that CP-ABE is well designed toidentify undesirable access relative to KP-ABE. Furthermore,experiments showed that the search time with CP-ABE isbetter than that provided with KP-ABE. Finally, xSE-ACAS

1-s2.0-S0167404816301377-main.pdf.title: A survey of cloud computing data integrity schemes: Design challenges, taxonomy andfuture trends
1-s2.0-S0167404816301377-main.pdf.abstract:
Cloud computing has gained tremendous popularity in recent years. By outsourcing computation and storage requirements to public providers and paying for the services used,customers can relish upon the advantages of this new paradigm. Cloud computing pro-vides with a compar ably lower -cost, scalable, a location-independent platform for managing
clients' data. Compared to a traditional model of computing, which uses dedicated in-house infrastructure, cloud computing provides unprecedented benefits regarding cost andreliability. Cloud storage is a new cost-effective paradigm that aims at providing high avail-ability, reliability, massive scalability and data sharing. However, outsourcing data to a cloudservice provider introduces new challenges from the perspectives of data correctness andsecurity. Over the years, many data integrity schemes have been proposed for protectingoutsourced data. This paper aims to enhance the understanding of security issues associ-ated with cloud storage and highlights the importance of data integrity schemes for outsourceddata. In this paper, we have presented a taxonomy of existing data integrity schemes usefor cloud storage. A comparative analysis of existing schemes is also provided along witha detailed discussion on possible security attacks and their mitigations. Additionally, wehave discussed design challenges such as computational efficiency, storage efficiency, com-munication efficiency, and reduced I/O in these schemes. Furthermore, we have highlightedfuture trends and open issues, for future research in cloud storage security.
1-s2.0-S0167404816301377-main.pdf.intro_text:
1. Introduction
Cloud computing provides a flexible and cost-effective solution for many services through Internet ( Nepal et al., 2011 ). It
is considered as a major Information Technology (IT) shift anda latest model of computing over pooled computing resources such as bandwidth, storage, servers, processing power,services, and applications. Today, this new model has gainedtremendous popularity and is receiving a lot of attention fromresearchers in the academic and industrial communities. Es-sential characteristics of the cloud-computing model include
E-mail addresses: faheemiiui@gmail.com (F. Zafar), abidkhan@comsats.edu.pk (A. Khan), saif_ur_rehman@comsats.edu.pk (S.U.R. Malik),
mansoor@comsats.edu.pk (M. Ahmed), adeel.anjum@comsats.edu.pk (A. Anjum), majid_iqbal@comsats.edu.pk (M. Iqbal), nadeemjavaid@
comsats.edu.pk (N. Javed), masoom.alam@comsats.edu.pk (M. Alam), fuzel01@yahoo.com (F. Jamil).
ScienceDirec t

on-demand self-service, rapid elasticity, resource pooling and
broad network access. Cloud computing has gained a lot ofpopularity, which is mainly due to the following reasons, asdiscussed in
Armbrust et al. (2009) : (a) Cloud computing has
eliminated the overhead of planning from the user, provid-ing resources that are available on-demand, self-service, andthe ability to scale according to requirements. (b) Cloud com-puting has eliminated up-front commitment by the end users.Pay-as-you-go model has allowed companies to start small andincrease their computing resources only when needed. The de-ployment models used by cloud computing are Private Cloud(internal datacenters of business or organizations which arenot available for general public use) (
Armbrust et al., 2009 ), Public
Cloud (private cloud made accessible for general public use onpay-as-you-go model) (
Armbrust et al., 2009 ), and Hybrid/Multi-
Cloud (cloud computing environment in which an organizationprovides and manages some resources in-house, and the otherservices are provided externally) (
Zhu et al., 2011 ). Cloud computing services are organized as Infrastructure as a Service(IaaS), Platform as a Service (PaaS) and Software as a Service(SaaS). Under the umbrella of these services a number ofnew services have also emerged, e.g. Data Integrity as aService(DIaaS) (
Nepal et al., 2011 ), Database as a Service ( ALzain
and Pardede, 2011; Arasu et al., 2013; Curino et al., 2011; Seiboldand Kemper, 2012
), Logging as a Service ( Frenot and Ponge, 2012;
Zawoad et al., 2013, 2015 ), Provenance as a Service ( Hammadand Wu, 2014 ), Security as a Service ( Al-Aqrabi et al., 2012;
Hussain and Abdulsalam, 2011 ), Big Data as a Service ( Zheng
et al., 2013 ) and Storage as a Service ( Calder et al., 2011; Nabeel
and Bertino, 2012 ). An overview of cloud architecture is shown
inFig. 1 .
Cloud Storage as a service is a growing trend with features like elasticity, pay-as-you-go, business continuity withlong-term retention and risk mitigation through disaster re-covery (
Nepal et al., 2011 ). Not all these features are available
with on-premises storage. Popular cloud-based storage ser-vices available today are Dropbox, One Drive, Amazon S3, GoogleDrive, Box, and Sugar Sync etc. Nowadays, to improve busi-ness strategies, organizations use analysis techniques over theirhistorical data (
Liu et al., 2014 ). Some business sectors, for instance telecom and e-health, have compliance requirements(
Luxton et al., 2012 ), which bind them to keep historical data
over a specified period. Not every organization is equipped tomanage large secondary storage or build their private datacenters (because of the cost associated with building and main-taining such infrastructure). Cloud storage can be of great serviceto such organizations because of its flexible model. However,the loss of control is an inherent issue with outsourced datastorage model. Although the cloud service provider (CSP) isbounded by a service level agreement (SLA) to ensure datasecurity, users cannot solely rely on such agreements. Fur-thermore, reliance on a contractual obligation may fail to detect
Fig. 1 -Overview of cloud architecture.30 computers & security 65 (2017) 29-49
the malicious behavior of the service provider. Cloud computing operational details are not transparent to the customersand the CSP may be untrusted. Therefore, besides the conve-nience provided by cloud model, data security issues such asconfidentiality, privacy, and data integrity are also associatedwith cloud storage service model. Data can be manipulated orlost due to accidental or intentional malicious activity, whichcan be a nightmare for the user and an embarrassment forcloud service provider. Cloud has a provision of "multi-tenancy" i.e. cloud resources will be shared and utilized bymultiple users; therefore, adversaries can take advantage ofvulnerabilities in the cloud (
Ristenpart et al., 2009 ). Administration errors can also damage data; for example, failures indata migration or backup/restore process. Consequently, dataintegrity is at the core of outsourcing data over the cloudstorage. Many data integrity schemes have been developed forensuring correctness of outsourced data in recent years. Whenthe integrity of outsourced data is to be checked, it is not fea-sible to download all data from the remote server and verifyas it incurs high communication and computational cost. Toavoid this high cost, most of the data integrity schemes performblockless verification (
Ateniese et al., 2007 ). This is done without
downloading actual data from the cloud, rather it is based onsome metadata, which is generated by using original data beforeit is outsourced. Later on, using this metadata, proofs for in-tegrity verification are generated by cloud service provider andverified by the data owner. Research on data integrity schemesstarted from work on static data or append-only data and thenextended to dynamic data (supporting CRUD operations onblock level) (
Ateniese et al., 2008 ). Initially, private (single user/
data owner) verifiability was supported and later on, public(multiple users) verifiability (
Wang et al., 2012 ) was introduced with or without third party auditor (TPA). Privacy issuessuch as leakage of data and user anonymity were identifieddue to the introduction of TPA. Thus, privacy preserving dataintegrity schemes (
Erway et al., 2009; Wang et al., 2012 ) were
developed to overcome the privacy concerns of the users. Ap-plication of these techniques extended from a single copy tomultiple copies of data (
Krzywiecki and Kuty, 2012 ), constraintbased data geolocation (CBDG) ( Rashid et al., 2013 ), multicloud (cloud of clouds) ( Cash et al., 2013 ), data deduplication
(Zheng and Xu, 2012 ) and proof of ownership ( Zhu et al., 2012 ).
Researchers used homomorphic tags, bilinear pairing ( Wang
and Zhang, 2014 ), algebraic signatures ( Yu et al., 2014 ), fountain codes ( Shi et al., 2013 ), erasure codes ( Chen, 2013 ), RS codes
based on Cauchy matrices ( Chen and Curtmola, 2012b ) and
other techniques as metadata for integrity verification. Effi-ciency being a real challenge in data integrity schemes ismeasured regarding computation, communication and storagecosts incurred. One other desirable characteristic of data in-tegrity is unbounded queries (which mean no restriction onhow frequently user can verify data). When data size is large,this desirable characteristic is hard to achieve (
Ateniese et al.,
2008 ). Achieving efficiency sometimes results in a compromise on security ( Chen, 2013 ). Implementation primitives also
impact computation, communication and storage efficiency.Asymmetric cryptographic primitives provide strong secu-rity, but efficiency decreases due to complex computation andlarge keys. This decline in efficiency becomes more promi-nent for large datasets. Disk I/O is another crucial factoraffecting the efficiency of the verification process in data integrity schemes. For allowing data sharing over cloud storage,strong data access control policies are needed to ensure datasecurity. The requirements of a data integrity scheme vary ac-cording to the deployment environment. Schemes designed fora single copy of data are not applicable within server and crossserver replication (
Curtmola et al., 2008 ). The requirements of
data integrity scheme change with cloud deployment model,e.g. hybrid or multi-cloud (
Zhu et al., 2011 ), whereby the scope
of data integrity is extended. Moreover, Service Level Agree-ment (SLA) checking is becoming a fundamental part of dataintegrity.
Existing surveys (
Oualha et al., 2012; Sookhak et al., 2014;
Worku et al., 2012 ) on data integrity schemes have performed only a comparative analysis based on theircharacteristics and discussed their internal implementation.These studies lack general guidelines about (i) the key param-eters which control overall efficiency of data integrity schemesand (ii) designing of an efficient, scalable and secure data in-tegrity scheme.
The key contributions of this paper are:
1. We define a taxonomy, based on the attributes of data integrity schemes; for instance, verification approach, natureof data they deal, nature of metadata they use, implemen-tation primitives used and deployment scenarios, to describeall aspects of data integrity.
2. We identify design challenges of data integrity schemes and
highlighted a set of parameters, which control their overallefficiency. We analyze the impact of these parameters, whichcan be helpful in designing scalable and efficient schemes.
3. We summarize the security aspects (possible attacks due
to vulnerabilities) of data integrity schemes as well as miti-gation techniques.
4. We identify the future trends (e.g. geolocation, assured deletion, computation assurance, deduplication) of researchin the context of data integrity schemes.
5. We perform a comparative analysis of the most recent
existing data integrity schemes based on identifiedcharacteristics.
1.1. Organization of the paper
The paper is organized as follows. In Section 2 , cloud storage
issues are discussed. Section 3 presents a thematic taxonomy of data integrity schemes. In Section 4 , common
characteristics are discussed and a comparative analysis of dataintegrity schemes is provided.
Section 5 covers the design challenges of data integrity schemes. Future research directions indata integrity schemes are presented in
Section 6 .Section 7
concludes the paper.
2. Cloud storage issues and need of data
integrity schemes
Cloud storage is beneficial for both users and service providers. Cloud users incur minimum capital investment and arerelieved from infrastructure maintenance responsibility (
et al., 2011 ). By utilizing cloud storage, a user is provided
location-independent access to services ( Armbrust et al., 2009 ).
Meanwhile, a CSP gains high revenue over available infrastruc-ture by sharing it among multiple users. This high level ofresource utilization leads to optimized energy consumption.Despite these benefits, cloud storage has some inherent weak-nesses, as CSP is always assumed untrusting, which may havemalicious motivations. A CSP may behave maliciously eitherintentionally (charging for all data and deleting the un-accessed data after usage statistics analysis or keeping the fewerreplicas as per commitment) or unintentionally (service pro-vider himself is unaware if bad sectors created in disk or harddisk crashes). The user's concerns about cloud storage are givenbelow (
Chow et al., 2009; Sookhak et al., 2014; Wei et al., 2014 ):
1-s2.0-S0167404816301377-main.pdf.conclusion_text:
8. Conclusion
Cloud storage is a cost effective solution and provides elas-ticity, pay-as-you-go, and business continuity with long-termretention and risk mitigation through disaster recovery. Thesefeatures are not available with on-premises storage. However,cloud storage model has inherent weaknesses; a user out-sourcing data loses control over his data. Data confidentialityand integrity are the major concerns of the user when movingdata from on-premises storage to the cloud storage. Many dataintegrity schemes have been proposed over the years to addressthe security issues associated with cloud storage. In this paper,we have discussed cloud computing and cloud storage. We havealso discussed the cloud storage issues. Our focus in this paperwas to improve the understanding of security aspects of dataintegrity schemes. Schemes proposed over the years are notsuitable for all types of data and environments. Therefore, we45 computers & security 65 (2017) 29-49
establish taxonomy based on attributes that can help the
readers to understand different aspects (nature of data, natureof deployment environment, etc.) of data integrity schemes.The said attributes impact the applicability and efficiency ofschemes in practical scenarios. We provide a comparative analy-sis based on characteristics of data integrity schemes. Weestablish the timeline of the evolution of data integrity schemesand also identify future aspects that researchers can exploitto perform their future research. We identify the design chal-lenges in performance and security of data integrity schemes.We also identify performance-degrading factors of data integ-rity schemes and provide a discussion on mitigation strategiesfor the possible attacks.
Acknowledgments
We are grateful to the anonymous reviewers for their deepinsight in improving the quality of the manuscript. The authorswould like to thank COMSATS Institute of Information tech-nology and Higher Education Commission (HEC) for theirsupport and encouragement.

1. Introduction
Cloud computing provides a flexible and cost-effective solution for many services through Internet ( Nepal et al., 2011 ). It
is considered as a major Information Technology (IT) shift anda latest model of computing over pooled computing resources such as bandwidth, storage, servers, processing power,services, and applications. Today, this new model has gainedtremendous popularity and is receiving a lot of attention fromresearchers in the academic and industrial communities. Es-sential characteristics of the cloud-computing model include
E-mail addresses: faheemiiui@gmail.com (F. Zafar), abidkhan@comsats.edu.pk (A. Khan), saif_ur_rehman@comsats.edu.pk (S.U.R. Malik),
mansoor@comsats.edu.pk (M. Ahmed), adeel.anjum@comsats.edu.pk (A. Anjum), majid_iqbal@comsats.edu.pk (M. Iqbal), nadeemjavaid@
comsats.edu.pk (N. Javed), masoom.alam@comsats.edu.pk (M. Alam), fuzel01@yahoo.com (F. Jamil).
ScienceDirec t

on-demand self-service, rapid elasticity, resource pooling and
broad network access. Cloud computing has gained a lot ofpopularity, which is mainly due to the following reasons, asdiscussed in
Armbrust et al. (2009) : (a) Cloud computing has
eliminated the overhead of planning from the user, provid-ing resources that are available on-demand, self-service, andthe ability to scale according to requirements. (b) Cloud com-puting has eliminated up-front commitment by the end users.Pay-as-you-go model has allowed companies to start small andincrease their computing resources only when needed. The de-ployment models used by cloud computing are Private Cloud(internal datacenters of business or organizations which arenot available for general public use) (
Armbrust et al., 2009 ), Public
Cloud (private cloud made accessible for general public use onpay-as-you-go model) (
Armbrust et al., 2009 ), and Hybrid/Multi-
Cloud (cloud computing environment in which an organizationprovides and manages some resources in-house, and the otherservices are provided externally) (
Zhu et al., 2011 ). Cloud computing services are organized as Infrastructure as a Service(IaaS), Platform as a Service (PaaS) and Software as a Service(SaaS). Under the umbrella of these services a number ofnew services have also emerged, e.g. Data Integrity as aService(DIaaS) (
Nepal et al., 2011 ), Database as a Service ( ALzain
and Pardede, 2011; Arasu et al., 2013; Curino et al., 2011; Seiboldand Kemper, 2012
), Logging as a Service ( Frenot and Ponge, 2012;
Zawoad et al., 2013, 2015 ), Provenance as a Service ( Hammadand Wu, 2014 ), Security as a Service ( Al-Aqrabi et al., 2012;
Hussain and Abdulsalam, 2011 ), Big Data as a Service ( Zheng
et al., 2013 ) and Storage as a Service ( Calder et al., 2011; Nabeel
and Bertino, 2012 ). An overview of cloud architecture is shown
inFig. 1 .
Cloud Storage as a service is a growing trend with features like elasticity, pay-as-you-go, business continuity withlong-term retention and risk mitigation through disaster re-covery (
Nepal et al., 2011 ). Not all these features are available
with on-premises storage. Popular cloud-based storage ser-vices available today are Dropbox, One Drive, Amazon S3, GoogleDrive, Box, and Sugar Sync etc. Nowadays, to improve busi-ness strategies, organizations use analysis techniques over theirhistorical data (
Liu et al., 2014 ). Some business sectors, for instance telecom and e-health, have compliance requirements(
Luxton et al., 2012 ), which bind them to keep historical data
over a specified period. Not every organization is equipped tomanage large secondary storage or build their private datacenters (because of the cost associated with building and main-taining such infrastructure). Cloud storage can be of great serviceto such organizations because of its flexible model. However,the loss of control is an inherent issue with outsourced datastorage model. Although the cloud service provider (CSP) isbounded by a service level agreement (SLA) to ensure datasecurity, users cannot solely rely on such agreements. Fur-thermore, reliance on a contractual obligation may fail to detect
Fig. 1 -Overview of cloud architecture.30 computers & security 65 (2017) 29-49
the malicious behavior of the service provider. Cloud computing operational details are not transparent to the customersand the CSP may be untrusted. Therefore, besides the conve-nience provided by cloud model, data security issues such asconfidentiality, privacy, and data integrity are also associatedwith cloud storage service model. Data can be manipulated orlost due to accidental or intentional malicious activity, whichcan be a nightmare for the user and an embarrassment forcloud service provider. Cloud has a provision of "multi-tenancy" i.e. cloud resources will be shared and utilized bymultiple users; therefore, adversaries can take advantage ofvulnerabilities in the cloud (
Ristenpart et al., 2009 ). Administration errors can also damage data; for example, failures indata migration or backup/restore process. Consequently, dataintegrity is at the core of outsourcing data over the cloudstorage. Many data integrity schemes have been developed forensuring correctness of outsourced data in recent years. Whenthe integrity of outsourced data is to be checked, it is not fea-sible to download all data from the remote server and verifyas it incurs high communication and computational cost. Toavoid this high cost, most of the data integrity schemes performblockless verification (
Ateniese et al., 2007 ). This is done without
downloading actual data from the cloud, rather it is based onsome metadata, which is generated by using original data beforeit is outsourced. Later on, using this metadata, proofs for in-tegrity verification are generated by cloud service provider andverified by the data owner. Research on data integrity schemesstarted from work on static data or append-only data and thenextended to dynamic data (supporting CRUD operations onblock level) (
Ateniese et al., 2008 ). Initially, private (single user/
data owner) verifiability was supported and later on, public(multiple users) verifiability (
Wang et al., 2012 ) was introduced with or without third party auditor (TPA). Privacy issuessuch as leakage of data and user anonymity were identifieddue to the introduction of TPA. Thus, privacy preserving dataintegrity schemes (
Erway et al., 2009; Wang et al., 2012 ) were
developed to overcome the privacy concerns of the users. Ap-plication of these techniques extended from a single copy tomultiple copies of data (
Krzywiecki and Kuty, 2012 ), constraintbased data geolocation (CBDG) ( Rashid et al., 2013 ), multicloud (cloud of clouds) ( Cash et al., 2013 ), data deduplication
(Zheng and Xu, 2012 ) and proof of ownership ( Zhu et al., 2012 ).
Researchers used homomorphic tags, bilinear pairing ( Wang
and Zhang, 2014 ), algebraic signatures ( Yu et al., 2014 ), fountain codes ( Shi et al., 2013 ), erasure codes ( Chen, 2013 ), RS codes
based on Cauchy matrices ( Chen and Curtmola, 2012b ) and
other techniques as metadata for integrity verification. Effi-ciency being a real challenge in data integrity schemes ismeasured regarding computation, communication and storagecosts incurred. One other desirable characteristic of data in-tegrity is unbounded queries (which mean no restriction onhow frequently user can verify data). When data size is large,this desirable characteristic is hard to achieve (
Ateniese et al.,
2008 ). Achieving efficiency sometimes results in a compromise on security ( Chen, 2013 ). Implementation primitives also
impact computation, communication and storage efficiency.Asymmetric cryptographic primitives provide strong secu-rity, but efficiency decreases due to complex computation andlarge keys. This decline in efficiency becomes more promi-nent for large datasets. Disk I/O is another crucial factoraffecting the efficiency of the verification process in data integrity schemes. For allowing data sharing over cloud storage,strong data access control policies are needed to ensure datasecurity. The requirements of a data integrity scheme vary ac-cording to the deployment environment. Schemes designed fora single copy of data are not applicable within server and crossserver replication (
Curtmola et al., 2008 ). The requirements of
data integrity scheme change with cloud deployment model,e.g. hybrid or multi-cloud (
Zhu et al., 2011 ), whereby the scope
of data integrity is extended. Moreover, Service Level Agree-ment (SLA) checking is becoming a fundamental part of dataintegrity.
Existing surveys (
Oualha et al., 2012; Sookhak et al., 2014;
Worku et al., 2012 ) on data integrity schemes have performed only a comparative analysis based on theircharacteristics and discussed their internal implementation.These studies lack general guidelines about (i) the key param-eters which control overall efficiency of data integrity schemesand (ii) designing of an efficient, scalable and secure data in-tegrity scheme.
The key contributions of this paper are:
1. We define a taxonomy, based on the attributes of data integrity schemes; for instance, verification approach, natureof data they deal, nature of metadata they use, implemen-tation primitives used and deployment scenarios, to describeall aspects of data integrity.
2. We identify design challenges of data integrity schemes and
highlighted a set of parameters, which control their overallefficiency. We analyze the impact of these parameters, whichcan be helpful in designing scalable and efficient schemes.
3. We summarize the security aspects (possible attacks due
to vulnerabilities) of data integrity schemes as well as miti-gation techniques.
4. We identify the future trends (e.g. geolocation, assured deletion, computation assurance, deduplication) of researchin the context of data integrity schemes.
5. We perform a comparative analysis of the most recent
existing data integrity schemes based on identifiedcharacteristics.
1.1. Organization of the paper
The paper is organized as follows. In Section 2 , cloud storage
issues are discussed. Section 3 presents a thematic taxonomy of data integrity schemes. In Section 4 , common
characteristics are discussed and a comparative analysis of dataintegrity schemes is provided.
Section 5 covers the design challenges of data integrity schemes. Future research directions indata integrity schemes are presented in
Section 6 .Section 7
concludes the paper.
2. Cloud storage issues and need of data
integrity schemes
Cloud storage is beneficial for both users and service providers. Cloud users incur minimum capital investment and arerelieved from infrastructure maintenance responsibility (
et al., 2011 ). By utilizing cloud storage, a user is provided
location-independent access to services ( Armbrust et al., 2009 ).
Meanwhile, a CSP gains high revenue over available infrastruc-ture by sharing it among multiple users. This high level ofresource utilization leads to optimized energy consumption.Despite these benefits, cloud storage has some inherent weak-nesses, as CSP is always assumed untrusting, which may havemalicious motivations. A CSP may behave maliciously eitherintentionally (charging for all data and deleting the un-accessed data after usage statistics analysis or keeping the fewerreplicas as per commitment) or unintentionally (service pro-vider himself is unaware if bad sectors created in disk or harddisk crashes). The user's concerns about cloud storage are givenbelow (
Chow et al., 2009; Sookhak et al., 2014; Wei et al., 2014 ):

1.Unauthorized physical access
In cloud-based storage, data may be scattered across multiple servers and locations. The user loses control over his dataand is unable to inspect the data links visually. Therefore, therisk of unauthorized physical access is one of the issues of cloudstorage. Re-allocation of storage space, disposal of old equip-ment or reuse of drives can result in data breaches.
2.Cloud storage provider espionage
Users are usually concerned that service providers may
attempt to obtain sensitive information from their data withouthis consent or knowledge. Especially when data contains con-fidential information such as credit card numbers, medical data,etc., users hesitate to adopt cloud storage.
3.Data lock-in
No standards exist that define how data should be stored
and maintained in the cloud. Every CSP has its proprietaryformat for data storage. Therefore, users get bound to aparticular CSP and switching to another is not simple. Fre-quent changes in cloud-based services also cause problems forusers.
4.Cloud storage provider stability
Cloud storage claims to provide high availability. However,
what about the CSP stability? This includes:
 How many services provided by a CSP are stable and how
frequently do they change?
 Is the CSP capable of mitigating natural disasters? Reliability of other company's services hired by the CSP? What if the CSP goes bankrupt?
All these questions can result in user hesitation for the adoption of cloud storage.
5.Accessibility effectiveness or high availability
The effectiveness of data access from the cloud depends
on the performance of network and WAN bandwidth. The choiceof network hardware and algorithms used by the CSP and thelevel of precautions taken affect the reliability and availability of data. One major obstacle in the adoption of public cloudfrom the user perspective is high availability, as pointed byauthors in
Armbrust et al. (2009) . The assurance of high availability and QoS is part of SLA, and the end user does not haveto worry about how it is provided. For example, CSP may haveagreed with the user to provide a level of 99.95% availability,i.e. 4.38 hours of downtime is to be expected in a year.
6.Security against internal and external network attacks
When user data leaves the premises, the risk of attack it
is subjected to is also increased. At the CSP end, user's datais insecure and exposed to internal and external attacks duringdata transmission and retrieval. Malicious users can damageother user's data as cloud resources are shared among mul-tiple users. Faulty equipment, hardware or software bug orcriminal activity may result in data security breaches. Securechannels are used to ensure that the data transmission is pro-tected. Furthermore, encryption of data also helps to preservedata confidentiality.
7.Cloud computation reliability
How can a user be ensured that CSP is faithfully performing the computations and giving correct results? Trustedhardware or software is used which provides proof of compu-tation authenticity.
8.Cloud forensic investigation: a real challenge
Digital forensics investigation is a challenging task for anysecurity incident due to the multi-tenancy environment anddecentralized data processing in cloud computing. Tradi-tional techniques of investigation and evidence collectionare not practical in the context of a cloud environment. Na-tional Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST) defines"Digital Forensic" as a science of identification of an inci-dent, collection, examination and analysis of evidence datawhile keeping the integrity of evidence information intactand maintaining the strict chain of custody of data (
Kent
et al., 2006 ). The nature of forensic evidence data varies according to cloud services and deployment model. From atechnical perspective, evidence data can be at "rest", "motion"or "execution" state (
Birk, 2011 ). Usually, data on disk storage
are considered to be in the rest state. Virtual machines' ac-tivity logs are an example of evidence data at rest and couldbe used for digital forensics (
Zawoad et al., 2015 ). Data traveling over the network from one entity/device to other isconsidered to be in motion. Irrespective of the cloud de-ployment model, the following three components serve asa potential source for evidence data in cloud forensic (
Birk,
2011 ):
a.Virtual instance: A virtual instance is assigned to each user
in the cloud and most of the security incidents happen onthis virtual instance. Therefore, it holds evidence data of anysecurity breach and could be analyzed by both the user andCSP. The forensic investigation becomes a real challenge inSaaS and PaaS cloud deployment models, as access to thevirtual instance for evidence data collection is either verylimited or none at all.32 computers & security 65 (2017) 29-49
b.Network layer: Network component logs contain evidence
data for any security breach. Different OSI/ISO network layersfacilitate by providing information on protocols and com-munication for instances within the cloud as well as outsidethe cloud.
c.Client system: The location of potential forensic evidence
data on a client's system layer entirely depends on the se-lected cloud deployment model (Iaas, Paas, Saas). Forexample, a client's browser is an application that is com-municating with SaaS services hosted in the cloud. For anysecurity incident, forensic data collected from the browserenvironment of the client is valuable for exhaustive foren-sic investigation. However, the integrity of forensic data itselfis a challenge.
Despite all the issues mentioned above, the user's primary
concern is that security; confidentiality, privacy, and data in-tegrity are not compromised. The CSP is bounded by SLA andstandards such as HIPAA (
Luxton et al., 2012 ), which are available for user's data security. Nevertheless, there is a need formechanisms to ensure data integrity, which is considered asthe essential requirement for cloud storage adoption.
3. Data integrity schemes
Data integrity schemes, discussed in this survey, are of proba-bilistic nature, tamper evident (not tamper resistant) and, areapplicable only to secondary storage (non-volatile data). In-tegrity schemes for volatile data are out of the scope of thissurvey. There are three entities in a data integrity scheme: (i)Data owner who outsources his data, (ii) a cloud storage pro-vider (CSP) to whom data are outsourced and (iii) an auditorwho verifies the integrity of the data. An auditor can be thedata owner himself or he can delegate this responsibility to
Third Party Auditor (TPA).
Fig. 2 shows the working of a data
integrity scheme protocol. There are two phases in a data in-tegrity protocol:
1. Preprocessing phase: data are preprocessed, and some additional metadata are generated. Then, both the data andmetadata are outsourced to the CSP.
2. Verification phase: an auditor sends a challenge request to
the CSP who generates the proof of possession using thedata and metadata and provides it to the auditor. Then, theauditor verifies the proof, to ensure that the data integrityis intact.
With the help of data integrity schemes, any data corruption or deletion can be timely identified and thus necessarymeasures can be taken to recover the data. To develop a betterunderstanding of data integrity schemes, we have defined athematic taxonomy based on the nature of data they deal, thenature of metadata they use, their capabilities, implementa-tion primitives used and deployment scenarios.
3.1. Taxonomy of data integrity schemes
The following are the attributes taken into consideration fordefining the taxonomy of data integrity schemes. These attri-butes not only describe the capabilities of any scheme but alsogive an idea about their limitations in practical scenarios.
Fig. 3
shows the summarized view of schemes with reference to theseattributes.
Approach: The approach refers to the nature of guarantee
provided by a data integrity scheme, which can be deter-ministic or probabilistic. Deterministic schemes (
Deswarte
Fig. 2 -Data integrity scheme protocol.33 computers & security 65 (2017) 29-49
Fig. 3 -Taxonomy of data integrity schemes.34 computers & security 65 (2017) 29-49
et al., 2004; Han et al., 2014 ) need to access the complete
file to determine the integrity and give 100% possession guar-antee. Whereas, probabilistic schemes (
Ateniese et al., 2007,
2008 ) use randomly chosen blocks of data to verify the integrity and give less than 100% guarantee. Deterministicschemes are not suitable for large size files, e.g. archiveshaving data size in GBs or TBs as integrity verification ofsuch a file takes too much time. This can lead to a limita-tion on how frequently the user can perform integrityverification. Therefore, probabilistic schemes are more ap-propriate for large files. On the other hand, deterministicverification is suitable for smaller sized files having the sizein few megabytes. Digital libraries (e-books, articles, re-search papers) and media contents (songs, pictures, etc.)normally have small size files but are large in number. Forsuch type of data, deterministic schemes are ideal to provide100% possession guarantees.
Ateniese et al. (2007) proposed the first probabilistic integrity scheme in 2007, whichused random sampling of blocks instead of reading thewhole file. By inspecting 4.6% of total blocks of a file, it pro-vides 99% possession guarantee. This scheme provided thebase for probabilistic integrity checking schemes, but its per-formance decreases as the size of data increase.
Nature of data :This attribute describes the nature of data,
on which the scheme is applicable. Data can be of staticnature (
Yuan and Yu, 2013 ; archival data, backups, or data
that are never modified but are appended only) or ofdynamic nature (
Curtmola et al., 2008; Zhang and Blanton,
2013 ; that frequently change due to operations like create,
read, update and delete). The integrity of volatile data is notconsidered in this survey. The nature of data also plays animport role in scheme evaluation. The scheme proposed in
Ateniese et al. (2007) is only suitable for static data, because
it requires re-computation of complete file tags when newdata are inserted in between existing data. Similarly, thescheme (
Ateniese et al., 2008 ) provides support for modification, deletion and append operations; however insertionoperation is not supported. The scheme in
Erway et al. (2009)
provides support for dynamic data operations such as in-sertion, updating, deletion, and modification. Furthermore,the scheme (
Erway et al., 2009 ) also kept the fundamental
properties of verification (unforgeability, unbounded queries)intact. The use of authenticated skip list based FlexList tosupport dynamic operations was proposed in
Esiner et al.
(2013) .Dodis et al. (2009) improved the efficiency of POR
schemes for static data. Bowers et al. (2009b) proposed a POR
scheme for static data having low storage overhead and pro-viding higher tolerance rate for errors. The highlightedfeature of their proposed design was an incremental en-coding of data blocks enabling the support for handling largesize datasets.
Barsoum and Hasan (2012) extended the integrity checking of static data for multiple copies. Thestrength of their proposed scheme is that verification timeis independent of the number of copies.
Stefanov et al. (2012)
introduced a framework named "Iris" giving cloud users highassurance of data integrity, data freshness and data recov-ery in case of any corruption.
Setup :The setup refers to the nature of environment in which
data integrity scheme will be deployed. Since data areplaced over the cloud, which can be simple (self-sufficient,providing all types of capabilities itself) or "hybrid/cloud of
cloud/multi-cloud" (managing some resources internally andother are provided externally;
Zhu et al., 2011 ), nature of
setup leads to some additional requirements in data in-tegrity schemes. For example, a cloud setup can supportreplication within a server and across servers, extending theintegrity checking from single to multiple replicas of files.
Curtmola et al.'s (2008) proposed scheme is called "multiple replicas pro vable data possession scheme (MR-PDP)",
which is capable of generating new replicas on demand incase of any corruption or deletion. Application of MR-PDPis limited to a single cloud having multiple storage serversbut is not extendable to a multi-cloud environment.
Bowers
et al. (2009a) focused on high availability by exploiting the
redundancy within a server and across servers. Peterson et al.
(2011) extended data integrity and added support for verification of SLA to ensure that outsourced data are in specifiedgeographical boundary.
Zhu et al. (2012) introduced characteristics of the high security, high performance, andtransparent verification for PDP model in the context ofmulti-cloud.
Tendency: The tendency of a data integrity scheme can be
verification only (identification of corruption/deletion of data)or verification with data recovery (if any corruption is iden-tified). From the perspective of tendency, data integrityschemes are categorized into pro vable data possession (PDP)
or proof of retrievability (POR) (
Xu and Chang, 2012 ).
a.Provable Data Possession (PDP): PDP schemes are probabilistic as they use the sampling of random blocks insteadof reading the whole file for verification. In PDP, the origi-nal data are preprocessed to generate some metadata.These metadata are placed with original data and is usedlater to verify the integrity of a user's data. These schemescan only identify corruption in data but do not support-corrupted data recovery.
b.Proof of Retrievability (POR): POR is very much similar to
PDP, but it also provides data recovery. POR schemes usethe redundant encoding of data and hence provide re-covery in case of failure. In other words, a PDP schemecan be transformed into POR by using error correctingor erasure codes. The auditing protocol of POR providesthe guarantee that CSP is holding all the data of clientand is still retrie vable. However, PDP only ensures that
the server is holding most of the data of a particularclient, due to the probabilistic nature of the algorithmused in verification.
Metadata: All data integrity schemes use some additional
metadata along with the original data, which are utilizedin integrity verification process. These metadata can be "tags"(
Yuan and Yu, 2013; Zhang and Blanton, 2013; Zhu et al.,
2012 ), having homomorphic verifiable property, which help
in generating an aggregated value in the verification process.The metadata may be "signatures" (
Luo and Bai, 2011; Tate
et al., 2013 ), which are used as an alternative to tags, e.g.
algebraic signatures to improve techniques like Reed-Solomon codes. Finally, metadata may consist of networkcodes that also support data recovery instead of tags andsignatures, which may be used for identifying small (bit orbyte level) data corruptions. For example, RSA-based ho-momorphic tags are used as verification metadata in35 computers & security 65 (2017) 29-49
Ateniese et al. (2007) , "HAIL" ( Bowers et al., 2009a ) scheme
provides data recovery capability using integrity-protectederror-correcting code (IP-ECC).
Chen et al. (2010) utilized
network codes to provide reduced storage overhead in dis-tributed storage system used for backup and archival data.Okamoto protocol based signatures have been used in thescheme (
Mohan and Katti, 2012 ) as metadata. Ateniese et al.
(2011) improve the capability of PDP scheme by introducing robustness using Forward Error-Correcting Codes (FEC).
Encryption: Over the year's data, integrity schemes have utilized both symmetric ( Ateniese et al., 2008; Lee and Chang,
2011 ) and asymmetric encryption ( Barsoum and Hasan, 2012;
Chen et al., 2013; Luo and Bai, 2011; Shen and Tzeng, 2011;Tate et al., 2013; Wang, 2013; Yuan and Yu, 2013; Zhu et al.,2011, 2012
) for security. Ateniese et al. (2008) achieved scalability and efficiency by using symmetric key encryption andcryptographic hash functions.
Ateniese et al. (2007) used RSA
based homomorphic verifiable tags (HVT) as the metadata.HVTs are unforgeable and are used in blockless verifica-tion by which a server can construct a proof of possessionand the client can verify it to ensure data integrity.
Curtmola
et al. (2008) utilized homomorphic tags of Ateniese et al.
(2007) for multiple replicas and used a Pseudo Random Function (PRF) for masking. Wang et al.'s (2009) proposed a
scheme for proof of retrievability using BLS signatures sup-porting public verifiability along with dynamic dataoperations.
Zhu et al.'s (2012) proposed scheme is based on
homomorphic verifiable response (HVR) and hash index hi-erarchy (HIH) and presents a cooperative PDP (CPDP) schemefrom bilinear pairings to provide knowledge soundness.
Lee
and Chang (2011) proposed a hybrid scheme using both symmetric and asymmetric encryption for efficient data integrityverification protocol.
Wang's (2012) scheme is based on group
signatures and Homomorphic MACs, providing integrity veri-fication of data shared among large user groups.
Auditing: Data integrity schemes either allow public (conducted by a third party auditor or multiple users) auditingor private auditing (performed by data owner). In the caseof public auditing, privacy issues such as data leakage, useranonymity, etc. are associated. Public auditing schemes arefurther categorized into privacy preserving and non-privacy preserving schemes.
Ateniese et al. (2009) proposed
a public verifiable scheme, which is called Proof of Storage(POS). However, this scheme was vulnerable to privacyattacks. The same author proposed another POS scheme (
Lee
and Chang, 2011 ), which provided privacy protection.
Krzywiecki and Kuty's (2012) scheme utilized secure pseudorandom numbers (SPRN) with Lagrangian interpolationand provides broadcast encryption with public auditabilityfor multiple users.
Shacham and Waters (2008) have used
BLS signatures to provide public auditing. Public auditingwith privacy protection is achieved along with traceabil-ity, which means that the original user can trace a signatureon a block and reveal the identity of the signer. Another sig-nificant contribution of this work is that it supports a largenumber of users without affecting the performance of theverification process (
Wang et al., 2012 ).Shen and Tzeng's
(2011) scheme supports delegation of auditing authority;
however, there is a restriction of re-delegation. Therefore,a user authorized for auditing cannot re-delegate theauthority to others.
Wang (2013) has discussed the issues
of public auditing and proposed pro vable secure and efficient proxy pro vable data possession (PPDP) scheme for cloud
storage.
In this thematic taxonomy, we have described the attributes over which data integrity schemes can be categorized.The details of scheme's workings have not been discussed here.See
section 4.1 for comparative analysis of schemes.
4. Characteristics of data integrity schemes
All data integrity schemes possess a subset of followingproperties:
Remote verification: Remote verification means that a verifier should not retrieve file blocks from the remote serverfor verification. This property is also termed as blockless veri-fication (
Ateniese et al., 2007 ). Blockless verification is an
essential requirement for all data integrity schemes, as re-trieving complete data file (especially with large sized files)on a client machine and verifying integrity is not practi-cal. In blockless verification, the cloud accesses block of adata file and generate a proof of possession, which is laterverified by a client to verify data integrity.
Unrestricted challenge frequency: Unrestricted challenge frequency means that there should be no restrictions on thenumbers of challenges made by a client or auditor to verifythe integrity of remote data (
Ateniese et al., 2007 ). Unrestricted challenge frequency property is also calledunbounded queries. Integrity verification of outsourced cloudstorage data is not a one-time activity. A client executes theverification protocol with intervals to timely identify anydata corruption. The frequency of challenge requests is di-rectly affected by the computation efficiency of the dataintegrity scheme. If verification process of a data integrityscheme is computationally expensive, then the client willuse it less frequently and as a result unbounded chal-lenge frequency would suffer.
Soundness: An untrusted server should not be able to deceive
(pass auditing without holding data) a challenge request(
Wang and Zhang, 2014; Zhu et al., 2012 ). The soundness
property of data integrity schemes ensures data reliabil-ity. The nature of a data integrity schemes is tamper evident,not tamper resistant. Therefore, if metadata are tamperedwith, or data get corrupted intentionally by the CSP or un-intentionally, this should be timely identified by a dataintegrity scheme. If the CSP can pass a challenge requestwithout holding the data or with corrupted data, then aclient will never be able to identify data corruption promptly,and the value of the data will be lost. Therefore, the reli-ability of data directly demands the soundness of a dataintegrity scheme for cloud storage.
Stateless verification: Neither a client nor the server needs
to store the results of previous audits to verify future audits.Every challenge request is independent of all past verifi-cations performed by the client. Stateless verification isindirectly an essential requirement of data integrity schemes.Otherwise, it will lead to a situation where state keeping36 computers & security 65 (2017) 29-49
itself would become a burden either for client or for CSP.
Keeping states of previous audits is unnecessary and mightincur some cost regarding resources, e.g. memory or diskstorage.
Robustness: Robustness is the ability to identify even the
smallest size of data corruptions (
Ateniese et al., 2011; Chen
and Curtmola, 2012a, 2012b ). Data integrity schemes of deterministic nature have a limitation of computationalefficiency and are not applicable for large datasets. To over-come these limitations, a probabilistic approach is adopted.Probabilistic data integrity schemes work on randomlychosen samples of data for integrity verification, which raisesthe issue of identification of small (bit or byte level) cor-ruptions. Robustness property enhances the soundness ofdata integrity schemes by providing the identification ofminor level corruptions in data.
Data recovery: For a data integrity scheme, it is not sufficient just to identify the misbehavior by the remote storageserver. As cloud users are also interested in, complete datarecovery to avoid an unpleasant situation. Data integrityschemes are divided into two categories depending on theirtendency. All data integrity schemes provide identifica-tion of corruptions (
Ateniese et al., 2007; Yuan and Yu, 2013 ),
but some also support data recovery along with identifi-cation (
Bowers et al., 2009b ). Usually, error-correcting codes
(ECC) are used by the schemes providing data recovery(
Bowers et al., 2009a; Chen and Curtmola, 2012b ). The
network codes have been utilized by ( Chen et al., 2010 )t o
provide better efficiency in comparison with erasure codesfor archival systems.
Dynamic data handling: Data can be either static (backup or
archival data) or dynamic nature (supporting operations likeinsertion, deletion, and modification). Providing data integ-rity for dynamic data is more challenging than for static dataor append-only data. Most of the schemes proposed in theliterature are not capable to handle dynamic data. Dynamicdata handling characteristic (
Curtmola et al., 2008; Zhang
and Blanton, 2013 ) demands that the data integrity should
remain intact, even after insertion, deletion or modifica-tion operation is performed.
Public/Private auditability: There are two approaches to audit
the data stored at the remote server. The first method allowsthe data owner to do the audit. The second method allowsa third party auditor to audit. The audit process in both ap-proaches is performed without retrieving the remote data(
Ateniese et al., 2009; Luo and Bai, 2011 ). In both approaches, the audit is performed without retrieving theremote data (
Ateniese et al., 2009; Luo and Bai, 2011 ). If only
the data owner can verify the integrity of the outsourceddata, then this scheme is considered to provide privateauditability. However, in some cases, it is not practically fea-sible for the data owner to remain online all time for dataintegrity verification. Hence, the data owner can delegatethis responsibility of integrity verification to a third partyauditor or other users. A data integrity scheme must supportpublic auditability property to support this audit delegation.
Privacy preserving: During the audit, the verification process
should protect data privacy. Privacy (
Wang et al., 2010; Zhu
et al., 2010 ) concerns are introduced due to public verifiability. As the data owner will not allow the disclosure ofhis private data to a third-party auditor, the privacy preservation property demands that a third party auditor shouldnot obtain any confidential information about the user's databut can still verify the integrity of outsourced data.
Fairness: Fairness means that a data integrity scheme should
provide protection to an honest CSP against legitimate butdishonest users, who may attempt to accuse a CSP of ma-nipulation in the outsourced data (
Zhu et al., 2011 ). If a data
integrity scheme does not support fairness, it means dis-honest users can damage CSP reputation.
Based on the aforesaid common characteristics, we
conduct a comparative analysis of data integrity schemes in
subsection 4.1 .
4.1. Comparative analysis of data integrity schemes
In this section, we will provide a comparative analysis and com-parison of data integrity schemes, as illustrated in
Table 1 .
Ateniese et al.'s (2007) proposed technique have used the
RSA-based homomorphic tags as verification metadata. The saidtags enabled blockless verification and are unforgeable. However,the scheme is applicable only to static data or appends onlydata. In the case of dynamic data operations, if any block isinserted or deleted from within the file, tags re-computationis required for all blocks ahead of modification point, whichis not practical. PDP scheme proposed public verifiability withweaker possession guarantees and posed no limitation on thefrequency of challenges. In 2008, these limitations of dynamicdata operations in PDP were partially handled in
Ateniese et al.
(2008) , by allowing modification, deletion and append operations. Support for insertion operation was still not supportedby this technique. Scalability and efficiency were achieved byusing symmetric key encryption and cryptographic hash func-tions. This technique lacked randomness in the challenges andthus proved to be unreliable as the CSP could cheat by cachingthe responses.There existed a limitation on the number of timesa data owner could query for the integrity of his data. Fur-thermore, both of these schemes were applicable to only a singlecopy of data.
Curtmola et al. (2008) proposed a scheme, which
extended the application of the PDP scheme to multiple rep-licas of files.This scheme was capable of generating new replicason demand in case of any corruption or deletion of any replica.It employed masking by utilizing the PRFs (Pseudo RandomFunctions) and is capable of resisting collusion attacks bystorage servers. Application of MR-PDP is limited to a singlecloud having multiple storage servers but is not extendable toa multi-cloud environment. All PDP schemes allowed detec-tion of corruption or server misbehavior but lacked the abilityto recover lost data after detection. The proof of retrievability(POR) schemes extended the PDP schemes by adding data re-covery feature.
Shacham and Waters (2008) proposed two POR
schemes for static data. 1) BLS Signatures for short query andshort response with public verifiability. 2) Pseudorandom func-tions for longer query and short response, having only privateverifiability support. Other characteristics included blocklessverification, unforgeability, unbounded queries, however, butthese schemes still fail to prevent data leakage. In 2009,

keeping the basic properties of verification, unforgeability, and
