Bowers
et al. (2009a) proposed HAIL, which kept existing features of
the PDP schemes intact, and focused on high availability byexploiting the redundancy within a server and across server,along with the provision of data recovery capability. HAIL usedintegrity-protected error-correcting code (IP-ECC) whose con-struction draws together PRFs, ECCs, and universal hashfunctions (UHFs), which serve the purpose of MACs.
Wang et al.
(2009) used RSA, bilinear pairing and Merkle Hash Tree construction for BLS based block tag authentication to achievepublic verifiability and data dynamics. However, this schemelacked privacy protection, which is an essential requirementwith public auditability. Among all these schemes, those havingthe capability of public auditing lacked privacy-preservingfeature.
Wang et al. (2010) proposed a data integrity scheme
that provided public auditing along with privacy protection, byusing random masking integrated with homomorphic authen-ticator.
Wang et al. (2010) also introduced the concept of batch
auditing, which allows multiple auditing tasks to be per-formed simultaneously. Nonetheless, it incurred performanceoverheads when failed auditing responses were received.
Chen
et al. (2010) used the construction of a message authentication code (MAC), proposed in Bowers et al. (2009a) , by combining
universal hashing with PRFs. One of the salient features of Chen
et al. (2010) was that it incurred less storage overhead though
it increased communication overhead. However, it only sup-ported private verifiability and was only applicable for a singlecloud. From a security perspective, the scheme in
Chen et al.
(2010) was secured against reply and pollution attacks. On the
contrary, by using network codes, a small portion of a file cannotbe read without reconstructing the entire file, thus, networkcodes based approach is not suitable for online storage systemswhere the read operation is extensively performed. However,they may be used for backup and archival system. In 2011,
Ateniese et al. improved the capability of PDP schemes by introducing robustness. However, it still lacked support fordynamic operations and public verifiability. The said schemeutilized Forward Error-Correcting Codes (FEC) to achieve datarecovery.
Lee and Chang (2011) proposed a scheme implemented using RSA-based algorithms and used hash functions(SHA-1 and SHA-512), pseudo-random functions, and pseudo-random permutations. This scheme is efficient in terms of datastorage and as well as in its utilization of bandwidth. However,the said scheme is only applicable to static data though it sup-ports public verifiability with privacy protection. In anotherstudy,
Liu et al. (2011) improved the dynamic PDP performance by using skip lists and hashes. However, this schemelacked support for public verifiability and suffered from the in-crease in the client side storage, as it needs to store secret valuesto perform the hashes.
Peterson et al. (2011) extended PDP to
include the verification of SLA, used in keeping the data incertain geographical boundaries. The authors have used MACsfor the proof of possession and network delay measurementto identify the geo-location of data. However, no guarantee canbe provided regarding the instantiation of extra copies outsidethe prescribed geographic area. The scheme in
Peterson et al.
(2011) is only applicable to static data, does not support public
verifiability, and fails if there is a proxy server between the clientand server. Zheng and Xu (2011) introduced the concept of fairness in data integrity schemes, which means the protectionfrom dishonest client accusing the honest CSP of his data cor-ruption. This scheme used authenticated data structure (range-based 2-3 trees), incremental signature scheme and errorcorrecting codes (ECC), but performance was low due to ECC.This scheme supported dynamic data operations but lackedsupport for public verifiability.
Chen and Curtmola (2012a)
achieved robustness using Reed-Solomon codes based onCauchy matrices to provide bit and byte level data corruptiondetection. Although the scheme provided robustness fordynamic data operations, however, data recovery and publicverifiability are not supported. The scheme proposed by
Krzywiecki and Kuty (2012) utilized secure pseudorandom
numbers (SPRN) with Lagrangian interpolation to improve com-putation and storage efficiency. Hence, it can be compatible withsmartphones and embedded systems with limited resources.In another study,
Mohan and Katti (2012) achieved reduced exponentiations to generate proof of possession irrespective ofchallenge size. The scheme is based on zero knowledge proofand witness hiding (malicious verifier cannot learn about theunderlying witness by talking to prover). The signature schemeused is based on Okamoto protocol using pseudorandom per-mutations and pseudorandom generators. However, no supportfor data dynamic operations and public verifiability is pro-vided. The scheme proposed by
Wang et al. (2012) supports
public verifiability of remote outsourced storage shared by mul-tiple users in a privacy-preserving manner (privacy protectionof user's data is provided against external auditors). In orderto support public auditing, group signatures are used for ho-momorphic authenticators and homomorphic MACs for storageefficiency. An index hash table holds identifiers of blocks.Despite providing privacy preservation, the scheme is unableto identify small corruptions with sampling and computa-tion cost increases with the increase of sampled blocks.Traceability is supported for large number of users without af-fecting the performance of the verification process. Here,traceability means that the original user can trace a signa-ture on a block and reveal the identity of the signer.
Zheng and
Xu (2012) utilized Merkle Hash Tree (MHT), pseudorandom functions and hashing. Pro vable data possession (PDP) and proof
of ownership (POW) are combined along with deduplication,which means detection and deletion of unwanted duplicatecopies. Furthermore, the scheme supported public auditing,which was missing in previous schemes.
Zhu et al. (2012) introduced high security, high performance, and transparentverification for PDP model in the context of multi-cloud. Thisscheme is based on homomorphic verifiable response (HVR)and hash index hierarchy (HIH), and presented a cooperativePDP (CPDP) scheme using bilinear pairings. It supports all char-acteristics of data integrity schemes such as blocklessverification, unforgeability, unbounded queries, data recov-ery, public auditing, privacy protection and data dynamicoperations. However,
Zhu et al. (2012) failed to provide knowledge soundness and is unable to resist against data leakage,and tag forgery attacks as pointed by
Wang and Zhang (2014) .
Shi et al. (2013) reduced bandwidth overhead by using a Merkle
Hash Tree (MHT). Other major contributions of this work areindependent metadata size, reduce disk seeks, and support fordynamic data.
Sarkar and Safavi-Naini (2013) utilized raptor41 computers & security 65 (2017) 29-49
codes (a special class of fountain codes) and supported data
recovery in case of corruption. The uniqueness of this schemeis that the numbers of blocks in a challenge are not fixed.
Gondree and Peterson (2013) combined the data integrity verification with constraint-based data geolocation using latency-based techniques in multi-cloud environment. However, thisscheme is not suitable in a setting where a private networkof significantly better quality than the Internet connects remotesites.
Esiner et al. (2013) proposed an approach that was the
first to use variable length for input file data blocks. It em-ployed authenticated skip list based FlexList and RSA-basedhomomorphic tags with support for data dynamic opera-tions and multiple audit requests handling simultaneously.
Chen
(2013) utilized algebraic signatures to take advantage of low
network bandwidth, with reasonable computation load. Thescheme provided resistance to malicious modification, al-though it still suffered from limitations such as signatures usednot being cryptographically secure, no support for data recov-ery, public verifiability and the lack of support for dynamic dataoperations.
Cash et al. (2013) combined the concept of oblivious RAM with erasure codes to hide the access pattern henceavoiding correlation of challenged blocks to physical storage.This scheme can efficiently update redundantly encoded data.However, it introduced high network communication costs.
Wei et al. (2014) focused on secure computation and used
commitment-based sampling (CBS) using Merkle hash tree, bi-linear pairings, and designated verifier signatures. This schemelacks dynamic data operations and data recovery capability butsupports public verifiability.
Zhu et al. (2012) presented an efficient PDP scheme where multiple CSPs cooperatively storeand maintain the clients' data. Then, based on homomorphicverifiable response (HVR) and hash index hierarchy (HIH), theypresented a cooperative PDP (CPDP) scheme from the bilin-ear pairings. They claimed that their scheme satisfied thesecurity property of knowledge soundness. However,
Wang and
Zhang (2014) performed cryptanalysis of this scheme and argued
that it lacked knowledge soundness.
We perform a comparative analysis of all the schemes above
inTable 1 . The analysis reveals that all data integrity schemes
have three major concerns, which are (a) computation, (b) com-munication, and (c) storage efficiency. There is always a tradeoffbetween computation, communication, and storage effi-ciency. Besides the said tradeoffs, security of data integrityschemes becomes a challenging problem when dynamic dataoperations (insert, update, and delete) are supported. Public au-diting and access control in a multiuser environment is alsoanother important issue handled by data integrity schemes.The PDP schemes have focused on verification. Identificationof bit or byte level corruptions using PDP schemes is a chal-lenging task due to probabilistic nature of these schemes. Toidentify bit or byte level corruptions with 100% guarantee, wehave to verify the complete file, whereas verification is per-formed through random sampling in the case of a probabilisticapproach. On the other hand, the POR schemes support veri-fication and data recovery. Selection of metadata primitives isa major issue of data integrity schemes. Metadata primitives,chosen for implementation of both PDP and POR schemessignificantly affect storage and computation efficiencies. A cryp-tographic primitive provides security but incurs increasedcomputation overhead, whereas other non-cryptographic (
Chen,2013 ) primitives are computation efficient but lack security.
Details of design challenges of data integrity schemes are dis-cussed in
Section 4 . From the deployment environment
perspective, said schemes are mostly applicable for a singlecloud environment, whereby only a few have focused on multi/hybrid cloud environment. Most of the existing schemes canhandle a single copy of data, and very few are applicable inreplication scenarios. Therefore, one future aspect of data in-tegrity schemes would be handling of multiple replicas of dataand issues associated with it (such as proof of ownership,deduplication). We have discussed such future trends of dataintegrity schemes in
Section 5 . See Table 1 below for the comparative analysis of data integrity schemes.
5. Design challenges of data integrity
schemes
Main design challenges of data integrity schemes are discussed below:
5.1. Computation efficiency
All data integrity schemes preprocess the data before out-sourcing it to a cloud storage server, similarly, at the cloudstorage server; metadata are generated from original data. Theoverhead of performing such processing may affect the com-putation efficiency of data integrity schemes. Computationefficiency of preprocessing phase does not matter for smalldatasets, but it has a serious impact for large datasets. Com-putation cost on the server side for the proof generation limitson how frequently user can verify the integrity of outsourceddata. Primitives used by the data integrity scheme as metadataalso impact computation time. For example, algebraic signa-tures (
Chen, 2013 ) based metadata provide an improved
computation efficiency of verification process as compared tocryptographic primitives. In the case of POR schemes, networkcodes (
Chen et al., 2010 ) provide better efficiency than the error
correcting codes. Han et al. (2014) have used maximum rank
distance (MRD) codes ( Han et al., 2014 ) to generate homomorphic tags, which improved the server-side computation.
Chen et al. (2014) have utilized Hill cipher for integrity verification with fast computation. Kwon et al. (2014) have reduced
the auditing cost with the application of new index tablemanagement.
5.2. Storage efficiency
For integrity verification and data recovery by any data integ-rity scheme, additional metadata is required along with theoriginal data. The above storage overhead on client and serverside describes storage efficiency. In
Chen et al. (2010) , the client
needs to store some secret values thus requiring an extrastorage, such as for a 4 GB file, a 2MB storage is needed.
Chen
et al. (2014) managed to produce small size metadata and improved the storage cost. Furthermore, data de-duplication(
González-Manzano and Orfila, 2015; Latesh Kumar and
Lawrance, 2015; Miao et al., 2015 ) plays a significant role in the
reduction of storage though will incur some cost.42 computers & security 65 (2017) 29-49
5.3. Communication efficiency
Three aspects describing the communication efficiency of data
integrity schemes are ( Wang, 2014 ): (1) Overhead of the initial
data transfer along with metadata, (2) challenge requests forpossession verification by data owner and (3) challenge re-sponse for proof of possession sent back by the cloud storageserver. In the case of dynamic data, this communication over-head also includes the updates verification. Primitives used formetadata have an impact on the communication cost. Alge-braic signatures (
Chen, 2013 ) provide communication efficiency
through low network bandwidth utilization during challengerequest and response time. By using Hill cipher (
Chen et al.,
2014 ), the size of challenge and response is usually small, providing that communication is efficient. In the case of multiplereplicas, MuR-DPA (
Liu et al., 2014 ) scheme improved communication for both update verification and integrity verification.
5.4. Reduced disk I/O
Disk I/O efficiency of a data integrity scheme is derived by theoverhead in block access and metadata access for proof gen-eration on cloud storage server. Accessing all blocks for proofgeneration impacts the efficiency of a scheme and makes thescheme impractical for large datasets.
The following parameters influence the overall efficiency
of any disk I/O:
Size of block: Data integrity schemes either use fixed (
Ateniese
et al., 2007 ) or variable length block size ( Esiner et al., 2013 ).
The smaller the size of the block, the larger the number ofblocks in a file. This will impact preprocessing time in gen-erating metadata tags for all blocks. Depending on thisparameter, the efficiency of a scheme is significantly af-fected by the variable length of metadata, the number ofblocks in the challenge and unordered indexes in the chal-lenge request.
Variable length of data/metadata: The data or metadata for
a particular block index cannot be directly accessed, dueto variable length. The searching of data or metadata in theabove case is similar to searching in a link list. To reach aparticular index data or metadata, preprocessed file has tobe traversed sequentially, skipping undesired blocks/tags.As a result, this increases disk I/O as the number of blocktags increases in the verification process, hence proof gen-eration time increases (
Esiner et al., 2013 ). Schemes have
improved this searching using a tree-like data structures,such as authenticated data structure (skip list), balanced tree,and hash indexes. The performance of a scheme can be sig-nificantly improved by reducing the sequential access todirect access. By reducing the sequential access to directaccess, a significant performance gain can be achieved.
Number of blocks in challenge: This parameter has an impact
on both computations cost and I/O cost. A large numberof blocks in a challenge will lead to increased time ingenerating the proof (
Sarkar and Safavi-Naini, 2013 ). Implementation primitives used as metadata directly affectcomputation cost. An important property of unboundedqueries (no limit on how frequently user can verify its data)is lost for large datasets depending on this parameter.Okamoto protocol based signatures (
Mohan and Katti, 2012 )
have been used to reduce exponentiations needed in gen-erating proofs, independent of the number of blocks inchallenge thus improving computational efficiency.
Unordered block indexes in challenge: If indexes of blocks are
in ascending order in the challenge generated by a client,then verification time can be improved (
Shi et al., 2013 ). The
reason is that searching for each metadata tag in a chal-lenge will not require the traversal of the metadata file fromthe start; hence, the server can continue to look for the nextindex in the challenge from the current position. With thisparameter, disk I/O is significantly reduced, improving proofgeneration time.
5.5. Security
Data integrity schemes are vulnerable to a variety of attacks.Therefore, care must be taken in designing such a scheme. Fol-lowing are the possible attacks that may be launched againsta data integrity scheme along with their possible solutions:
Tag forgery attack: Forgery of tags is possible by malicious
CSP in an attempt to hide the user's data damage and bypassthe auditing challenge. A CSP may try to forge the tags todeceive the verifier successfully (
Yang and Jia, 2012; Yu et al.,
2014; Zhu et al., 2012 ). Cryptographic homomorphic tags can
provide unforgeability ( Ateniese et al., 2007; Zhu et al., 2011 ).
Data deletion attack: CSP may pass the challenge by generating a valid proof of possession using tags only whereoriginal data might have been deleted entirely (
Yu et al.,
2014 ).
Replace attack: CSP may replace the corrupted or deleted pair
of data blocks and respective tags with another valid pairin the response of a challenge, whereby deceiving the veri-fier (
Yang and Jia, 2012; Yu et al., 2014 ).
Replay attack: The CSP may cache the previous challenge
responses of verification phase and eventually replays thecached responses for new challenges matching it. In thecontext of remote storage, the term such as "rollback attack"or "forking attack" is also used for indicating replay attacks(
Tate et al., 2013 ).
Pollution attack: In pollution attack, a dishonest server uses
correct data in response generation against a challenge butprovides corrupted blocks in repair phase for coding newblocks (
Chen et al., 2010 ).
Data leakage attack: In this type of attack, an adversary can
extract stored data during the verification protocol usingtechniques such as wiretapping (
Zhu et al., 2011, 2012 ).
In the presence of such attacks, data integrity schemes may
fail in timely identification of data corruption and result in anunrecoverable damage. Following guidelines may be used indesigning a data integrity scheme, which can resist against thedescribed attacks:
1. Metadata should be tamper evident (
Ateniese et al., 2007 ).
2. Proof generation mechanism must utilize the data blocks
along with metadata ( Ateniese et al., 2007 ).
3. Proof of possession should be semantically secure ( Zhu et al.,
2011 ).43 computers & security 65 (2017) 29-49
4. Metadata and its corresponding data block must have strong
binding with each other ( Yang and Jia, 2012; Yu et al., 2014 ).
5. Randomness must be used in the challenge-response protocol ( Ateniese et al., 2007 ).
These simple characteristics can make a data integrity
scheme more secure and reliable. Verification protocol of dataintegrity schemes rely on the security of metadata.
If metadata are not secured, then this raises concerns over
the security and reliability of the underlying scheme. To hidethe data corruptions, malicious CSP may modify metadata topass the challenge phase. If a malicious CSP can do that suc-cessfully without being detected, then the underlying schemeis not secured and reliable. This case justifies the require-ment of having a tamper evident metadata (
Ateniese et al., 2007 )
to identify a malicious CSP. Recommendations (2) and (3) arerelated and collectively define the strength of the data integ-rity scheme. If a scheme solely relies on the metadata forgeneration of proof of possession, then CSP may delete the dataand keep only the metadata. So utilizing data and metadatain the proof generation can mitigate the data deletion attack.However, if proof of possession is not semantically secured,then challenge-response phase is vulnerable to data leakageattack. Therefore, while designing a data integrity scheme, caremust be taken so that the metadata used for verification maynot result in leakage of original data during the verification pro-tocol. Modern cryptographic techniques such as "Multi-Prover Zero-Knowledge Proof System" (
Zhu et al., 2011 ) can
provide protection against tag forgery attack and data leakageattack. Using homomorphic verifiable responses and hash indexhierarchy "Collaborative PDP" (
Zhu et al., 2011 ) ensures that any
adversary capable of eavesdropping the communication channelwill not be able to extract any information from data ex-changed in verification protocol. Metadata must be tamper-evident and proof generation process must utilize data and thecorresponding metadata; however, if data and metadata haveno strong binding then still a scheme may be vulnerable toreplace attack. By establishing a strong binding between datablocks and corresponding metadata, replace attack can be miti-gated. Furthermore, replace attacks can also be avoided usingauthentication of block indexes (
Yu et al., 2014 ). The last recommendation is randomness in the challenge-responseprotocol. In the absence of randomness, a scheme may be prone
to replay attack, i.e. CSP replaying cached responses of past chal-lenges (
Chen et al., 2010; Erway et al., 2009; Yang and Jia, 2012 )
in response to new challenges.
6. Discussion
We have identified the trend of evolution of data integrityschemes by surveying existing schemes.
Fig. 4 depicts the
timeline, which presents the scope of data integrity schemesthat keep on extending with the passage of time. Research ondata integrity schemes started before 2007. However,
Ateniese
et al. (2007) proposed first the formal method for pro vable data
possession in 2007. Due to the deterministic nature of thescheme, performance was a major issue at that time. PDP(
Ateniese et al., 2007 ) was the first probabilistic approach that
improved performance by random sampling of blocks in theverification process. Up to 2007, all schemes handled static dataonly. In 2008, the focus was shifted to dynamic nature, where(insert, update, delete operations) were supported (
Ateniese
et al., 2008; Chen et al., 2010; Curtmola et al., 2008; Erway et al.,2009; Wang et al., 2009, 2010; Zhu et al., 2010
). While handling dynamic data, different attacks (e.g. replay attacks; Erway
et al., 2009 ) were identified in the schemes proposed at that
time. Attacks on integrity schemes lead the research to shifton the security of data integrity schemes themselves. With theusage of authenticated data structures such as skip list andMerkle hash tree, data integrity schemes were able to handledynamic data securely and efficiently (
Esiner et al., 2013 ). For
large datasets, the performance of data integrity schemes wasstill a major issue. However, new aspects kept on evolving inthe context of data integrity schemes. In
Ateniese et al. (2007) ,
the concept of public verifiability was discussed with weakerpossession guarantees. Later in 2009 (
Wang et al., 2009 ), secured
public verifiability became a part of data integrity. With the in-troduction of public verifiability, privacy issues were identified,and in 2010 privacy-preserving public verifiability (
Wang et al.,
2010 ) became an essential part of data integrity. Before 2010,
all research on data integrity schemes had focused on a singlecloud environment. In 2010, for the first time, data integritywas discussed in the context of hybrid or multi-cloud (
Zhu et al.,
Fig. 4 -Data integrity schemes evolution timeline.44 computers & security 65 (2017) 29-49
2010 ). During 2010-2012, major focus areas of data integrity
schemes were efficiency (computation, communication, andstorage), dynamic data, and security. In 2011, service level agree-ment (
Popa et al., 2011 ) verification emerged as a new feature
of integrity checking. Geolocation of data ( Peterson et al., 2011 )
was also included as an aspect of service level agreement veri-fication. Later on, assured deletion (
Habib et al., 2013 ) and reoutsourcing of data ( Jiang et al., 2014 ) were also discussed. In
2014, computation assurance ( Wei et al., 2014 ) aspect was highlighted in the context of secure data integrity. Since 2015, thededuplication (
Bellare et al., 2013a, 2013b; González-Manzano
and Orfila, 2015; Latesh Kumar and Lawrance, 2015; Miao et al.,2015; Zheng and Xu, 2012
) issues are considered necessary for
large datasets, as storage cost and access efficiency are sig-nificantly affected in replication scenarios.
7. Future trends of data integrity schemes
Cloud computing model is evolving dynamically, and differ-ent business and cloud usage aspects are emerging. New trendshave broadened the scope of data integrity schemes. The fol-lowing are the recently emerging research trends in the contextof data integrity schemes.
1.Geo-location assurance: Service level agreements may restrict the CSP to keep data, in a particular geographic regionat the granularity level of city, state, time zone, or politicalboundary. For compliance purpose, some cloud users arerequired to ensure geolocation of data (
Albeshri et al., 2014;
Fu et al., 2014; Gondree and Peterson, 2013; Hildebrandt, 2014;Paladi et al., 2014; Peterson et al., 2011
). Cloud users cannot
trust the CSP. A careless or naïve CSP may migrate user datato an overseas data center violating the SLA in an attemptto leverage cheaper IT cost. Such actions of the CSP can makeuser data available for foreign governments indirectly whocan inspect it through search warrants or any legal mecha-nism. In this scenario, timely identification of intentionaldishonesty or violation of SLA by the CSP is an essential re-quirement for the data owner. New research on data integrityschemes is incorporating geolocation verification.
2.Assured deletion: In cloud storage, data deletion should be
guaranteed otherwise it could result in breaches (
Tang et al.,
2012 ). Another important aspect in assured deletion is that
other versions, which share the common data of the deletedversions or files, should remain unaffected.
Rahumed et al.
(2011) proposed "FADE" which is a version controlled backup
system with the capability of fine-grained assured dele-tion. The deleted versions are ensured to be permanentlyinaccessible. SoK (
Reardon et al., 2013 ) highlights the approaches for secured, assured deletion and taxonomy ofadversaries and their capabilities to take advantage of cloudstorage in the absence of a secured deletion. Assured de-letion is also termed as self-destruction (
Cachin et al., 2013;
Chaoling et al., 2014; Habib et al., 2013; Xiong et al., 2013 ).
3.Deduplication: Deduplication is the detection and deletion
of unwanted duplicate copies to improve storage cost.Deduplication can be at the file level or block level. Sidechannel attacks (
Harnik et al., 2010 ) have been launched
through the deduplication process. Benefits of deduplicationinclude reduced backup cost, lower cost of business continuity or disaster recovery, increased storage efficiency andenhanced network efficiency. Simplest approach used fordeduplication is to compare objects (file or blocks of a file)and remove copies, which already exist in storage (
Bellare
et al., 2013a, 2013b; González-Manzano and Orfila, 2015;Latesh Kumar and Lawrance, 2015; Miao et al., 2015; Zhengand Xu, 2012
). For identification of copies, hashes of files
or blocks are computed and stored, and if hash for any newblock is found to be present in the stored hashes, then thecopy is removed.
4.Computation assurance: Computational reliability is a future
requirement for any efficient CSP. Computation assurancecan increase the level of confidence of cloud users for out-sourcing their data storage. Google cloud platform has annualaudits from standards like ISO 27001 and FISMA to guar-antee the assurance. But the user cannot solely rely on theguarantees claimed by CSP. Some attempts have been madeon data integrity schemes to ensure computation reliabil-ity. These include approaches with a trusted hardwarecomponent (
Tate et al., 2013 ) such as "Trusted Platform
Module (TPM)" and without a hardware component ( Wei
et al., 2014 ). Whereas, Khan and Hamlen (2013) allow
untrusted, mostly serial computations in untrusted com-puting environments to be independently and efficientlyvalidated by trusted, commodity clouds.
5.Self-controlling objects (SCO): Some attempts have been made
to encapsulate sensitive resources along with their accesscontrol policies in programmable Java Achieves (JARs). Thisapproach assured the protection of sensitive resourcesthrough the provision of adaptive security policies(
Squicciarini et al., 2013 ). This idea of SCO can be extended to achieve geolocation, assured deletion, anddeduplication in the future.
6.Re-outsourcing data: Data integrity schemes are incorporating verification of SLA, and they are a practical requirementof cloud users, which CSP must satisfy and ensure. Clientdata should not be re-outsourced without prior knowl-edge and consent of client (
Jiang et al., 2014 ). Re-outsourcing
detection is indirectly a necessary part of data integrity whenoutsourcing data to a multi-cloud environment.

8. Conclusion
Cloud storage is a cost effective solution and provides elas-ticity, pay-as-you-go, and business continuity with long-termretention and risk mitigation through disaster recovery. Thesefeatures are not available with on-premises storage. However,cloud storage model has inherent weaknesses; a user out-sourcing data loses control over his data. Data confidentialityand integrity are the major concerns of the user when movingdata from on-premises storage to the cloud storage. Many dataintegrity schemes have been proposed over the years to addressthe security issues associated with cloud storage. In this paper,we have discussed cloud computing and cloud storage. We havealso discussed the cloud storage issues. Our focus in this paperwas to improve the understanding of security aspects of dataintegrity schemes. Schemes proposed over the years are notsuitable for all types of data and environments. Therefore, we45 computers & security 65 (2017) 29-49
establish taxonomy based on attributes that can help the
readers to understand different aspects (nature of data, natureof deployment environment, etc.) of data integrity schemes.The said attributes impact the applicability and efficiency ofschemes in practical scenarios. We provide a comparative analy-sis based on characteristics of data integrity schemes. Weestablish the timeline of the evolution of data integrity schemesand also identify future aspects that researchers can exploitto perform their future research. We identify the design chal-lenges in performance and security of data integrity schemes.We also identify performance-degrading factors of data integ-rity schemes and provide a discussion on mitigation strategiesfor the possible attacks.

1-s2.0-S016740481730144X-main.pdf.title: Sticky policies approach within cloud computing
1-s2.0-S016740481730144X-main.pdf.abstract: This paper discusses a secure document sharing approach, which addresses confidentiality, integrity and authenticity concerns related to cloud-based data sharing. This researchis focused on a secure construct that would integrate with other cloud ready standards andproducts for data protection. Sticky policies, recently considered as one of the preferred clouddata protection techniques, are here combined with standardized OOXML data package. Thedefined model leverages the Identity Based Encryption (IBE) scheme to attach sticky poli-cies to the data. This paper also shows several security features and functions that are suitablefor secure data sharing in the cloud. Technologies used for proposed construction are notnew, therefore only their unique combination with AES key derived from XACML sticky policyvia IBE and OOXML wrapper constitutes novelty of this research.
1-s2.0-S016740481730144X-main.pdf.intro_text:
1. Introduction
Cloud service providers (CSPs) are offering services that in large
organizations and enterprises were previously delivered onlyon-premises. This introduced completely new challenges forpotential CSP customers. Major security organizations offeredtough security standards that CSP have to comply with andstandards that customers from governmental, financial andpublic sectors have to implement (
Luna et al., 2015 ). Security
standards compliance however is a regulatory form of infor-mation security practice not a safeguard that can actuallyprotect the data.
To compete with new technological challenges, many data
protection services that were previously only delivered withinstrict security boundaries are offered as a cloud service. Oneof the major threats is related to data encryption. It has beenshown that currently used cryptographic schemes withcommon setup could be broken with an emerging quantumtechnology (
Chen et al., 2016 ). Quantum computing has been
hanging as sword of Damocles over the cloud security for adecade. No data could be safe but its governance could be im-proved. Another possible threat is related to Big Data wheresimple machine learning and business intelligence as a servicebecomes a way to efficiently process large amounts ofanonymized or encrypted personal data. Extracted anonymizeddata becomes highly sensitive due to fact that illegitimate dataanalysis applied on a large scale could have a potentially serioussocial impact (
Reimsbach-Kounatze, 2015 ). Without right dedicated information security techniques for cloud, it is rather hardto protect information. Nevertheless, different security tech-niques having various vulnerabilities could serve better ifproperly chosen for the right purpose.
CSPs protecting data at rest on per-database basis can leverage database encryption. Recently Microsoft researcherspublished results around a new efficient homomorphic en-cryption what could be a solution for a medical data (
Dowlin
et al., 2015 ) that has to be processed in a secure manner without
ScienceDirec t

divulging underlying information. On the other side, just a few
months earlier, another group of Microsoft researchers provedthat database CryptDB encryption, previously acknowledgedas a secure data protection technique can be broken with asingle trick (
Naveed et al., 2015 ). It might be just a matter of
time until homomorphic encryption becomes vulnerable to anew type of attack. Database encryption approach is highly ef-ficient from indexing perspective, however single vulnerabilitymay compromise entire database security and divulge vastamount of sensitive information.
On the other hand, Information Rights Management (IRM)
approach applies security on per data piece or data-in-motionbasis. CSPs offering IRM as a service encrypt every single docu-ment or message separately therefore risk that entire datarepository is compromised due to a single vulnerability isreduced. IRM seems to be a relevant and an easy solution fordocument and message exchange; although to deliver both se-curity and performance, it involves at least two cryptographicmethods where keys management becomes complex.There isstill a lack of cloud standards and just a few products that makesdata exchange between parties homogeneous therefore hard.IRM providers considered new security countermeasures (i.e.Microsoft), to improve keys protection, enabled on-premisesHardware Security Module (HSM) support (
Sergey et al., 2015 )
for its flag, cloud-based IRM product MS Rights ManagementServices (RMS) Online. IRM itself does not provide functional-ity for data indexing and its applications without prior dataextractions and anonymisation are limited in comparison toDB encryption. However still large organizations and enter-prises as well as small businesses and individuals store andexchange sensitive information using unprotected docu-ments and single plain text messages. IRM is not suitable formany applications, although it is very efficient when it comesto constrained data exchange where two or more parties sharea data in asynchronous manner.
In regards to frameworks for cloud data sharing, data hosted
and protected by one cloud service provider cannot be se-curely transferred outside of a single CSP security boundary.Such a migration would require either data to be re-encryptedbefore migration or cloud providers would have to exchangecryptographic master keys. Cloud data hosting very often isbased on storing data by homogeneous application in a publicInternet space; what bends initial cloud service principals. Theo-retically cloud provider should offer a transparent service thatcould be dynamically transferred or seized by other cloudservice provider without loss of actual service quality and dataavailability (
Leimbach et al., 2014 ). Using IRM with properly designed infrastructure of distributed trust authorities (TA) couldaddress problem of data sharing and data migration betweenCSPs and other data sharing parties.
The solution presented here is built on top of existing open
standards mainly authentication and authorization frame-work that have been developed over years and that have beenused for various global implementations. Security AssertionMarkup Language (SAML) authentication standard developedby The Organization for the Advancement of Structured In-formation Standards (OASIS) (
Cantor et al., 2005 ) is not discussed
further in this paper but it is worth mentioning in regards toactual future implementation. SAML authenticates identityacross different organizations and cloud providers. Forauthorization this research applied an existing eXtensible Access
Control Markup Language (XACML) policy framework (
Saldhana
et al., 2013 ), which can enforce Discretionary Access Control
(DAC) rights and leverage non-DAC roles like in Role-based AccessControl (RBAC) systems (
Anderson, 2004 ) or can be combined
with modern risk assessment engine ( Gasparini, 2013 )t oc o n t r o l
access using dynamic risk calculations. Most of the modernaccess control techniques can be combined with XACML policyincluding dynamic membership and rights revocation. Docu-ment data format used in this research is based on Office OpenXML (OOXML) an open standard (
Apple et al, 2006 ), which defines
the XML schemas with conforming vocabularies for word-processing, spreadsheet and presentation documents, as wellas the documents packaging. Finally both the XACML-basedsticky policy and the data are bound together using IdentityBased Encryption (
Boneh and Franklin, 2003 ), a cryptographic
primitive that protects the data's confidentiality and integrityas well as sticky policy authenticity.
Section 2 shows other works this research refers to and other
papers that discuss secure data sharing in the cloud and stickypolicies as a cloud enabler.
Section 3 describes Identity Based
Cryptography and shows its applications for discussed stickypolicies model.
Section 4 explains Identity Based Encryption
with other entities i.e. sticky policy used for key construc-tion.
Section 5 shows IBE security proof that applies to sticky
policies model. Section 6 discusses integrity and nonrepudiation. Section 7 shows how sticky policies can protect
the data and what safeguards they bring.
1-s2.0-S016740481730144X-main.pdf.conclusion_text:
11. Summary
There are many security gaps that need to be analyzed before
any cloud data sharing framework can be used to host sensi-tive data. This paper shows how to address some of the threatsand how the presented solution could protect the data. Modelsimplicity might be very attractive for implementations thatlook for a simple solution with a potential to be extended byeither additional cryptographic primitive or various obfusca-tions and encoding methods. Authors suggest that AES is onlyone of many possibilities for data encryption and under otherIBE schemes the well-designed sticky policies model mightbecome even a simpler solution.
Using unencrypted access policy attached to the data helps
identify the data as well as the owner in the cloud space. Trivialattributes encoding and anonymization should be sufficientto protect the policy from malicious use. By introducing well-defined policy templates, the policy controls when the datacould be accessed, when the data should be re-encrypted withlatest TA public key or when the data processor should renewIBE parameters for a given TA.
By keeping authorization and authentication components
separate from the actual cryptographic operations on dataunlike in ABE, allows a faster development of existing modelsand easier integration with existing frameworks that are alreadyused for large-scale implementations. Existing ABE-based imple-mentations if used seem over-engineered and therefore stillneed to reach the maturity level sufficient for large and complexenvironments.
Furthermore, MS RMS Online is an IRM product that
requires existing data governance and for more moderatelarge organizations that care about data protection is stillusually deployed only on-premises. This IRM system haslegacy XrML policy format limitations and therefore may notbe sufficient for large-scale cloud-based access managementsystems. MS RMS Online introduced various features likeOne Time Passwords (OTP) and Hardware Security Module(HSM) what helps companies with secure cloud on-boarding,however there are clear technical limitations that wouldrequire further research and development. Nevertheless, MSRMS is not an open standard and there are IRM marketcompetitors. There seems to be no single secure data sharingservice that would suit large organizations and enterprisesas well as small businesses. Sticky policies with IBE imple-mentation when clearly defined can become a good openalternative for the existing IRM market.
In regards to actual implementation of XACML entities and
XACML policy generation, Axiomatics and WSO
2have products and set of stable libraries ready to combine with existingaccess management systems. Oracle formally Sun Microsystems,Quest Software and other larger software companies have alsoown XACML implementations, however these are either dis-continued or closed for integration with third party systems. Todevelop an OASIS XACML complaint product is highly simplifieddue to various open projects with source code available via publiccode repositories.
To introduce other less functional and more security related
techniques, some of the fundamental policy engine and keymanagement components were not discussed here.

organizations and enterprises were previously delivered onlyon-premises. This introduced completely new challenges forpotential CSP customers. Luna et al., 2015 ). Security
standards compliance however is a regulatory form of infor-mation security practice not a safeguard that can actuallyprotect the data.

protection services that were previously only delivered withinstrict security boundaries are offered as a cloud service. Oneof the major threats is related to data encryption. It has beenshown that currently used cryptographic schemes withcommon setup could be broken with an emerging quantumtechnology (
Chen et al., 2016 ). Quantum computing has been
hanging as sword of Damocles over the cloud security for adecade. No data could be safe but its governance could be im-proved. Dowlin
et al., 2015 ) that has to be processed in a secure manner without
ScienceDirec t

divulging underlying information. On the other side, just a few
months earlier, another group of Microsoft researchers provedthat database CryptDB encryption, previously acknowledgedas a secure data protection technique can be broken with asingle trick (
Naveed et al., 2015 ). It might be just a matter of
time until homomorphic encryption becomes vulnerable to anew type of attack. Database encryption approach is highly ef-ficient from indexing perspective, however single vulnerabilitymay compromise entire database security and divulge vastamount of sensitive information.
On the other hand, Information Rights Management (IRM)
approach applies security on per data piece or data-in-motionbasis. CSPs offering IRM as a service encrypt every single docu-ment or message separately therefore risk that entire datarepository is compromised due to a single vulnerability isreduced. IRM seems to be a relevant and an easy solution fordocument and message exchange; although to deliver both se-curity and performance, it involves at least two cryptographicmethods where keys management becomes complex.There isstill a lack of cloud standards and just a few products that makesdata exchange between parties homogeneous therefore hard.IRM providers considered new security countermeasures (i.e.Microsoft), to improve keys protection, enabled on-premisesHardware Security Module (HSM) support (
Sergey et al., 2015 )
for its flag, cloud-based IRM product MS Rights ManagementServices (RMS) Online. IRM itself does not provide functional-ity for data indexing and its applications without prior dataextractions and anonymisation are limited in comparison toDB encryption. However still large organizations and enter-prises as well as small businesses and individuals store andexchange sensitive information using unprotected docu-ments and single plain text messages. IRM is not suitable formany applications, although it is very efficient when it comesto constrained data exchange where two or more parties sharea data in asynchronous manner.
In regards to frameworks for cloud data sharing, data hosted
and protected by one cloud service provider cannot be se-curely transferred outside of a single CSP security boundary.Such a migration would require either data to be re-encryptedbefore migration or cloud providers would have to exchangecryptographic master keys. Cloud data hosting very often isbased on storing data by homogeneous application in a publicInternet space; what bends initial cloud service principals. Theo-retically cloud provider should offer a transparent service thatcould be dynamically transferred or seized by other cloudservice provider without loss of actual service quality and dataavailability (
Leimbach et al., 2014 ). Using IRM with properly designed infrastructure of distributed trust authorities (TA) couldaddress problem of data sharing and data migration betweenCSPs and other data sharing parties.
The solution presented here is built on top of existing open
standards mainly authentication and authorization frame-work that have been developed over years and that have beenused for various global implementations. Security AssertionMarkup Language (SAML) authentication standard developedby The Organization for the Advancement of Structured In-formation Standards (OASIS) (
Cantor et al., 2005 ) is not discussed
further in this paper but it is worth mentioning in regards toactual future implementation. SAML authenticates identityacross different organizations and cloud providers. Forauthorization this research applied an existing eXtensible Access
Control Markup Language (XACML) policy framework (
Saldhana
et al., 2013 ), which can enforce Discretionary Access Control
(DAC) rights and leverage non-DAC roles like in Role-based AccessControl (RBAC) systems (
Anderson, 2004 ) or can be combined
with modern risk assessment engine ( Gasparini, 2013 )t oc o n t r o l
access using dynamic risk calculations. Most of the modernaccess control techniques can be combined with XACML policyincluding dynamic membership and rights revocation. Docu-ment data format used in this research is based on Office OpenXML (OOXML) an open standard (
Apple et al, 2006 ), which defines
the XML schemas with conforming vocabularies for word-processing, spreadsheet and presentation documents, as wellas the documents packaging. Finally both the XACML-basedsticky policy and the data are bound together using IdentityBased Encryption (
Boneh and Franklin, 2003 ), a cryptographic
primitive that protects the data's confidentiality and integrityas well as sticky policy authenticity.
Section 2 shows other works this research refers to and other
papers that discuss secure data sharing in the cloud and stickypolicies as a cloud enabler.
Section 3 describes Identity Based
Cryptography and shows its applications for discussed stickypolicies model.
Section 4 explains Identity Based Encryption
with other entities i.e. sticky policy used for key construc-tion.
Section 5 shows IBE security proof that applies to sticky
policies model. Section 6 discusses integrity and nonrepudiation. Section 7 shows how sticky policies can protect
the data and what safeguards they bring.

2. Related work
Many research projects aim to deliver new data protection safe-guards sufficient to protect highly sensitive personal data suchas medical or governmental records (
Jain and Farkas, 2013; Le
et al., 2012 ;Abbas and Khan, 2014 ;Li et al., 2012 ).
Sticky policy is not a new data protection model, it was first
discussed in Karjoth et al. (2002) , went through various transformation and has been used for many successful InformationRights Management (IRM) implementations. Existing IRMsystems Microsoft Rights Management Services (RMS) Onlineproduct (
Sergey et al., 2015 ) or Oracle Information Rights Management (IRM) ( Martin and Peet, 2010 ) and other same as sticky
policies model attach policy to the data. These are productsthat have been already evaluated on a larger scale, althoughnone of these products are based on any open standard thatcould be compatible in the cloud, and actually only RMS wasadapted for the cloud use. RMS IRM construct uses eXtensiblerights Markup Language (XrML), the rights expression lan-guage that was designed for closed environments (
Microsoft
Corporation, 2008 ) where cloud implementation was not considered. XrML ( ContentGuard, 2001 ) and XACML ( Saldhana et al.,
2013 ) are very similar; however the semantics used to express
access rights are different. Both define tuples where subjectis permitted to perform specific activity defined by predicateagainst access object. In XrML, a condition is a functionalequivalent of XACML obligations, although what give XACMLadvantages are complex expressions and predicates with nega-tive and deny assertions. Note that XrML supports only positiveassertion.367 computers & security 70 (2017) 366-375
XrML defines basic rights and digital licensing where the
issuer of the license is effectively an owner of the digital asset.Same as XACML, XrML supports basic cryptographic func-tions. While XACML leverages eXtensible Stylesheet LanguageTransformation (XSLT) to constrain the policy, XrML uses tem-plates within a license document. Both standards supportexternal references to policy internal elements. Flexibility ofboth in terms of elements extensions is similar; these are termsthat could be redefined to extend the schema.
Despite of XrML strengths, fundamentally it is not suited
for complex access policies and rules while XACML is in-tended to be suitable for a variety of application environments(
Saldhana et al., 2013 ) what perfectly fits various cloud
configurations.
XACML itself formulates Attribute-Based Access Control
(ABAC) phrases. Its profiles can support different access controlmodels as per
Anderson (2004) , and Gasparini (2013) . This policybased access control model delivers time constrained accessfunctionality as well as capabilities to handle emergency accessrequests like in Break-Glass scenario. Cross-Enterprise Secu-rity and Privacy Authorization (XSPA) (
Mohammad and
DeCouteau, 2014 ) is a XACML profile dedicated for large enterprise use but mostly for healthcare institutions that exchangeinformation across various security boundaries. Technically theXACML is not a part of any particular cryptographic primitivetherefore actual implementation can quickly adapt any newfunctional requirements. XACML offers various features likeJavaScript Object Notation (JSON) (
Lockhart and Parducci, 2014 )
profile to format the policy using light-sized attributes repre-sentation in compare to heavier XML predecessor. Furthermoreit could represent legacy access control objects structure viaefficient serialization as described in
Karjoth and Schade
(2013) .
Very interesting XACML evaluation has been conducted as
a part of Next Generation Access Control (NGAC) model de-velopment (
Ferraiolo et al., 2016 ). Other research on secure data
sharing in the cloud ( Brown and Blough, 2015 ), (Li et al., 2015 ),
(Pearson et al., 2015 ) show that sticky policies approach meets
major cloud-based data sharing security objectives. T wo works(
Brown and Blough, 2015 ), (Li et al., 2015 ) discuss similar constructions to sticky policies with IBE and prove its applicability,nonetheless described here use of IBE with XACML sticky-policy as a public key simplifies the proposed method.
Promising approach of using lattice-based cryptography with
IBE (
Ducas et al., 2014 ) makes any construct with Identity Based
Encryption IBE ready for quantum computing.
Recently widely discussed data protection approaches are
access models based on Attributes Based Encryption (ABE).Concept itself combines cryptography and elements of accesscontrol. Its early implementations (
Sahai and Waters, 2005 ) suffered from dynamic membership control, however later(
Boldyreva et al., 2008 ), (Fan et al., 2014 ), (Hur and Noh, 2011 )
ABE was reviewed and empowered with attribute revocationfunctionality that enabled actually the fundamental accesscontrol functions required for cloud-based access manage-ment. Each ABE construct (
Sahai and Waters, 2005 ), (Yao et al.,
2004 ), (Fan et al., 2014 ), (Hur and Noh, 2011 ), (Yang and Jia, 2014 )
concentrates on cryptographic operations under several attri-butes. ABE makes cloud-based authorization a cryptography-centric due to highly constrained implementations by selectedABE primitives. ABE implementations leverage many fundamental access control techniques like Break-Glass (
Li et al., 2012 )
where data could be accessed in an emergency scenario witha post-factum approval or a justification. Also time con-strained attributes (
Hur and Noh, 2011 ), (Yang and Jia, 2014 )
technique that compliments access control system using ABE.Despite of fact that ABE is functionally related to Attribute-Based Access Control (ABAC) model seems it has never beenwider discussed in context of standardization to simplify globalintegration for secure and flexible access control.
3. Identity-based cryptography
Shamir, in his work on public key encryption scheme ( Shamir,
1985 ), suggested that it is possible to build a secure construction where communicating parties could use a simple text asa public key without a need of keys exchange. Based onShamir's research, Dan Boneh and Matthew Franklin (
2003 ) constructed an efficient fully functional Identity Based Encryption(IBE) scheme using Pairing Based Cryptography (PBC). Pro-posed solution provided indistinguishability under chosencipher-text attack, in other words adversary given an en-crypted message randomly chosen from a two-element messagespace could not determine the plain-text message.
Under IBE scheme, a message sender can take any arbitrary text known to receiver and use it as a public key. Whileplain text does not require any further cryptographic safe-guards, a message receiver requires authentication that provesownership of the public key, i.e. email address. Using emailaddress as a public key here helps communicating parties toshare information about Trust Authority (TA) what con-strains the key domain for pairing operations.
Proposed here construction rearranges IBE schema model
entities like in
Sahai and Waters (2005) , where Alice does not
use Bob's identity, only the XACML-formatted sticky policy thatidentifies the data in a security context is mapped into a publickey. Bob does not use his private key but after he is autho-rized by Trust Authority (TA) policy engine, a private key iscalculated from the sticky policy and TA master key.
4. IBE-enabled sticky policy
Proposed solution combines several Identity-Based Encryp-tion (IBE) and policy framework components into simplifiedmodel. Trust Authority (TA) is responsible for policy manage-ment as well as for key management. It maintains policytemplates (see
Fig. 1 ) for TA responsible contexts, e.g. internal medical templates, private banking customer templates orhuman resources external candidate templates.
Policy templates together with the TA delivers IBE parameters to the editor application required to generate a policypublic key. Based on a policy request, the TA also makes anaccess decision or delegates part of the decision to a third partyTA. TA stores master key {s}for its domain (see
Fig. 2 ) and after
positive access request decision, it generates a policy privatedecryption key that is leased to the policy enforcement editorapplication. Client or server based editor application handles368 computers & security 70 (2017) 366-375
read or read-write document access based on response from
the TA.
How Sticky Policy-enabled OOXML protects files? Authenticated against verified Identity Provider (IdP) Alice in order toprotect the document selects preferred Trust Authority (TA) froma list of registered TAs, this way she receives a template of pos-sible policy rules in a given security context (see
Fig. 1 ). After
defining policy access rules, the policy set is extended by Alicerights, and together with document global unique identifier anda TA reference the sticky policy is ready to protect the document.
Alice, to encrypt the document using IBE BF (
Boneh and
Franklin, 2003 ) setup, requires policy public key QPOLtherefore she generates
QH P O LPOL ID= ()1 , (1)where H1is a hash function defined on group G1of prime order
q such as H1101:, * {} →*G, which maps sticky policy POL IDinto
a single point on an elliptic curve.

modulo qand calculates parameters:

mand bilinear map ê. Bilinear map êover Alice's public key

therefore used in IBE BF ( Boneh and Franklin, 2003 ) modulo

Next, both values U, V are stored inside the OOXML document wrapper and together with embedded sticky policyare shared in the cloud. Parameters same as other OOXMLwrapped data should be protected by cloud provider at rest andin transit.
How Sticky Policy-enabled OOXML opens protected file? Authenticated by verified Identity Provider (IdP) Bob accessing thedocument presents the policy with access request to Trust Au-thority (TA) using TA reference from sticky policy. 5. Security of sticky policies IBE
In evaluated Boneh-Franklin IBE the model security dependsmostly on difficulty of solving bilinear Diffie Hellman problem(BDHP) (
Boneh and Franklin, 2003 ) and also correct parameters selection that is must for making discrete logarithmproblem (DLP) hard to solve. Based on assumption that prob-ability Prof finding message m using algorithm

6. Sticky policies IBE authenticity
An adversary cannot tamper a policy attached to the data usingproposed method. Acting as a public key, the sticky policy isauthenticated by IBE scheme. Like in any symmetric or asym-metric encryption only the right key can be used to decryptthe cipher-text. IBE is a public key asymmetric cryptographicprimitive therefore for a given public key encrypting themessage exists one private key decrypting the cipher-text withthis message. If an adversary would try to change the stickypolicy attached to the data in this construct after TA autho-rizes falsified request the received private key cannot be usedto decrypt the cipher-text. Adversary having the advantage ina policy engine authorization flow (i.e. Trust Authority) stillcannot divulge the message by tampering the sticky policy.
The accepted security notion for the model (
Nigel et al., 2014 )
that could provide data non-repudiation assurance with an extracryptographic operation is an Authenticated Identity-BasedEncryption (Authenticated IBE). It delivers both messageconfidentiality and non-repudiation on top of IBE scheme (
Lynn,
2002 ). To implement this authorship safeguard either sticky policy
or OOXML document meta-data should carry information aboutthe data owner. Sender, i.e. Alice, using own private key, can au-thenticate the encryption. Albeit it requires policy private keybeing leased by the Trust Authority (TA) during initial encryption.
If data integrity is required there are existing Identity Based
Signature (IBS) schemes (
Cha and Cheon, 2002 ). This safeguard
is more expensive than non-repudiation as requires separateencryption and signing operations, while Authenticated IBE is
even faster than actual IBE encryption. Considering other avail-able technologies for integrity and non-repudiation Blockchainmight be a preferred option as unlike signature it verifies datain a historical context (
Okupski, 2015 ). Furthermore, Blockchain
service together with Trust Authority (TA) may govern any il-legitimate re-encryption attempt of the amended data. Changeddocument despite of initially defined sticky policy rights givingonly Read rights can be rejected by TA therefore change willnot be added to the block chain.
Furthermore, a well-defined policy template could highly
constrain a sticky policy in a given context as well as enforcerequirement to fill an authenticated originator attribute.Depends on the implementation each legitimate amend-ment made would require re-encryption with a different, newdocument version and it would require unique identifier of anauthenticated change originator. XACML policy defines two safe-guards, an obligation and an advice (see
Fig. 4 ). Both could carry
further instructions for policy enforcement point behind editorapplication defining how to handle the initial authorization in-cluding basic requirements for data re-encryption underupdated policy.
7. Sticky policies authorization
Sticky policies carry authorization information required toprotect the data. Unlike conventional policy framework wherepolicy are centrally stored and referenced to data, here policyis attached to the data and follows it to enforce access controlrules. Policy evaluation upon access request can check who you
are,what you have ,what you know ,where you are and when and
how you can access the data. Like in countries that adapted OECD
data protection directives (
OECD, 2013 ), owner consent related
to data access can be represented as an access rule and com-bined into a policy set. As mentioned before data access canbe constrained by time. E.g. sticky policy added to a financialreport would define any subject rights to process the reportwithin a defined time slot and before or after a specific date.
XACML access request construct represents access tuples,
with subject, object and predicate. Subject is the data owner ordata processor who wishes to access the object. Object is theresource document that can be represented by cloud data hostingprovider path and unique data identifier. Predicate defines anaction that subject is entitled to based on the policy rules. Becauseof its internal XML structure XACML policies are defined via at-tributes represented by name/value pairs. XACML sticky policysubject can be constrained by a technical Role (
Anderson, 2004 )
represented as a group in a target system, where e.g. Role isequal «BusinessEngineering». Because sticky policy remainsunencrypted its attribute values could be anonymized or ob-fuscated as a further safeguard. «BusinessEngineering» Role couldbe represented by a global unique identifier (see
Fig. 3 ) from within
given Trust Authority context. Several Attribute Based Encryp-tion (ABE) works proposed attributes representation using binary-state attributes where attributes unlike in arbitrary-state binaryattributes do not directly disclose any information about thecontent of the protected message. XACML rules may remain inarbitrary-state however in a form that requires attributes mappingto some predefined encoded unique attributes.370 computers & security 70 (2017) 366-375
XACML policy simply combines Rules, Policies and PolicySets
into Policies or PolicySets (see Fig. 4 ) to protect the resource
and enforce access rights defined by data owner. Possibility ofPolicy and PolicySet nesting gives many possibilities to repre-sent access conditions, however from architectural perspectiveit seems reasonable to keep the policy relatively flat and con-strained by templates from a given TA context.
XACML policy defines multiple subjects construct with more
than one subject involved in access control decision (
Saldhana
et al., 2013 ). This technique implements separation of duties
security principle. This non-cryptographic safeguard can havefunctional application similar to Attribute-based Encryption(ABE) or Shamir shared secret (
Shamir, 1979 ) concept. While
entire access model document is not cryptographically pro-tected the TA still can reject document access and its decryptionif not all policy conditions are met. I.e. document could be accessed only if all subjects agree to open the file.
Interesting functional part that is defined by XACML are obligations and advice. Obligation is a must requirement compared
to non-obligatory advice, which canbe considered during access
control decision. Obligation is a directive specifying obliga-tory operation after access request decision. For example, it caninstruct to raise a security incident after Eve was denied accessto the data. Advice can instruct Bob to use his academic emailidentity because he does not have a valid educational
ac.uk
domain address. Important feature of both obligation as wellas advice is the fact that these can enforce data re-encryptionunder larger key space or even different cryptographic method.
Data access control implementation based on XACML sticky
policy can efficiently secure confidential information and Per-sonal Identifiable Information (PII), provide high accountabilitywhere single data access attempt is a subject of auditing(
Pearson and Bramhall, 2003 ). Comprehensive implementation of sticky policy model could support advanced securityauditing where, security breach or a data leakage incident canbe reported and collected, giving significant evidence for furtherlegal investigation. Policy construction is highly simplified withpolicy templates that could be pre-defined by each TA. Policytemplate can represent required access evaluation context tobe included in the policy (see
Fig. 5 ).
Policy template will use attribute designators to set the
correct rules in the right context (see Fig. 6 ).
Constrained XACML policy template simplifies policy generation and reduces complexity on the client side allowing onlypre-defied set of rules to be configured.<Policy>
<Rule Effect="Permit">
<Target>
<Subject "GROUP(BusinessEngineering):{956EFF...}"/>
<Resource "TA_URI/{8781F074-FAB1-4D5D-
BBF0...}"/>
<Action "Read"/>
</Target>
</Rule>
</Policy>

XACML policy defines which individual or group of individuals in what configuration (i.e. location, time) can be grantedpermissions to access the protected data in the cloud. Stickypolicies implemented based on XACML suffice wide range ofaccess control implementations. This includes modern cloud-enabled authentication and authorization frameworks whichleverage SAML and Claims-based authentication like in (
Bertocci,
2016 ) where individual prior to authorization would be a subject
of authentication (see Fig. 7 and Fig. 8 ) involving third party
Identity Providers (IdP).
8. Evaluation
Evaluation of proposed secure sticky policy construction wasperformed on selected functional part of the model com-pared to functional equivalent in Microsoft RMS Online service(
Sergey et al., 2015 ). As discussed earlier, sticky policy is used
to generate a symmetric key under IBE for AES. In MS RMS, thesymmetric AES key for data encryption is generated sepa-rately. RMS encrypts AES key using RSA as a key wrap, thencipher-text is attached to the data. Therefore, here, the evalu-ation looks only into the initial process of policy setup includingAES key protection without actual data encryption (i.e. AES 256).Discussed here MS RMS as well as Oracle IRM both use 2048as a default RSA asymmetric key size (
Sergey et al., 2015 ),(Martin and Peet, 2010 ) as well as AES with 256 key size. RSA
of 3072 and 4096 key sizes are added into evaluation scope asrecommended key size for any practical future use as per report(
Nigel et al., 2014 ) and also as a required key size if consider
more complex large size policies. Considering InformationRights Management (IRM) data access model itself, the cryp-tographic operations performance of all the crypto primitivesfrom the evaluation would be satisfactory therefore even slowerIBE with short pairings requiring more computational timewould be acceptable. This part of evaluation is strictly illus-trative to correlate IBE as a preferred policy encryption primitiveamong already used ones key wrap encryption methods.
Evaluation used the Ben Lynn's PBC libraries from Stanford University, while OpenSSL open source project librarieswere used for RSA evaluation part. Sticky policy was mappedinto a key space using IBE Boneh and Franklin (IBE-BF) scheme.IBE performance was compared to RSA encryption of pseudorandomly generated AES 256 key with no policy attached.
Results were calculated based on 400 different XACML
polices of size between 2[KB] and 18 [KB] for IBE and pseudorandomly generated AES 256 symmetric keys for RSA. Resultsshow (see
Fig. 9 ) that both RSA 2048 and RSA 3072 perform
better and require less time to complete cryptographic opera-tions of than IBE to pair XACML policy into AES key space.
Evaluation shows that IBE used in our construct performed well compare to RSA even though tests did not calculatepolicy protection operations. Performance-wise, IBE does notstay behind other cryptographic primitives considering the factthat it offers similar encryption strength. Furthermore otherresearch claims that PBC performance under specific configu-ration could be compared to AES 256 (
Scott, 2011 ) therefore it
seems that for evaluation the cryptographic operations per-formance will not be the main concern. The most importantIBE advantage has over RSA is to what extent it can simplifythe IRM model. The process of attaching the access policy andencrypting symmetric key using RMS, which implements RSAin comparison to what is described here, IBE-enabled stickypolicy is complex, non-generic and product specific.
By looking at 400 arbitrary-state policies (see
Fig. 10 ) defining different rules in medical context for external andinternal use, all verified with valid XACML request and
Fig. 7 -Sticky policy IBE secure sharing.
Fig. 8 -Sticky policy IBE secure access.
Fig. 9 -Times of sticky policy mapping into AES key space
using IBE-BF compared to 2048, 3072 and 4096 RSAoperations applied to AES 256 key.372 computers & security 70 (2017) 366-375
response message, we could see that any potential symmetric key wrap with asymmetric encryption would require ratherlarge keys. Serializing 400 policies from standard eXtensibleMarkup Language (XML) format into size-wise lighter JavaScriptObject Notation (JSON) shows that rich rule expression thatwould suffice cloud-based implementation requires larger policysizes. Policy cannot be protected with asymmetric key, there-fore it requires either standard symmetric key encryption orcould apply some more complex construct that leverages bothsymmetric and asymmetric encryption (
Sergey et al., 2015 )
9. Contribution
Security considerations for sticky policies are important forany sensitive data protection model where parties need tosecurely exchange the data across different security bound-aries. This research compliments various projects aiming tosecurely bring privacy protection and cloud together, espe-cially health-care strategy of moving into a cloud. It contributesto medical data sharing frameworks recently prototyped andevaluated approaches that leverage sticky policies model. Itsimplifies data encryption from the proposed frameworks byproviding novel application of Identity Based Encryptionscheme for sticky policies. Many sticky policy based solu-tions propose use of key-wrap and policy-wrap model buthere policy remains obfuscated in plain text attached to adata. This gives much more flexibility and allows accesscontrol decisions to be taken before any cryptographic opera-tions are made.
While other existing rights management systems inherit
the best security safeguards from their legacy predecessors,this approach is designed for the cloud use and therefore couldadapt selected modern security techniques that were invented to overcome limitations of legacy data protectionsystems.
10. Future work
Each part of the proposed model here was prototyped andevaluated separately. Research work requires model workflowsto perform a large-scale evaluation with complete model simu-lation proving that global implementation will not be anoverhead for Trust Authority from IBE parameters and key man-agement perspective. For better evaluation it is recommendedto deliver an on-premise solution implementation that wouldreside on an end-user machine and control documents wouldbe opened on a low driver and memory level. This work wouldbe focused on policy enforcement using system MiniDriver'sto intercept low-level document opening instructions and takefurther access decisions via policy engine.
IBE and RSA performance evaluation could be extended by
Microsoft RMS policy encryption, however this requires manypolicy samples from different templates while this would haveno significant impact on measured cryptographic operationtimes. Pairing Based Cryptography (PBC) parameters selec-tion is still an open topic. Latest IBE schemes like IBE overLattices (
Ducas et al., 2014 ) could highly improve the model
security, and while simplified IBE from Boneh and Franklin WeilPairing seems sufficient for prototyping, an actual implemen-tation with only asymmetric encryption will require other IBEschemes. From data integrity perspective it might be worth butnot necessary to consider a research around blockchain tech-nology towards high integrity and non-repudiation of data inspecific contexts.
Fig. 10 -Policy sizes comparison formatted with XML and Json. XACML policies.373 computers & security 70 (2017) 366-375

11. Summary
There are many security gaps that need to be analyzed before
any cloud data sharing framework can be used to host sensi-tive data. This paper shows how to address some of the threatsand how the presented solution could protect the data. Modelsimplicity might be very attractive for implementations thatlook for a simple solution with a potential to be extended byeither additional cryptographic primitive or various obfusca-tions and encoding methods. Authors suggest that AES is onlyone of many possibilities for data encryption and under otherIBE schemes the well-designed sticky policies model mightbecome even a simpler solution.
Using unencrypted access policy attached to the data helps
identify the data as well as the owner in the cloud space. Trivialattributes encoding and anonymization should be sufficientto protect the policy from malicious use. By introducing well-defined policy templates, the policy controls when the datacould be accessed, when the data should be re-encrypted withlatest TA public key or when the data processor should renewIBE parameters for a given TA.
By keeping authorization and authentication components
separate from the actual cryptographic operations on dataunlike in ABE, allows a faster development of existing modelsand easier integration with existing frameworks that are alreadyused for large-scale implementations. Existing ABE-based imple-mentations if used seem over-engineered and therefore stillneed to reach the maturity level sufficient for large and complexenvironments.
Furthermore, MS RMS Online is an IRM product that
requires existing data governance and for more moderatelarge organizations that care about data protection is stillusually deployed only on-premises. This IRM system haslegacy XrML policy format limitations and therefore may notbe sufficient for large-scale cloud-based access managementsystems. MS RMS Online introduced various features likeOne Time Passwords (OTP) and Hardware Security Module(HSM) what helps companies with secure cloud on-boarding,however there are clear technical limitations that wouldrequire further research and development. Nevertheless, MSRMS is not an open standard and there are IRM marketcompetitors. There seems to be no single secure data sharingservice that would suit large organizations and enterprisesas well as small businesses. Sticky policies with IBE imple-mentation when clearly defined can become a good openalternative for the existing IRM market.
In regards to actual implementation of XACML entities and
XACML policy generation, Axiomatics and WSO
2have products and set of stable libraries ready to combine with existingaccess management systems. Oracle formally Sun Microsystems,Quest Software and other larger software companies have alsoown XACML implementations, however these are either dis-continued or closed for integration with third party systems. Todevelop an OASIS XACML complaint product is highly simplifieddue to various open projects with source code available via publiccode repositories.
To introduce other less functional and more security related
techniques, some of the fundamental policy engine and keymanagement components were not discussed here.
