CSP should take consent from the user by following underlying regulations. Our scope is to identify the users who are under attack, analyse the collected evidences and then come up 
with forensic conclusions by ensuring the principle of chain 
of custody.

3. Related work 
As said, the feature of cloud VM snapshot is introduced for the 
backup purposes and not intend to solve the forensic challenges. So only limited work exists on using the cloud VM 
snapshots for forensic investigation. In Belorkar et al. (2011) , the authors used existing VNsnap 
model and then modified it to take the periodic snapshots during the time of attack. The advantages of their approach are: 
• They ensured the integrity of the acquired evidences which 
is important in the court of law. 
• The downtime of the cloud VM while capturing snapshots 
is less. 
• They have used fuzzy clustering techniques to increase the 
detection accuracy of cloud attacks. 
The drawbacks of their work are: 
• Their approach lacks practical implementation and the 
questions like, how to analyze a captured VM snapshot is 
not discussed. 
• The role of the CSP in capturing the snapshots is not mentioned without which forensic investigation in the cloud is 
highly difficult. The same is conformed by Khan and Suleman (2016) and Alqahtany and Saad (2016) . 
• They did not validate their approach using any evaluation 
parameters like, data loss and integrity of the evidence. 
In Rani et al. (2015) , the authors proposed a model that 
gives the details about using cloud VM snapshots for forensic investigation. The advantages of their approach are: 
• Identified various possibilities of enabling forensic investigation using snapshots. 
• They suggested IDS integration at VM and hypervisor level 
to know the exact triggering point for capturing snapshots. 
Their approach is limited because: 
• They suggested to deploy the Intrusion Detection Systems 
(IDS) at each cloud VM. This will not work in the real cloud 
environment as IDS could be disabled by a user who is having malicious intentions. 
• No mechanisms were suggested to reduce the storage 
occupancy of the collected snapshots. 
• Analyzing snapshots is different from analyzing other evidences and there was no discussion on how they can be 
analyzed. 
In Roussev and Vassil (2016) , the authors developed a forensic prototypes which can acquire the data from public cloud 
environments. The contributions and capabilities of their prototypes are: 
• They concluded that the client side evidences will not help 
the investigator to conduct cloud forensic analysis. 
• They built three interesting forensic prototypes for cloud 
drive acquisition (kumodd), Google docs Acquisition 
(kumodocs) and cloud drive data preservation (kumofs). 
The drawbacks with their approach are: 
• The tool works only for the SaaS models and cannot be applied to IaaS cloud environments. 
86 (2019) 92-111 95 
• The challenges of data gravity were not handled. This is 
especially important when the huge sized evidences like 
VM snapshots have to be acquired and then transferred to 
the investigator's environment through network. 
• Their approach is generic and did not emphasize on the 
VM snapshot acquisition and analysis. 
In Almulla et al. (2013) , the authors experimented on 
whether traditional snapshot acquisition techniques can be 
applied to the cloud VM snapshots or not. They proposed a 
framework for snapshot acquisition ensuring its admissibility. 
They did not mention any practical findings of their approach. 
We discuss and address the above issues in the rest of the 
paper. 
4. Improving the availability of the cloud 
virtual machine snapshots 
There are several Cloud Forensic Readiness (CFR) models 
and the motivation behind each of them is to increase the 
evidence availability for forensic investigation ( Kebande et al., 
2014; Marco et al., 2013 ). The core idea of these models is 
to acquire the possible evidences before the incident itself. 
Unfortunately, each of them is faced with its own set of 
drawbacks i.e. 
• The monitoring capabilities of most of the existing models are deployed at VM level. This may make intruder to 
alter/stop the running CFR model. 
• Some CFR models rely on the user to collect the evidences 
which may be an overhead to the user. 
• Did not mention many practical details about the suspicious activity detection in a VM. 
To overcome the above, we propose an improved Cloud 
Forensic Readiness Model (iCFR). It is deployed at hypervisor 
level and uses the Virtual Machine Introspection (VMI) techniques to detect suspicious activities of the target VM ( Raju 
et al., 2016 ). It is important to note that, iCFR also uses the 
cloud service logs stored in the hypervisor to analyze the VM 
level activities. Once the suspicious activity is detected, the 
target evidences are collected. Here, the evidences of our interest are snapshots. The trust of the user on cloud is reducing with the reported recent cloud attacks. Any CSP's objective 
is to provide reliable services to the user. To make this possible, CSPs are spending a lot of money and they are increasing the replication factor in the cloud data centres ( Chen and 
Sion, 2017; Linthicum, 2016 ). In view of cloud forensics, the 
CSP stores the evidences (here, snapshots) only when a suspicious activity is detected. This in turn reduces the space consumption and the investigator can come with reliable conclusions. To make the evidence management (collection, storage 
and analysis) a reality, the user should subscribe to the paid 
feature like Forensic-as-a-Service (FaaS). 
The definition of a suspicious activity may vary depending on the organization policies and cloud provider terms and 
conditions. In general, the activities which can be treated as 
suspicious are, over resource consumption, violation of security 
policies, an attempt to obtain unauthorized access, modifications without the actual owner instruction or knowledge etc . For illustration, we applied iCFR to know whether the activity of over resource consumption by a VM is suspicious or not ( Section 4.1 ). 
4.1. Measuring the resource consumption of virtual 
machines in the cloud environment 
For this, iCFR uses the default metering service running in the 
cloud hypervisor and measures the resources consumed by 
the target VM. If the VM resources are over utilized then we 
may consider the activities running in it as suspicious. But in 
all the cases, over utilization should not be concluded as suspicious due to the rapid elasticity property of cloud. To reduce 
the false positives, iCFR checks multiple conditions i.e. 
We consider the recent resource consumption history of a 
target VM and if there is a drastic deviation from its average 
resource consumption then we may consider that as suspicious (Base Condition). To conform whether the over resource 
consumption is suspicious or not, along with the base condition we check many additional conditions. Some of them are 
presented below: 
1. To strengthen the base condition, we check the extent of 
VM errors generated during the same time in the cloud service logs. We consider this as one of the supporting conditions because, when the over resource consumption in 
cloud is non-suspicious, service logs may not generate error events. If the number of errors is more than usual, then 
with more belief, one can conform the over resource consumption as suspicious. It is important to note that, service 
logs reside at the hypervisor level and the events in those 
logs cannot be deleted/altered by the intruder VM. 
2. If the same VM is used from different geographical locations at the same time then it increases the confidence to 
confirm the resource consumption as suspicious. 
3. We also check whether there is an over resource consumption or not, especially after observing multiple login failures from the same system. If they are present, it indicates that the intruder had compromised the original cloud 
user account and he (intruder) started over-consuming the 
resources in a short time. 
These are only a few and new rules can be framed in accordance with the underlying cloud SLAs to strengthen the suspiciousness of the activity. iCFR selects and checks the rules 
depending on the suspicious activity. 
4.2. Scenario testing: over-resource consumption of a VM 
in Openstack cloud 
Experimental Setup : We setup Openstack private IaaS cloud 
with four rack servers, each with a high end configuration i.e. 
server 1: Controller node, server 2: Compute node, server 3: 
Storage node, server 4: Object storage node. 
Each VM will be billed and charged according to its usage. In Openstack, it is achieved by the ceilometer service. The 
testing is performed on a VM named test2_010316_clr which is 
present in the demo user account. We queried various service 
logs pertaining to the ceilometer service and then calculated 
the average resource consumption of test2_010316_clr VM. In 
Fig. 1 -C P U utilization of the target VM. 
Fig. 2 - Read-write cycles calculated for the target VM. 
Fig. 1 , the middle line shows the average CPU utilization of 
that VM and the top line shows the upper standard deviation. 
We calculated the read-write cycles during the same time period and observed a deviation ( Fig. 2 ). We observed that, these 
deviations are more than the threshold value. This indicated 
us that, the resources which are over consumed may be used 
for performing suspicious activities. To confirm this, we also 
checked other framed conditions i.e. 
Error events in the cloud service logs: When there is an 
increase in the resource consumption, we observed that for 
non-victim VM, the error events are stable ( Fig. 3 ). But for 
a suspicious VM (i.e. here, test2_010316_clr ), the number of 
error events increased drastically ( Fig. 4 ). Since this deviation 
is unusual when compared to the history of test2_010316_clr 
VM, the argument of considering over resource utilization as 
suspicious can be strengthened. 
Multiple logins at the same time: We initially identified the 
system IP from which cloud VM ( test2_010316_clr ) is being accessed. From the IP-172.16.6.186 ( IP 1 ), there are few login failures. After receiving a complaint by the victim from IP 1 regarding failed login attempts, we observed that there are multiple 
successful logins from different systems with IPs-172.16.6.193 
( IP 2 ), 172.16.6.112 ( IP 3 ), 172.16.6.124 ( IP 4 ). This confirms the CSP 
(or investigative agency) that test2_010316_clr VM was compromised and misused by multiple hosts, each having different IPs. We got this information by normalizing the format of various cloud service logs. Then we integrated all of those to form 
a single log revealing the details as shown in Fig. 5 . 
After verifying multiple conditions, we confirm whether 
an over consumption of resources may be considered as 
suspicious or not. Thus, the number of false positives are reduced. This is a simple scenario to detect a suspicious activity 
using the conditions mentioned above. Defining multiple 
customized conditions will help the investigator to detect a 
suspicious activity. Once a suspicious activity is detected, we start 
capturing multiple snapshots of the corresponding VM at frequent 
intervals till the effect of the incident is reduced . 
If the investigator has multiple snapshots of the target VM 
then it will definitely aid the investigator to perform faster indepth analysis and generate better logical conclusions about 
the incident. During the process of transferring the cloud VM 
snapshots to the investigator environment, we identified that 
the following issues need to be addressed: 
• Data gravity: Transferring the giga or tera bytes of snapshots to the investigator environment via network is time 
consuming and error prone ( Section 5 ). 
• Legally admissible: The evidences transferred to the investigator's infrastructure should be complete and reliable 
( Section 5 ). 
86 (2019) 92-111 97 
Fig. 3 -N o . of non-target VM error events in the service logs. 
Fig. 4 -N o . of target VM error events increased drastically in service logs. 
To achieve both the above, we propose a model namely 
SNAPS- Sna pshot based P rovenance S ystem which we describe in the next section. It is not uncommon to store the 
user data in cross border states and this aggravates the cloud 
forensic process. Each country has its own privacy laws for 
securing the user data. For example, Spain is very stringent 
about user privacy and the CSP should get consent from the 
user in writing before proceeding to the cloud forensic investigation. On the other side, Argentina has an exception in getting the user consent when it is linked with legal obligations. 
This motivated many of the researchers to discuss cross border legal challenges and approaches ( Dykstra et al., 2011 ). This 
is an ongoing challenge and that the proposed approach has 
dependency on regional/international privacy laws. For example, when an incident happened, how to collect the evidences 
which are spread across multiple jurisdictions, and how to convince the court of law based on the logical conclusions 
made out of cloud forensic investigation. 
5. Proposed model for efficient storage of 
cloud VM snapshots 
The domain of cloud forensics introduces new challenges for 
the investigator in various aspects like, technical, organizational and legal issues. Most of the researchers in the cloud 
forensics go with the assumption that the investigator will get 
support from the CSP during forensic examination ( Alqahtany 
and Saad, 2016; Khan and Suleman, 2016 ). Our approach also 
assumes support from the CSP. With this assumption we have 
designed and deployed the some of the SNAPS modules at the 
cloud side and other modules at the investigator side. 
Fig. 5 -L o g events showing multiple login failures. 
SNAPS contains various modules like, Monitoring module 
(deployed at CSP), Snapshot accumulator (deployed at CSP), 
spatio-temporal modules-iRSM (deployed at CSP) and iSTSM 
(deployed at the Investigator side), Regeneration module 
(deployed at the Investigators side). Each of these modules is 
described as below. 
5.1. Modules of the SNAPS 
• Monitoring Module: This collects the data regarding the VM 
activities. We use virtual machine introspection (VMI) technique and cloud service logs to collect the target VM events. 
Those are then analyzed to check for suspicious events. 
This module also uses iCFR capabilities for evidence collection. The advantage of this is, the functionality of the 
module cannot be altered/stopped by the intruder as all 
the monitoring capabilities of the module are from the hypervisor which is generally inaccessible to any user. 
• Snapshot Accumulator: Once a suspicious activity is detected in a VM, the snapshots of it will be captured at 
frequent intervals of time. Each of those snapshots are stored in a sandbox environment (It is an isolated space to 
which only the CSP has access). Before sending the snapshots to the investigator's environment they are passed to 
the spatio-temporal module (iRSM) deployed at the cloud 
end. The details about iRSM is discussed in the following 
subsection. 
5.2. Spatio-temporal model for efficient storage of cloud 
VM snapshots 
In general, spatial temporal models are used when the data 
has both the time and space attributes. Research in this 
domain had started three decades ago and most of the 
research papers used these models to solve the environmental issues. For example, the occurrence of acid rains in 
New York (USA) has been predicted along with the possible location and time ( Hernández-Campos et al., 2006 ). Various spatial-temporal models were proposed in the literature 
( Kebande et al., 2014; Raju et al., 2016 ). In this paper, we use 
two of those models namely Refined Snapshot Model (RSM) 
86 (2019) 92-111 99 
Fig. 6 -T h e organization of iRSM (deployed at cloud side to reduce the difficulties in the issue of data gravity) and iSTSM 
(deployed at investigators end to generate provenance). 
( Pelekis and Nikos, 2004 ) and Simple Time Stamping Model 
(STM) ( Fernndez and Cabrera, 1995 ) for efficient storage and 
analysis of the cloud VM snapshots. 
A brief on the two models is given below: 
• Refined Snapshot Model (RSM) ( Pelekis and Nikos, 2004 ): 
Initial snapshot is stored completely. Then the sequence 
of changes to it are stored as Delta objects. To get the 
current state, each delta object has to be applied on its 
previous complete snapshot. The existing literature in 
spatio-temporal domain considered snapshot as current 
state showing the geographical location. But in our case, 
snapshot refers to the current state of the cloud virtual 
machine. 
• Simple Time Stamping Model (STSM) ( Fernndez and Cabrera, 1995 ): Each object in this model is tagged with its creation time and cessation time. It also maintains links to 
the preceding and succeeding versions of the object. The 
advantage with this model is that the state of any object 
can be retrieved with respective to time. 
We identified that spatio-temporal models (RSM and STSM) 
cannot be directly applied to the cloud VM snapshots due to 
the following reasons. 
• As the number of VM snapshots in the sandbox goes 
higher, the number of delta objects which needs to get 
transferred to the investigator will also go high. When we 
passed all of them to the investigator's environment, we 
observed that the time to reconstruct the recent snapshot 
from its delta file is time consuming. The reason is, from 
the base snapshot to the current delta file, a sequence of 
multiple regenerations have to be performed. So, to reduce 
the time spent in regenerating a snapshot from its delta file, we modified the basic RSM and propose an improved 
version of RSM (i.e. iRSM). 
• Similarly we observed that, when STSM is directly applied 
to the cloud VM snapshots it cannot answer forensically 
relevant questions. For example, STSM only stores the time 
associated with an object. Due to this, the investigator can 
only get the objects which are modified/deleted at a certain 
point of time. But in most of the cases, the investigator is 
interested in both the spatial and temporal attributes. So 
we propose a new version from the basic STSM and we call 
it as iSTSM where each object in it is associated with both 
the time and location attributes. 
The organization of both the iRSM and iSTSM is shown in 
Fig. 6 . All the target VM snapshots in the cloud sandbox cannot be directly sent to the investigator as it can complicate the 
issue of data gravity. So all of them were passed through iRSM 
module where it considers to keep multiple full snapshots 
instead of only one. The investigator then receives multiple 
full snapshots and several delta files . But he/she cannot perform 
forensic analysis on the delta files. So, the investigator has 
to regenerate the complete snapshot from the corresponding 
delta file. 
One of our spatio-temporal modules (iRSM) reduces the 
time spent on regeneration of the snapshots. For example, if 
sandbox contains seven snapshots then there are two possible 
ways to store them. 
• Case 1 (for RSM): In this case, the first snapshot will only 
be stored completely S 1 full and remaining as delta files 
i.e. S 1 delta (1) , S 1 delta (2) , S 1 delta (3) , S 1 delta (4) , S 1 delta (5) and S 1 delta (6) . 
If the investigator wants to regenerate seventh snapshot 
from its delta file i.e. S 1 delta (6) then all its previous snapshots 
should also be regenerated which is a time consuming job 
Fig. 7 - Snapshot regeneration time for all the three approaches. 
especially when the number of snapshots in the sandbox 
is more. 
• Case 2 (for iRSM): For iRSM, we store a full snapshot followed by delta files then again a full snapshot followed by 
delta files i.e. S 1 full , S 1 delta (1) , S 1 delta (2) , S 1 delta (3) , S 2 full , S 2 delta (1) , 
S 2 delta (2) . Here, if investigator wants to regenerate seventh 
snapshot from its delta file i.e. S 2 delta (2) then the time to accomplish the same will be relatively less. The reason is, full 
snapshot S 2 full is present and the investigator directly uses 
this to get the seventh snapshot instead of using the initial 
full snapshot S 1 full every time. The intervals at which the 
full snapshots should be kept in the sandbox depends on 
the underlying cloud storage policy. 
This implies that iRSM benefits the investigator in several 
aspects. All of them are discussed and validated in the following subsection. 
5.3. Experiments and results 
With the said experimental setup in Section 4 , we created two 
virtual machines. One is configured with tinyOS and the other 
virtual machine holds Ubuntu Server. To illustrate the proposed model-SNAPS, we will present a simple investigative 
scenario. 
5.3.1. Scenario description 
Through our iCFR model, we captured several snapshots of the 
TinyOS VM after observing suspicious activity in it. All those 
snapshots are initially stored in the sandbox of the cloud. 
After the suspicious activity was confirmed then the spatiotemporal model-iRSM is applied. 
We compare our proposed approach (A3) with the existing 
approaches (A1 and A2) as given below. 
• Approach-1 (A1): In this, the captured snapshots will be 
stored in its original form. 
• Approach-2 (A2): Only one full snapshot is stored in the 
sandbox and remaining all snapshots of the same VM are 
stored as delta objects. • Approach-3 (A3-Proposed approach (SNAPS-iRSM)): Instead of one full snapshot, we will have multiple complete snapshots which are captured after every threshold 
number of delta objects. 
For comparison, we have taken eight snapshots of both the 
virtual machines separately. If the investigator's interest is to 
regenerate and analyze the recent snapshot (worst case) then 
the comparison parameters are: 
Regeneration Time : A3 has lesser regeneration time than A2 
due to its intermediate complete snapshots ( Fig. 7 ). It is important to note that, A1 has least regeneration time but even 
we do not consider it as an efficient approach due to its higher 
space occupancy. 
Space Consumption : There is no much difference in the space 
occupancy of the snapshots between A2 and A3. The reason 
is, among the eight snapshots there is only one additional 
full snapshot in A3 when compared with A2. The threshold 
for taking full snapshots is three i.e. after every three delta 
objects, full snapshot is captured. The threshold value can 
change depending on the number of snapshots for that virtual machine ( Fig. 8 ). 
So from the above discussion, it is clear that A3 is better 
than others in terms of handling data gravity issue with less 
space consumption, less regeneration time and less data loss. 
It is important to note that, the values of various parameters 
may change subjective to network bandwidth, investigator resources and extent of changes from one snapshot to the other 
but the logical conclusions made will not change. 
Ensuring Forensic Evidence Admissibility: In forensics, the acquired evidences should be legally admissible; otherwise the 
logical conclusions made out of them will not withstand in 
the court of law. Two main aspects to confirm evidence admissibility is completeness and no alteration. These properties are followed for all the three approaches (A1, A2 and A3). 
For a snapshot or a delta object we would associate the corresponding hash value and then send it to the investigator's environment. The investigator recalculates the hash value and 
if a mismatch occurs then it indicates that the snapshot is

same is shown in Table 2 . 
5.3.2. Analyzing a single regenerated snapshot 
Once snapshots are transferred from cloud infrastructure to 
the investigators environment using iRSM, then the two common tasks in the forensic process are: (1) Automatically regenerate a snapshot from its delta file such that the entire process should be legally admissible (2) Perform analysis on the 
regenerated snapshot. 
Using the forensic tools, the above tasks can be accomplished. But unfortunately, there is no forensic tool that can 
analyze cloud VM snapshots (to the best of our knowledge). So we developed a forensic prototype named as (CFI)-Cloud 
Forensic Investigator. The features of the developed tool are: 
• It identifies various evidences of the target virtual machine 
and acquires them from the cloud in a legally admissible 
manner. The evidences can be vdisk, vRAM, log events and 
snapshots. 
• Each acquired evidence is analyzed by the tool using its 
various modules. Some of them are Indexing, Tagging, 
Obfuscation finder, Unallocated data unit analyzer, Deleted 
files extractor etc. 
Mentioning each and every detail of our tool is out of scope 
for this paper. But we mention its features pertaining to the 
Snapshot Analysis . 
All the collected snapshots by the investigator will be 
given as input to the CFI. Then the investigator can select 
the snapshot of interest (complete or delta) for performing the analysis. When delta object is chosen at the back 
end, the regeneration of the complete snapshot will be 
done. For example, a forensic case was created containing 
eight snapshots i.e. Snapshot_1, Snapshot_2.diff, Snapshot_3.diff, Snapshot_4.diff, Snapshot_5, Snapshot_6.diff, 
Fig. 9 -C F I can regenerate the complete snapshot from its delta object. 
Snapshot_7.diff, Snapshot_8.diff ( Fig. 9 ). We selected the last 
delta object i.e. Snapshot_8.diff and then, its complete snapshot is regenerated and given as input to the analysis engine 
of our tool where the investigator can select and perform the 
required analysis tasks ( Fig. 10 ). 
Rest of the paper : Our CFI prototype can regenerate and analyze only a single snapshot at once. But in reality, the investigator may want to analyze multiple snapshots at a time 
and in that situation, the time for performing forensic analysis 
would further increase. To address this, the rest of the paper is 
organized as below: 
• Building provenance: It represents the history of an object. We propose that, building provenance from multiple 
snapshots would reduce the time for analyzing multiple 
snapshots ( Section 6 ). 
• Generating recommendations: Using the provenance system built, we generate the recommendations. Each of 
these recommendations will help the investigator to generate quick logical conclusions about the incident or the 
suspicious activity ( Section 6 and Section 7 ). 
The above two modules are deployed at the investigator's 
end. We use the other spatio-temporal model- iSTSM for 
building provenance . 
6. Building a provenance aware system for 
analyzing the cloud VM snapshots 
Provenance is a metadata that describes the object history 
( Gehani et al., 2012 ). It has a lot of forensic applications and we emphasize on two aspects of it i.e. to know how an object 
reached to the current state and to improve the quality of search . We 
built provenance using the objects in multiple snapshots. This 
section is organized as below: Based on the literature survey, 
we propose a taxonomy on existing provenance systems and 
the same is discussed in Section 6.1 . The novelty of SNAPS is 
discussed in Section 6.2 . The proposed Architecture and Algorithm for building the provenance is described in Section 6.3 . 
Then various experiments are conducted to evaluate the proposed provenance system-SNAPS in Section 6.4 . Finally, we 
present various advantages of SNAPS in Section 6.5 . 
6.1. Proposed taxonomy for existing provenance systems 
for cloud and non-cloud environments 
Upon extensive literature, we prepared a taxonomy of existing 
provenance systems and the same is shown in Fig. 11 . Provenance systems exist for both non-cloud and cloud environments. To collect provenance data, some provenance systems 
mandate the requirement of modifying the underlying file 
system (Eg: ProTrack, ProFS) and some other systems can collect the provenance data without any requirement of changing the target file system (Eg: SPADE) ( Gehani et al., 2012 ). Pro- 
Track is a provenance data collector and builds Versioning File 
System (VFS) ( Das, 2016 ). Most of the VFS based provenance 
systems suffers with memory overhead. ProFS is a non-VFS 
provenance system which will not suffer with memory overhead issues but it cannot provide effective querying capabilities ( Wagner and Alan., 2012 ). 
Similarly, provenance systems can also be built for the 
cloud environment. An initial effort in this direction is made 
by authors in Muniswamy-Reddy et al. (2010) . They have 
86 (2019) 92-111 103 
Fig. 10 -F e a t u r e s provided by the tool for analyzing a snapshot. 
Fig. 11 - High level proposed taxonomy for existing provenance systems. 
customized the existing Provenance Aware Storage System 
(PASS) to suit the cloud requirements. Their provenance collector has to be configured and installed in the target VM. This 
may not be always possible as the user of a VM may not be 
interested in the provenance data. Moreover, if the intruder 
attacking a VM finds a provenance system then he/she can 
disable it or atleast delete/modify the collected data. Observing these drawbacks, SNAPS proposes to capture and acquire the VM snapshots from the hypervisor level and then build 
the provenance for each object in multiple snapshots. 
6.2. Novelty of proposed SNAPS 
• SNAPS is first of its kind which builds provenance system 
from snapshots by considering various forensic aspects 
• It cannot be disabled by the intruder as it is hosted at VMM 
level 
• It facilitates efficient querying capabilities and can answer 
several forensically interesting questions like 
- Get all the objects modified between time t x and t y in 
the location Loc t 
-F i n d the most commonly modified object across multiple snapshots in the location Loc p 
-I d e n t i f y all the copied files in various locations within 
the given time range 
-T h e location which had undergone more changes etc., 
• Analyzing multiple snapshots is a time consuming task. 
SNAPS considers this and reduce the time spent on analyzing the snapshots. It accomplishes this using its recommendation engine. 
6.3. SNAPS architecture and algorithm to build 
provenance 
6.3.1. General terms 
The following terms are worth mentioning: 
• Provenance chains: Each object's history is represented as 
a provenance chain. All the nodes in a provenance chain 
represents the same object. A node to an O i provenance 
chain is added when there is a change in O i 's metadata. 
The provenance chain of O i will stop growing when it is 
deleted from the target VM. 
• Provenance graph: Combining provenance chains of all objects creates a provenance graph. Generally provenance 
graph is a DAG but we call the provenance graph generated from iSTSM as informal DAG ( Muniswamy-Reddy et al., 
2010 ). The reason of calling it as informal is, our provenance graph does not represent a cause and effect relation 
but can represent only the effect. 
6.3.2. Provenance builder 
Once the set of snapshots and delta files are transferred to 
the investigator's machine, then the forensic tool (here, CFI) 
will regenerate the complete snapshots. During the process of 
regeneration, it will build the provenance chain for each object using iSTSM as shown in Fig. 12 . Once the provenance 
system is constructed, any existing object can be queried to 
get its provenance chain using the query engine. The output 
of this engine is given to the recommendation module where 
it will give a set of logical clues about the object which was 
queried. The exact steps for provenance generation is given 
in the Algorithm 1 . 
6.4. Results and discussion 
We validated the SNAPS model using Openstack cloud. After 
detecting the suspicious activity in two Openstack VMs (Tiny 
OS VM and Ubuntu Server VM), SNAPS captured several snapshots of each of them at frequent intervals of time. A total of 
eight snapshots for each VM were captured and stored in the 
sandbox. After confirming the suspicious activity, all the snapshots are passed through our spatio-temporal model (iRSM) 
and then they are transferred to the investigator. Then the 
protocol followed by the investigator is shown in Fig. 13 . All Algorithm 1 Building the provenance chain for each queried

From the above, it implies that building provenance chains 
takes time and it depends on the size of the snapshots. The 
overhead in time is obvious as the indexer in the configured 
search engine has to go through each regenerated snapshot. 
We do not consider this as setback as this is done only once. 
On the other hand, we observed that querying the indexed 
provenance chains had less response time. Even with complex 
queries, we observed quick response time ( Table 4 ). 
If the investigator wants to analyze specific sets of objects across multiple snapshots then doing the same manually 
will consume a lot of time. To reduce this, our recommendation engine will give possible conclusions about those objects. 
The recommendation engine will work for simple scenarios to 
complex ones. Some of them are described in the next section. 
6.5. Advantages of our provenance system 
SNAPS benefits the investigator in multiple aspects i.e. 
• Resistant to Anti-Forensic measures: SNAPS can collect the 
evidences (here, snapshots) even though anti-forensic approaches are employed. This model can work in a realistic environment as series of suspicious activities may lead 
to an incident and before which the evidence collection 
can be completed. Since, SNAPS is deployed at hypervisor, 
intruder cannot alter/stop the monitoring capabilities of it. 
• Reduces the analysis time: Analyzing a single snapshot 
involves human effort. If investigator wants to analyze 
multiple snapshots then the situation becomes more 
complex. With the aid of recommendation engine, SNAPS 
86 (2019) 92-111 105 
Fig. 12 - Building the provenance system from multiple snapshots acquired from the cloud. 
Fig. 13 -P r o t o c o l followed by the investigator after acquiring the snapshots.

Fig. 14 - Recommendation about whether a queried object is copied or not. 
gives the high level clues about the queried object. This 
saves a lot of time and effort for the investigator. 
• Data Independent Persistence: When an object is deleted, 
its provenance data should not be deleted. In our case, each 
object provenance chain is stored in the elastic search engine and will not be removed even after the same object is 
deleted from the VM. 
• Query Capabilities: The implemented query engine will accept multiple conditions and can list all the objects which 
satisfy the query along with the possible recommendations. 
• Extensible: Can be applied to any environment with the input as snapshots. 
• Scalable: The query results are faster even for huge size 
snapshots as each evidence is configured to go through the 
indexing module of the elastic search engine. 
• Available: With the aid of iCFR model, the snapshots are 
always made available to the investigator. 
7. Forensic applications of the proposed 
provenance system 
SNAPS can reduce the time spent by the investigator during 
snapshot analysis. It is designed to solve many interesting 
forensic problems. Some of them are presented below: 
7.1. Application-1: Giving a recommendation about 
whether a queried object is copied or not 
Forensically, this recommendation is used to identify digital 
forgery. It is very important in forensics from where a particular object is originated i.e. whether it is created or copied 
from some other location. We framed rules for each of these 
operations and configured them into elastic search engine 
( Algorithm 2 ). After applying them to the acquired cloud VM 
snapshots, the recommendations are automatically generated 
as shown in Fig. 14 . For quick interpretation of the same, our 
provenance system also provides a visualization capability as 
shown in Fig. 15 . 
Digital forgery is a common operation done by the intruders. There are various types of digital forgery like splicing, 
retouching, copy-move attacks ( Amerini and Irene, 2011 ). We Algorithm 2 Recommendation about whether a queried object is copied or not. 
1: Input: Query( O i ) ⊿ O i is forensically significant object for 
the investigator 
2: if O i ∈ S i then ⊿ Say, O i is present in location Loc x and S i is 
the first snapshot captured

4: if O i ∈ S i +1 then

6: if S i _ hash (O i ) == S i +1 _ hash (O i ) then 
7: reco( O i ) = "File may be copied from Loc x "
8: end if 
9: end if 
10: end if 
11: end for 
12: end if 
also identified and created rule sets for detecting each of 
these. For example, in copy-move attack , the object is moved 
to some other location and it is then modified keeping the 
base properties intact. Algorithm 3 detects the objects with

is undergone with copy-move attack or not.

6: reco( O i ) = "Object O j is a victim of Copy-Move

9: end for

copy-move attack. The Sim( O i , O j ) function uses document 
similarity techniques like cosine, Term Frequency- Inverse 
Document Frequency (TF-IDF) etc. 
86 (2019) 92-111 107 
Fig. 15 -A visual representation of the queried object copy history. 
7.2. Application-2: Identifying the backdoors created 
A backdoor can provide unauthorized access to a remote system. Any intruder who created a backdoor wants its life to 
stay for a long time. There are many ways to achieve this and 
the most common way is performing a waterhole attack ( Sarala, 
2014 ). It is a malicious program which can poison the system by injecting a backdoor at most frequently used locations. So, 
the backdoor will be reinitialized each time the victim had 
interactions with that common location/files. 
A set of rules were devised to detect backdoors. Some of 
the important ones are shown in Algorithm 4 . We also framed 
a waterhole condition i.e. whether queried object O i is commonly used by the victim or not. Moreover, we apply all the 
Algorithm 4 Recommendation about whether a queried object is used as backdoor. 
1: Input: Query( O i ) ⊿ O i is forensically significant object for 
the investigator 
2: if O i ∈ S i then ⊿ S i is the first snapshot captured 
3: Store_ O i = sym_link ( O i ) 
4: end if 
5: for each Snapshot S i +1 do 
6: if O i ∈ S i +1 then 
7: if sym_link( O i != NULL) then 
8: Store_ O /prime 
i = sym_link( O i ) 
9: if Store_ O i != Store_ O /prime 
i then 
10: reco( O i ) = "May be to O i , backdoor was linked"
11: reco /prime (O i ) = "The backdoor name is O j "
12: end if 
13: end if 
14: end if 
15: end for 
relevant rules to increase the correctness of recommendation. 
For example, in Fig. 16 , we identified that abc.txt was a symbolic link attached to abc.jpeg. 
We initially identified from victim network logs that 
abc.txt was injected from external VM. Then, after applying 
Algorithm 6 ( Section 7.4 ), we came to know that abc.txt was 
obfuscated and its actual extension is .so and contains malicious code which was attached as symbolic link to abc.jpeg. So 
whenever VM restarts, the backdoor life may get expired. But 
it gets reactivated each time when the victim interacts with 
the abc.jpeg file (frequently used object in victim VM and it is 
known from its history of of modification times). This makes 
the intruder to always have a remote access of the victim machine. It is important to note that, the rules of the backdoor 
detection may vary depending on the type of backdoor. 
7.3. Application-3: Identifying suspicious activities 
Once a backdoor was created, the intruder may alter/delete 
the data or metadata of an object in the victim VM which 
he/she is not supposed to do and can be treated as suspicious. For example, one of the common attacks is, Host File 
Redirect ( Grimes ) i.e. in Windows, /Etc/Hosts file resolves the 
name-to-IP lookup as always contacting a DNS server for the 
same name resolution is a time consuming task. The intruder 
may add a malicious server IP to the commonly visited domain name of the user for which he initially has to change 
the permissions of the file. 
Algorithm 5 takes any suspicious permission change as 
input and gives all the objects associated with that activity. 
A similar detection was observed in the acquired snapshots 
and the same is shown in Fig. 17 . When the input changes, 
the set of conditions we check will also change. 
7.4. Application-4: Detecting the obfuscated files 
Obfuscation is a process used to confuse, disorient and divert 
the forensic investigator ( Battistoni et al., 2016 ). One of the 
common obfuscation techniques is to change the extensions Algorithm 5 Detects the suspicious activities on system files. 
1: Input: Query(reco) ⊿ suspicious permission change 
2: if O i ∈ S i then ⊿ S i is the first snapshot captured 
3: for each Snapshot S i +1 do 
4: if O i ∈ S i then 
5: if modified_time( O i (S i )) != modified_time( O i (S i +1 )) 
then 
6: if permissions( O i (S i )) != permissions ( O i (S i +1 )) 
then 
7: reco( O i ) = "Unauthorized operation, may be 
suspicious"
8: end if 
9: end if 
10: end if 
11: end for 
12: end if 
Algorithm 6 Detecting the obfuscated objects from the acquired snapshots. 
1: Input: Query( O i ) ⊿ O i is forensically significant object 
2: external_ext = O i .ext; 
3: for each Snapshot S i do ⊿ i is max. no. of snapshots 
4: if O i ∈ S i then 
5: internal_ext = carve( O i .ext); 
6: if external_ext != internal_ext then 
7: reco( O i ) = "File O i may be obfuscated"; 
8: current = i; 
9: break; 
10: end if 
11: end if 
12: end for 
13: for each object O x in S current -1 do 
14: if hash (O i ) == hash (O x ) then 
15: if inode (O i ) == inode (O x ) then 
16: reco( O i ) = "O i may be obfuscated"; 
17: reco( O i ) = "Obfuscated from O x "; 
18: break; 
19: end if 
20: else if sim (O i , O x ) > Thresh max then 
21: reco( O i ) = "O i may be obfuscated"; 
22: reco( O i ) = "Obfuscated from O x "; 
23: break; 
24: else

26: end if 
27: reco( O i ) = "O i is newly created and obfuscated in S i and 
its source may not exist in previous snapshot"
28: end for 
of the objects. There are many ransomwares in the market today which can change the file extensions intelligently. They 
use unicode characters like (U+202e) which can override the 
file name from right to left and can force the file system to display the text in the reverse order. For example, a ransomware 
using this unicode character combination will make the file 
name BirthdayGift by [U+202e]3pm.scr to appear as BirthdayGift 
by RCS.mp3 . This activity may create curiosity in the target 
86 (2019) 92-111 109 
Fig. 16 - Backdoor is detected from the acquired VM snapshots of Openstack cloud. 
Fig. 17 - Change in access control policy was detected on the system files of the target VM. 
Fig. 18 - Recommendations about the obfuscated objects in multiple snapshots. 
user and makes him open the file which thereby may affect 
his/her system as it is actually an executable file (i.e..scr). 
In file extension based obfuscation, the external extension 
will not be equal to internal extension. Using carving techniques, we have read the first few bytes of the header to know 
a file internal extension. If both the external and internal extensions of an object are not equal then our recommendation 
engine in elastic search says that, the object has been obfuscated. Using the provenance information generated from 
multiple snapshots, the capabilities of the recommendation 
engine were extended to identify the source of the incident. In 
this case, there are two possible approaches: (1) Obfuscation is done on the existing object (2) A duplicate copy of the object 
may be created and then obfuscation may be performed on it. 
For both the cases, we identify the source object of the 
obfuscation and the same is shown in Algorithm 6 . When an 
obfuscated file is identified in a snapshot S i then to identify 
its source, we search in its previous snapshot ( S current -1 ). In 
the second case, the copied object may get a few modifications before it is actually obfuscated. SNAPS detect it using 
the similarity between the two objects and in this case, the 
similarity between the two objects ( O i and O x ) should be high. 
We configured the rules in the Algorithm 6 in to the elastic 
search. Then the acquired Openstack VM snapshots are given 
as input to the recommendation engine. Finally, it identified 
an obfuscated object in one of the snapshots and the same in 
shown in Fig. 18 . The source object of the obfuscation is also 
detected and given as recommendation. 
7.5. Future work 
The future directions to consider snapshots as an evidence for 
cloud incident handling are: 
• The recommendation engine devised for snapshot 
analysis can be integrated with more features. 
• The capabilities of the proposed comprehensive model can 
be extended to address other challenges of cloud incident 
handling. 
• Additional cloud incidents can be used to validate the 
proposed model. 
Even though multiple snapshots are taken in a short interval of time, they may not capture every change in the VM. 
This drawback may get complemented with several advantages that SNAPS provides i.e. it is the most reliable provenance system as it cannot be disabled/altered by the intruder, 
handles the data gravity and helps the investigator by giving 
possible recommendations. There will be cases where the existing provenance systems may not capture anything when 
they are disabled by the intruder but SNAPS can ensure the 
capture of multiple snapshots before the actual incident.

8. Conclusion 
VM Snapshots are the most crucial evidences for forensic investigation in cloud. But the challenge is, always the snapshots may not be available. We addressed this problem by using iCFR and validated it with a scenario. We also reduced 
the difficulties in the issue of data gravity. The acquired snapshots from the cloud are transferred to the investigators environment where we build a provenance system using those 
snapshots. The design of our provenance system is inspired 
from the spatio-temporal models. Finally, we identified several forensic applications of the proposed provenance system 
and a few have been presented with proper validation. 
Declaration of interests 
The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

us.

1-s2.0-S0167404818303948-main.pdf.title: A self-protecting agents based model for high-performance mobile-cloud computing
1-s2.0-S0167404818303948-main.pdf.abstract:
Mobile-cloud computing (MCC) allows devices with resource and battery limitations to 
achieve computation-intensive tasks in real-time. While this new paradigm of computing 
seems beneficial for real-time mobile computing, existing MCC models mainly rely on keeping full clones of program code at remote sites and unstandardized/uninteroperable environments, hampering wider adoption. Moreover, the security risks arising from offloading 
data and code to an untrusted platform and the computational overhead introduced by 
complex security mechanisms stand as deterrents for adoption of MCC at large. In this 
paper, we present a context-dependent computation-offloading model for MCC, which is 
based on application segments packed into autonomous agents. This approach only requires isolated execution containers in the cloud to provide a runtime environment for 
the agents, and minimal involvement of the mobile platform during the computation process. The agents in the proposed model are able to protect themselves from tampering 
using integrity-checkpointing and an authenticated encryption-based communication 
mechanism. Experiments with two mobile applications demonstrate the effectiveness of 
the approach for high-performance, secure MCC.
1-s2.0-S0167404818303948-main.pdf.intro_text:
1. Introduction 
Mobile computing devices have replaced desktops and mainframes for daily computing needs for the past decade. Despite 
the everyday advances in mobile computing technology, size 
restrictions impose limitations on the processing power and 
battery life of these devices, which limits their capabilities 
for real-time, computing-intensive applications such as image 
processing. Cloud computing offers the ability to fill the gap 
between the resource needs of mobile devices and availability 
of those resources, through the concept of mobile-cloud computing (MCC), which partitions mobile applications between 
E-mail addresses: pangin@ceng.metu.edu.tr (P. Angin), bb@cs.purdue.edu (B. Bhargava), ranchal@us.ibm.com (R. Ranchal). mobile and cloud platforms for execution, by dynamically offloading parts of mobile computation to cloud hosts. 
Achieving high performance with mobile-cloud computing requires optimal partitioning of the mobile application 
components between the mobile and cloud platforms based 
on dynamic runtime conditions. Recent work on this problem has resulted in frameworks with various partitioning and 
optimization techniques. However, most of these frameworks 
impose strict requirements on the cloud side, such as a full 
clone of the application code or special application management software, hindering wide applicability in public clouds. 
The other major obstacle for wider adoption of MCC is the 
security risks associated with sending sensitive data and code 
77 (2018) 380-396 381 
to an untrusted platform, including but not limited to the following: 
• Lack of control on resources and multi-tenancy of different 
users' applications on the same physical machine make 
cloud platforms vulnerable to attacks ( Feng et al., 2011 ). 
• In addition to privacy issues, programs running in the 
cloud are prone to tampering with code, data, execution 
flow and communication, as well as masquerading attacks ( Chou, 2013 ). 
• Mobile code can navigate through multiple platforms before returning to the origin, giving rise to the end-to-end 
security problem, which involves decreasing control with 
every further hop in the chain of platforms ( Ali et al., 2015 ). 
In order to provide complete security, the application 
should ensure all communication with/execution on the cloud 
platforms are trusted. Achieving ultimate flexibility and performance in mobile-cloud computing is contingent upon 
the availability of a secure computing framework capable of 
dynamic decision making with regards to the execution location of different program partitions. The performance requirements of real-time MCC call for a generalized computation 
offloading model that requires minimal involvement of the 
mobile platform for monitoring of offloaded computation, 
where all dynamic decision-making processes and integrity 
checks are achieved with lightweight components. The main 
challenges for achieving dynamic tamper resistance in MCC 
are the following: 
• The tamper detection mechanism should involve minimal 
modification to the software structure, and be transparent 
to the programmer. 
• Appropriate measures should be taken in case of tamper 
detection in a way that is transparent to the software. 
• The runtime performance overhead of tamper detection 
and response should be minimal. 
• Communication costs for detection and reporting of tamper should be minimal. 
• The size of the program code/data should not be increased 
significantly due to the protection mechanism. 
In this paper we present a mobile-cloud computation 
model based on autonomous agent-based application modules, which are augmented with performance checkpoints for 
self-performance evaluation and integrity verification units 
for dynamic detection and reporting of code tampering. The 
main contributions of this paper are as follows: 
• We present an effective and easy-to-adopt dynamic computation offloading model for high-performance MCC. 
• We present a dynamic performance estimation model that 
allows for tracking and relocation of offloaded computation to ensure optimal performance under varying mobilecloud context. 
• We propose a tamper resistance model for MCC that allows 
for dynamic integrity-checking and migration of offloaded 
computation to ensure trustworthiness of results received 
from a cloud platform. • The proposed computation offloading and protection 
model relies on lightweight, transparent mechanisms for 
application code transformation/augmentation, allowing 
for low response time overhead and high interoperability. 
The rest of this paper is organized as follows: Section 2 describes an autonomous agents-based computation-offloading 
model, namely AAMCC , for high-performance mobile-cloud 
computing. Section 3 presents experiment results for the application of the proposed model on two real-world mobile applications. Section 4 presents the proposed dynamic performance profiling model for allowing AAMCC agents to monitor their own performance during runtime and Section 5 provides the related experiment results. Section 6 provides the 
details of the proposed dynamic tamper resistance model for 
protecting the autonomous agents of AAMCC from malicious 
activity in the cloud. Section 7 provides a security analysis 
of the proposed tamper resistance model. Section 8 presents 
the results of experiments performed to evaluate the overhead of the proposed tamper resistance model. Section 9 reviews related work in high-performance MCC and MCC security. Section 10 concludes the paper.
1-s2.0-S0167404818303948-main.pdf.conclusion_text:
10. Conclusion 
In this paper we proposed a mobile-cloud computation 
model based on autonomous agent-based application modules, which only requires the presence of isolated execution 
containers for the offloaded agents in the cloud, as opposed 
to other approaches requiring replication of full application 
code. We presented the design of a prototype of the proposed 
model with Android devices and cloud hosts on Amazon EC2 
and showed through experiments that autonomous agentbased computation offloading is an effective tool for MCC, 
providing application execution times significantly lower than 
on-device execution. We discussed major security issues with 
offloading code and data to a public cloud and proposed a 
tamper-resistance model for MCC based on self-protecting 
agents, using various integrity checkpoints of code introspection to detect and report tamper. The proposed model mitigates the issues of end-to-end security, cloud multitenancy 
vulnerabilities and tampering attacks in MCC by making offloaded code self-aware both with the use of self-integrity and 
self-performance checkpoints. The integration of these checkpoints is performed in a transparent manner from the software development perspective, through utilization of aspectoriented programming. The employment of mobile agents in the model enables automated migration of tasks between 
clouds, decreasing the burden on the mobile platform. The 
agents are autonomous in the sense that they monitor their 
own integrity and performance, according to which they make 
migration decisions without the need for management by 
their originating mobile platform. 
We also discussed details of a state-based programming 
model for MCC, where the agent can continue execution from 
the last integrity-preserving checkpoint after migration to a 
different platform, instead of starting execution from the beginning. The proposed tamper-resistance model was shown 
to incur very low runtime overhead and successfully detect 
load-time and runtime code tampering attacks. The mobilecloud computation model presented in this paper is promising 
to meet high-performance computing and integrity assurance 
needs in real-time mobile-cloud computing.

• In addition to privacy issues, programs running in the 
cloud are prone to tampering with code, data, execution 
flow and communication, as well as masquerading attacks ( Chou, 2013 ). 
• Mobile code can navigate through multiple platforms before returning to the origin, giving rise to the end-to-end 
security problem, which involves decreasing control with 
every further hop in the chain of platforms ( Ali et al., 2015 ). 
In order to provide complete security, the application 
should ensure all communication with/execution on the cloud 
platforms are trusted. Achieving ultimate flexibility and performance in mobile-cloud computing is contingent upon 
the availability of a secure computing framework capable of 
dynamic decision making with regards to the execution location of different program partitions. The performance requirements of real-time MCC call for a generalized computation 
offloading model that requires minimal involvement of the 
mobile platform for monitoring of offloaded computation, 
where all dynamic decision-making processes and integrity 
checks are achieved with lightweight components. The main 
challenges for achieving dynamic tamper resistance in MCC 
are the following: 
• The tamper detection mechanism should involve minimal 
modification to the software structure, and be transparent 
to the programmer. 
• Appropriate measures should be taken in case of tamper 
detection in a way that is transparent to the software. 
• The runtime performance overhead of tamper detection 
and response should be minimal. 
• Communication costs for detection and reporting of tamper should be minimal. 
• The size of the program code/data should not be increased 
significantly due to the protection mechanism. 
In this paper we present a mobile-cloud computation 
model based on autonomous agent-based application modules, which are augmented with performance checkpoints for 
self-performance evaluation and integrity verification units 
for dynamic detection and reporting of code tampering. The 
main contributions of this paper are as follows: 
• We present an effective and easy-to-adopt dynamic computation offloading model for high-performance MCC. 
• We present a dynamic performance estimation model that 
allows for tracking and relocation of offloaded computation to ensure optimal performance under varying mobilecloud context. 
• We propose a tamper resistance model for MCC that allows 
for dynamic integrity-checking and migration of offloaded 
computation to ensure trustworthiness of results received 
from a cloud platform. • The proposed computation offloading and protection 
model relies on lightweight, transparent mechanisms for 
application code transformation/augmentation, allowing 
for low response time overhead and high interoperability. 
The rest of this paper is organized as follows: Section 2 describes an autonomous agents-based computation-offloading 
model, namely AAMCC , for high-performance mobile-cloud 
computing. Section 3 presents experiment results for the application of the proposed model on two real-world mobile applications. Section 4 presents the proposed dynamic performance profiling model for allowing AAMCC agents to monitor their own performance during runtime and Section 5 provides the related experiment results. Section 6 provides the 
details of the proposed dynamic tamper resistance model for 
protecting the autonomous agents of AAMCC from malicious 
activity in the cloud. Section 7 provides a security analysis 
of the proposed tamper resistance model. Section 8 presents 
the results of experiments performed to evaluate the overhead of the proposed tamper resistance model. Section 9 reviews related work in high-performance MCC and MCC security. Section 10 concludes the paper.

2. Autonomous agents-based mobile-cloud 
computing (AAMCC) 
In this section we present the details of an autonomous 
agents-based model for performance optimization in MCC. 
2.1. Agent-based computation model 
A mobile (autonomous) agent is a software program with mobility, 
which can be sent out from a computer into a network and roam 
among the computer nodes in the network ( Chess et al., 1997 ). It 
can be executed on those computers to finish its task on behalf of its owner. Note that hereafter, we will be using the 
phrases mobile agent and autonomous agent interchangeably, as 
the agents in our proposed framework are both mobile and 
autonomous. 
Fig. 1 shows a high level view of the agent-based MCC architecture, and Fig. 2 shows the model in action for a mobile 
application consisting of three modules. Each module of a mobile application in AAMCC is either an agent-based application module that is offloadable to the cloud ( P 2 and P 3 in Fig. 2 ), 
or a native application component. 
When a mobile application is launched, the execution manager contacts the cloud directory service to get a list of possible cloud hosts for offloadable application modules. After this 
step, an execution plan containing offloading decisions for the 
agent-based modules is created by the execution manager. If 
the execution plan requires offloading a particular application 
module, a bridge is formed between the caller of that module 
and the cloud host selected by the execution manager, through 
which the offloaded module migrates to the container in the 
host, carrying along its input parameters. Upon migration, the 
module starts executing and communicates its output data 
to the caller through the same bridge. In the case that the 
communication between the mobile platform and the cloud 
Fig. 1 - High level view of AAMCC architecture. 
Fig. 2 - AAMCC in action for sample mobile application. 
platform where the offloaded agent resides is disrupted before the mobile platform receives the computation result, the 
agent remains on the cloud platform until the mobile platform 
reconnects to request the result. The ability to reconnect to 
the agent provides high reliability for the model, obviating the 
need to execute the offloaded task again, to avoid performance 
penalties. In some cases it is possible that the connection to 
the cloud platform will be permanently disrupted due to platform failures etc. In order to avoid indefinite stalling in such 
a case, the execution manager enforces a timeout for remote 
partition execution, upon expiration of which a new agent is 
launched for the same task. 
2.2. Model components 
2.2.1. Autonomous application modules 
An autonomous application module is a chunk of application 
code packed in an autonomous (mobile) agent that is executable on a cloud host. Autonomy of these application modules provides great advantages in the context of MCC due to 
the capability of transparently moving between mobile and 
cloud platforms without requiring management by their caller and self-cloning to different virtual machines. Each mobile application in the framework consists of a set of autonomous 
agent-based application modules that are offloadable to the 
cloud for execution, in addition to a set of native application 
components that are always executed on the device due to 
constraints such as accessing native sensors of the device or 
providing the user interface of the application. Partitioning of 
the application into these two types of components is performed statically before installation of the application on the 
mobile device as described in Angin and Bhargava (2013) . During the offline application partitioning process, any program 
partition that is not computation-intensive is not set as an offloadable component even if it does not have to be pinned to 
the device, as this would incur additional runtime processing 
overhead for partitions that would likely never be offloaded. In 
the prototype framework, these modules are implemented as 
JADE ( Telecom Italia Lab ) agents and they exhibit all features 
of a mobile agent ( Chess et al., 1997 ) including stateful execution, asynchronous communication, autonomic decisionmaking and migration capability. 
2.2.2. Cloud directory service 
The cloud directory service is a web service that maintains an 
up-to-date database of agent containers available for use in 
the cloud. Along with the public IP addresses of the machine 
instances and the agent container identifiers, this database 
also keeps a record of machine instance architecture information and the number of currently active containers on each instance. This service is also responsible for the instantiation of 
new agent containers in case of need (invocation) by a mobile 
application and performance requirement-based allocation of 
cloud resources to different agents. The cloud directory service also maintains the service-level agreement (SLA) for each 
of the available instances. The execution manager on the device utilizes this information when making decisions regarding which cloud container to offload the modules to. 
2.2.3. Cloud hosts 
Cloud hosts (virtual machine instances) in the model provide platform as a service (PaaS) for agent-based application 
77 (2018) 380-396 383 
Fig. 3 - Execution tree structure. 
partitions. A container-based service model is employed, 
where each container provides an isolated runtime environment for an offloaded application module to execute in. In 
the prototype framework, this component corresponds to 
JADE ( Telecom Italia Lab ) agent containers running on Amazon EC2 virtual machine instances ( Amazon Web Services 
Inc.). Once an offloaded agent reaches the container selected 
by the execution manager as a result of solving the optimization problem described below and consulting the cloud directory service for the list of available agent containers, it starts 
(or if this is a relocation from another host, it resumes) execution of the application module contained in its code section. 
In addition to the regular containers for hosting the offloaded 
agents, each cloud host includes a main container, which has 
an agent management system controlling access to the platform and authenticating residing agents as well as controlling registrations, and a directory facilitator providing a yellow 
pages service to the agent platform. All containers on the host 
are registered with this main container for reachability from 
outside hosts. Each agent container is a multi-threaded execution environment composed of one thread for every agent 
plus system threads spawned by remote method invocation 
(RMI) runtime system for message dispatching. Each agent 
container is an RMI server object that locally manages a set of 
agents. It controls the life cycle of agents by creating, suspending, resuming and killing them. Besides, it deals with all the 
communication aspects by dispatching incoming messages 
and routing them according to the destination field. Upon 
completion of the execution of a specific agent, the agent is 
killed after communicating its output to the mobile device 
through the bridge established upon migration, and the container becomes available for hosting other agents. 
2.2.4. Execution manager 
One of the most important features affecting the performance 
of an MCC framework is its ability to adapt to changing network conditions when making offloading decisions. The offloading (execution) manager is a service running on the mobile device, which is responsible for dynamically determining 
whether a specific application module should be migrated to a 
cloud host to minimize total execution time, given the execution tree of the application. An execution tree, the structure of 
which is seen in Fig. 3 , is a representation of the interactions 
between the modules of an application. The node at the top of 
the tree is the application entry node (i.e. the main module). If 
a node is the direct child of another node in the execution tree, the module represented by the parent node makes a direct invocation to the module represented by the child node. In the 
case of cyclic dependencies between modules (which is not a 
good programming practice), the modules can be replicated in 
the execution tree, which will increase the depth of the tree, 
but will not require additional modifications to the solution 
of the optimization problem. Also, any application module invoked by multiple different modules is represented as a different node for each dependency (i.e. replicated), to avoid increased complexity of the offloading optimization problem. 
Each node p in the execution tree is annotated with a number m p . m p is the time to execute the module (partition) p on 
the mobile device less the time to execute its submodules. 
Each edge in the tree is annotated with a number c p , which 
is the total size of the data that flows into module p from its 
parent module and back for the invocation of p . Note that although the structure of the execution tree would not change 
between different executions, the edge annotations are determined at application runtime, as the execution time and size 
of data to be transferred between modules could be functions 
of the problem size. Note that this representation does not include numbers for execution time of a module in the cloud. 
Because the execution plan only includes offloadable modules, execution time in the cloud is considered negligible compared to that on the device. The limitations of this assumption 
can be overcome by augmenting agents with self-performance 
evaluation capability using a technique similar to the tamperresistance mechanism described in Section 6 . 
The offloading manager uses the following cost model to 
make offloading decisions for each offloadable application 
partition (A submodule s of p is a module invoked by p ): 
td p : on-device execution cost (time) of partition p 
to p : on-cloud execution cost (time) of partition p 
b : the network bandwidth available to the application 
p s : the set of submodules of p . 
Then td p and to p are calculated as follows: 
t d p = m p + /summationdisplay 
i ∈ p s min (t d i , t o i ) (1) 
to p = c p /b (2) 
In order to determine m p , we currently use a static application profiler measuring the execution time of each application 
partition and record the results as metadata of the application. 
As seen in Eq. (1) , the optimization problem has a recursive 
formulation: To determine the execution cost of an application module, we first need to determine the execution costs of 
its submodules. However, the offloading decisions regarding 
the submodules of a module could become invalid once the 
costs of executing the module on the device and in the cloud 
are calculated, as offloading the module implies offloading its 
submodules too. We solve the optimization problem with a 
depth-first traversal of the execution tree, updating the local 
and offloaded execution cost of each module once all of its 
submodule execution costs are set. If the data transfer cost 
for a module exceeds its local execution cost, the module is 
kept on the device, otherwise it is offloaded. Once the whole 
execution tree is traversed using this algorithm, all children 
of a node chosen to be offloaded are offloaded with it as well 
(even if the first decision for them during the traversal was to 
keep them local). Algorithm 1 shows how to solve the execution time optimization problem.

if childNode.m > childNode.o then 
child N od e.d ecision ← of f load; 
child N od e.cost ← child N od e.o; 
else 
child N od e.d ecision ← l ocal ; 
child N od e.cost ← child N od e.m ; 
end 
parentN od e.m ← parentN od e.m + child N od e.cost;

if parentNode.m > parentNode.o then 
parentN od e.d ecision ← of f load;

end 
else

end 
end 
3. Performance of agent-based offloading 
The performance of agent-based computation-offloading was 
evaluated with two mobile applications described below. 
3.1. Experiments with face recognition 
The first set of experiments was performed with a face recognition application based on the program at Darnok (2018) , 
which given the picture of a person, identifies the most similar 
face to it in a set of pictures. This application assumes that the 
set of pictures to compare against is only locally available on 
the mobile device, i.e. if the application component processing 
the pictures is offloaded, the data for each picture is sent with 
it too. The experiments were run using an Android emulator 1

3.2. Experiments with NQueens puzzle 
The second set of experiments were performed with the 
NQueens puzzle, which is the problem of placing n chess 
queens on an n x n chessboard such that no two queens can 
attack each other, i.e. no two queens share the same row, 
column or diagonal. In this set of experiments, we used an 
NQueens puzzle application, which finds all possible solutions to the puzzle for different number of queens using a 
recursive backtracking algorithm. The experiments were performed using a Motorola Atrix 4G ( GSM Arena. Motorola Atrix, 
2018 ), where the cloud host was a medium VMI in EC2. Each 
experiment was repeated 10 times and the average execution times for device-only execution, offloaded execution and 
the absolute time difference percentage (i.e. (device only - offloaded)/device only) for the two execution approaches are 
reported in Table 1 . While execution times in seconds are 
close to each other for up to 12 queens, offloaded execution 
achieves significantly better performance than on-device execution for more than 13 queens and it outperforms on-device 
execution by about 15 times when the number of queens 
is 15.

4. Dynamic performance profiling 
The ability to make a close estimation of the makespan (the 
time taken to run a program segment) of an application module is important for an MCC model to be effective. While most of the previously proposed makespan estimation models for 
MCC are based on the assumption that the execution time 
of an application module offloaded to the cloud will be negligible, this assumption may not always hold due to the dynamic nature of cloud platforms and the lack of information 
about the platform characteristics in some cases. In previous 
work ( Angin et al., 2015 ), we showed that the characteristics of 
cloud hosts can have significant effects on the performance of 
mobile applications. Additionally, because cloud resources are 
shared between multiple users, the existence of other users on 
the same platform can significantly affect the makespan of an 
application in the cloud as well. 
Estimation of the makespan of an application module 
serves the following purposes in the context of MCC: 
• The estimated makespan can be compared to an expected 
value to see whether the execution is likely to satisfy specific time constraints. In the case of a low-performance 
platform, the computation can be migrated to a different 
platform. 
• In the case of continuous or periodic makespan estimation 
updates, fluctuations in the estimates can provide an idea 
about the sharing of resources on the platform. 
4.1. Makespan estimation model 
Software execution profiling is an active area of research, as 
runtime performance of applications is of utmost importance 
for many systems. Although profiling the execution of an application under stable conditions on a specific architecture 
can yield accurate estimations for the makespan of the application under the exact same conditions, getting an accurate estimation under highly dynamic conditions and for platforms with different architectures is much more difficult. One 
of the main approaches used for makespan estimation is lowlevel analysis of a program to identify all instructions in the 
program and calculate the number of CPU cycles the program 
would take to execute, which then translates into a specific 
total execution time based on the architecture of the platform ( Bate et al., 2000 ). However, this approach is too specific 
and requires knowledge of the exact architecture of the target 
platform. It also does not account for the dynamicity of the 
runtime environment. In order to achieve accurate results, a 
makespan estimation model should continuously update its 
estimates based on changing runtime conditions, and not rely 
on a specific architecture. Fitness for MCC also requires that 
the model not be too complex for real-time requirements. 
The model we propose for makespan estimation of an application module is a simple, platform-independent model 
based on heuristics affecting makespan. The main idea is that 
the relative makespans of different execution blocks in an 
application should be fixed under stable runtime conditions. 
This means that, if it takes x seconds to execute a code block 
b 1 on a machine, and 2 x seconds to execute block b 2 on the 
same machine, then b 2 will take 2 y seconds to execute on another machine where b 1 takes y seconds. Exceptions are: 
• Code blocks involving network activity, since their 
makespan is highly dependent on network conditions. 
• Code blocks involving conditionals with branches of significantly different computational complexity. 
• Code blocks accepting input parameters of significantly 
different sizes at runtime (at different runs), where the input size determines computational complexity. 
The makespan estimation model works as follows: 
1. The list of exceptions above forms the list of heuristics we 
use in the model, i.e. when selecting the code blocks whose 
makespans are to be integrated into the estimation model, 
we eliminate such blocks. 
2. For the remaining code blocks, we run the application multiple times on the same platform and record the average 
makespan for each block. 
3. Performance checkpoints corresponding to the selected 
code blocks are inserted into the application. 
4. During application execution, each performance checkpoint records the actual makespan of the corresponding 
code block at runtime. It also makes an estimation of 
the makespan of the next code block with a performance 
checkpoint, based on the relative makespans of the two 
code blocks. 
For an application with n performance checkpoints, the 
makespan for code block number x is estimated as 
T est (x ) = T true (x -1) ×T avg (x ) 
T avg (x -1) (3) 
In Eq. (3) : 
T est : Estimated makespan for a code block 
T avg : Average makespan for a code block (from statistics) 
T true : Actual runtime makespan for a code block 
The estimated total makespan for the remaining code 
blocks after x is calculated using Eq. (4) : 
T remaining (x ) = n /summationdisplay 
i = x +1 T true (x ) ×T avg (i ) 
T avg (x ) (4) 
4.2. Performance checkpoints integration 
In order to be used in the proposed framework, the makespan 
estimation model needs to be integrated into the autonomous 
application modules, so that they can monitor their own performance during execution on a cloud platform and take 
appropriate actions based on the estimated remaining execution time. The integration is performed using aspectoriented programming (AOP), which provides complete transparency to the underlying application code. In the case of 
self-performance evaluating agents, an updated estimate of 
the remaining makespan is calculated at each performance 
checkpoint during agent execution. The agent uses this information to clone itself to a different platform if the estimate of 
the remaining makespan is higher than an expected threshold /epsilon1. Note that the agent code is transformed into a stateful 
program to use this cloning approach, so that the time spent 
on the already executed code blocks is not wasted. States correspond to the performance checkpoints. Table 3 -M a k e s p a n estimation errors for different VMIs.
