4.3. Cloud host relocation 
Allocation of agent containers by the cloud directory service is 
a significant component for the performance of the proposed 
MCC model, as it not only determines the first host an application module is assigned to, but also the hosts it might relocate to upon facing lower than expected performance. An optimal allocation scheme will also ensure cloud resources are 
used at full potential and costs are kept at minimum. In order to achieve optimal placement, the cloud directory service 
utilizes performance checkpointing data from autonomous 
agents. The following rules are used when assigning agents 
to cloud hosts: 
• If an agent relocates, it is always assigned to a host of 
higher computation power or fewer active containers. 
• If an agent is within a specified percentage of its threshold 
makespan, no new agents are assigned to its host until it 
terminates execution or leaves the host. 
• Container assignments start with small instances. 
The SLA information maintained by the cloud directory 
service is utilized for the optimal selection of the hosts during 
relocation in order to ensure that the performance will not fall 
below certain thresholds upon migration.2 
5. Performance profiling experiments 
We evaluated the performance of the makespan estimation 
model with the face recognition application running on different machine instances in EC2. 9 performance checkpoints 
were inserted into the application, which correspond to the 
major method calls. Each experiment was repeated 100 times 
and we report the average numbers over those runs. Table 3 
summarizes the average of the absolute errors and the ratio 
of the average absolute error to the average true makespans. 
As seen in the table, the average absolute error is less than 
or equal to 292 ms, which translates into less than 2% of the 
total makespan for all instance types, which is negligible for 
most computation tasks. We also observe that the error increases slightly on instances with smaller computing power.

6. Self-protecting agents model for tamper 
resistance 
In this section we present our proposed tamper resistance 
model for AAMCC. We start with a brief background on preliminary concepts related to the theory and implementation 
of the proposed model. 
6.1. Preliminaries 
6.1.1. Tamper resistance 
Tamperproofing a program is ensuring that it executes as intended , even when there is an adversary who tries to disrupt, 
monitor or change the execution. An attacker typically modifies a program with the intent to force it to execute in a way 
to serve malicious purposes. The attacker can achieve this in 
one of the following ways ( Collberg and Nagra, 2009 ): 
• Removing code from and/or inserting new code into the 
executable file before execution 
• Removing code from and/or inserting new code into the 
program during execution 
• Altering the runtime behavior of the program using externals agents such as emulators, debuggers, or a hostile operating system 
Cloud computing platforms are especially vulnerable to 
tampering due to the multi-tenancy of applications from different parties on the same physical machine. In a public cloud, 
it is hard to control the intentions and actions of the different users of a platform. A security case study of the Amazon EC2 
by Ristenpart et al. (2009) has revealed the vulnerabilities introduced by virtualization, which is the main technology most 
cloud computing providers rely on to provide shared access to 
the same physical infrastructure. According to the study, it is 
possible for an attacker to identify where a particular virtual 
machine is likely to reside and launch virtual machines on the 
same physical machine as the target. Tamperproofing of code 
executing in the cloud is especially important to mitigate the 
effects of such targeted attacks. 
Remote tamper resistance is a special case of tamper resistance, where the program to be protected runs on an untrusted platform, but communicates with a master program 
on the trusted platform, which detects or is made aware of 
any tampering with the program running on the untrusted 
platform ( Collberg and Nagra, 2009 ). This kind of tamper resistance is especially necessary in the context of MCC, where 
the mobile platform needs to ensure the integrity of offloaded 
computation before integrating results/data received from the 
cloud platform. 
One of the main techniques used for remote tamper resistance is the use of introspection for the program running 
on the untrusted platform and reporting the hash value of 
the code to the trusted platform, which can make sure that it 
matches a pre-computed value. Introspection is the examination of a program or code segment to check that its integrity 
is preserved. The main method used for code introspection 
is the augmentation of a program with code that computes 
a hash value (or a checksum) over a code region and compares it to an expected value ( Collberg and Nagra, 2009 ). Attacks against tamper-resistant software using introspection 
generally use pattern-matching methods to identify locations 
in the program that include suspicious code (such as conditionals checking the value of a hash). 
6.1.2. Authenticated encryption 
Authenticated encryption (AE) is a shared-key based transform 
whose goal is to provide both privacy and authenticity of the 
encapsulated data ( Bellare and Namprempre, 2008; Katz and 
Yung, 2001 ). The encryption process E in an authenticated encryption scheme takes a plaintext p and a shared secret key k 
and returns a ciphertext c , whereas the decryption process D 
takes the ciphertext c and the same key k and outputs either 
the plaintext p or a special symbol ⊥ . i.e. 
E(k, p) → c (5) 
D (k, c ) → p ∪ ⊥ 
An output of ⊥ by D means that the received ciphertext c 
was invalid or unauthentic. Using AE for a message sent by 
Alice to Bob, encrypted with their shared key k has two implications: 
1. An active adversary (man-in-the-middle) cannot create a 
ciphertext which would make Bob believe that it was encrypted by Alice, i.e. if D ( k, c ) /negationslash = ⊥ ⇒ c is from someone who 
knows the shared key k . 
2. The message is protected against chosen ciphertext attacks, i.e. the privacy of the message is preserved. 
6.2. Proposed tamper resistance model 
6.2.1. Tamper detection and reporting protocol 
The approach we propose for tamper-resistant execution of 
offloaded code in the cloud is based on augmenting the mobile 
agents sent to a cloud platform with self-protection capability, using aspect-oriented programming (AOP) based integrity 
checkpoints distributed throughout the agent code to ensure 
timely detection of tamper. Upon tamper detection, the agent 
stops execution, moves to a different platform and resumes 
execution from the last integrity-verified checkpoint. The selfprotection method relies on the following two main ideas: 
1. Tamper checking guards: As the agent code is executing in 
the cloud, the integrity of the code is checked using introspection by software guards placed at various integrity checkpoints (endings of consecutive code blocks). These guards 
are also capable of reporting tamper to the mobile platform. 
2. Guard tamper tracking code: Each time a software guard 
checks for tamper, its own hash value is saved in agent 
data. The agent accumulates the guard hash values in a 
variable, which forms the key to encrypt the result sent to 
the mobile platform when the computation is complete. 
The mobile platform is capable of computing the key value 
independently, therefore it can decrypt the message received to extract the result from the autonomous application module. 
Using an encryption key formed by the dynamic hash values of the software guard code in the authenticated encryption of the result has the following implication: For the correct 
encryption key to be formed, the guard hash value at each 
integrity checkpoint needs to be integrated into the key and 
the integrated value needs to match the true (original) hash 
value for that guard. If the result received from the mobile 
agent cannot be decrypted by the mobile platform, there are 
two possibilities: 
1. The message containing the result was modified in transit 
to the mobile platform. 
2. The message was not encrypted with the correct key on 
the cloud platform. 
In either of these conditions, the result cannot be integrated into the program on the mobile platform. 
In the proposed security approach, accumulation of guard 
hash values during agent code execution is a stealthy tamper 
resistance mechanism in the sense that it does not involve any 
checks (conditional expressions) that are prone to detection by 
pattern matching methods used by attackers. It is important 
to form the encryption key using all guard hash values, as we 
are interested in detecting any tamper during the execution of 
the agent. This incremental formation of the encryption key 
serves two purposes: 
1. The encryption key is not revealed until the end of program execution, which makes it harder for an attacker to 
dynamically swap the value of the corresponding variable 
with the original key value to hide tamper. 2. The mobile platform can ensure that all integrity verification blocks in the offloaded module were executed in the 
case of a correct key value, as the execution of guard hashing code is triggered by the execution of guard code blocks, 
which allows for checking the authenticity of the results 
As evident from the above description, the strength of the 
mechanism for detecting tampering with the software guards 
relies heavily on the strength of the encryption key. In order to 
create a strong key, allowing for the detection of tamper with 
high probability, the process of forming the key should satisfy 
the following conditions: 
• All guard hash values should be integrated into the key. 
• The individual guard hash values should be formed using 
a collision-resistant hash function. 
• The function forming the encryption key from the individual guard hash values should output uniformly distributed 
(pseudorandom) results in its output domain. 
• The function forming the encryption key should be able 
to operate incrementally, instead of requiring all input at 
once. 
Let H be a collision-resistant hash function used to calculate the guard hash values. The algorithm to compute the encryption key is provided in Algorithm 2 . 
Algorithm 2: Encryption key generation. 
input : guard C od e : guard bytecode array 
H: secure hash function 
F : guard hash accumulation function 
output: encryption key for autonomous application 
module result

key ← 0 ; 
most Recent Guard ← 0 ; 
while most Recent Guard < numBl ocks do 
hashOfGuard ← H( guard C od e [ most Recent Guard] ) ; 
key ← F( key, hashOfGuard ) ; 
most Recent Guard ← most Recent Guard + 1 ; 
end 
Another condition that needs to be satisfied for the tamper detection mechanism to operate correctly is the ability of 
the mobile platform to calculate the encryption key independently, as a symmetric-key encryption mechanism is used in 
the model. This means that integrity checkpoints should be 
placed in the code in such a way that their execution is independent of the control-flow of the program, i.e. the corresponding guard code is executed no matter which path the 
program takes based on the runtime conditions. Furthermore, 
care should be taken to use encryption parameters that differ 
from the previous run of the same code in the cloud, to prevent 
attacks where the encryption key and initialization vector (IV) 
could have been saved and reused by malicious parties. This 
issue is handled by random generation of an IV on the mobile platform before each agent offload. Additional security 
can be achieved by introducing a mechanism that randomcomputers & security 77 (2018) 380-396 389 
izes the values of guard hashes integrated into the encryption key through insertion of random bytes into the code with 
no effect on guard operation for different runs of the agent.

end Fig. 6 shows the UML activity diagram of the execution lifecycle of a mobile agent in the proposed tamper resistance 
framework. The main steps taken by the agent during execution are as follows: 
• Step 1: The running guard hash and agent state values for 
mobile agent MA are reset. 
• Step 2: MA is sent to a selected VMI for execution. 
• Step 3: The integrity of the code and data of the MA is 
checked before starting execution on the cloud platform. 
• Step 4: If the integrity of MA was preserved during the 
transfer, MA starts executing the first code block in the application module and goes onto Step 5. Otherwise, a new 
VMI V is selected by inquiring the cloud directory service , a 
new MA is sent to V , and execution restarts from Step 1. 
• Step 5: The software guard for the most recently executed 
code block checks the runtime integrity of the block. If no 
tamper was detected, the running guard hash value is updated with the hash of the guard code performing the most 
recent check and the agent state is updated to the last integrity checkpoint. Step 5 is repeated until there are no 
more code blocks to execute. If tamper was detected by the 
guard, a new VMI V is selected by inquiring the cloud directory service , MA sends a message to the mobile platform including the tamper report and its new VMI address, moves 
to V and resumes execution from the last tamper-free execution checkpoint. 
• Step 6: The result of the offloaded computation is encrypted with the guard hash value and sent to the mobile 
platform. 
Fig. 6 - Activity diagram for tamper resistance mechanism. 
• Step 7: If the mobile platform is able to successfully decrypt the result message received from MA, it integrates 
the result into the program. If the decryption of the result 
message fails on the mobile platform, the whole process of 
MA's execution is repeated starting with Step 1. 
6.2.2. Implementation details 
In the implementation of the proposed tamper resistance 
model, integrity checkpoints are inserted around every 
method call using the AspectJ AOP ( Påhlsson , 2002 ) framework. AOP in general allows for injection of code at three 
different points: (a) at run-time through a dynamic proxy (b) 
at load-time when the target classes are being loaded (this 
happens at byte-code level for Android) (c) at build-time by 
modifying the compiled classes before packaging. The code 
injection process is completely transparent to application 
development. Advices (the code injected into the class files, 
i.e. the guard code) do not require direct access to the source 
code of the class. It is possible to insert advices around every 
method call in every class using regular expressions to define 
the pointcuts (locations to insert the advices). 
The code snippet in listing 1 shows how to implement a 
guard using the AspectJ AOP framework for the methods of an 
application class file QueensBehaviour . Line 2 in the code declares a pointcut methodCalls , which is a regular expression to 
match during the execution of the program. In this case, the 
pointcut matches all invocations of all methods of the Queens- 
Behaviour class. Line 5 declares the code (advice) to be executed upon matching the declared pointcut. The around keyword specifies that the enclosed code has parts to be executed 
before (lines 8 -18 ) and after (lines 21 -28 ) the body of the matched methods, with the body of the method executing in 
between (as specified by the special method proceed ). We see 
that the whole process of adding integrity checking functionality is completely transparent to the actual application code. 
The hash value for each method is calculated on the bytecode for that method using the SHA-256 secure hash algorithm, and the encryption key is formed using a bitwise XOR 
of the individual guard hash values. The Galois/Counter (GCM) 
mode of operation ( Grew and Viega, 2004 ) is used for the authenticated encryption of the result by the mobile agent in 
the cloud platform and the decryption on the mobile platform. 
GCM is a block cipher mode of operation that uses universal 
hashing over a binary Galois field to provide authenticated encryption. It can be implemented in hardware to achieve high 
speeds with low cost and low latency, and software implementations can achieve very high performance by using tabledriven field operations. Authenticated encryption using GCM 
takes three inputs: 
• A secret key K 
• An initialization vector IV , that can have any number of 
bits between 1 and 2 64 . For a fixed value of the key, each IV 
value must be distinct, but need not have equal lengths. 
• A plaintext P, which can have any number of bits between 
0 and 2 39 - 256. 
The output is a ciphertext C of the same length as P . The 
decryption process takes the inputs C, K , and IV and either outputs P or ⊥ , depending on whether there was tampering on the 
cloud platform or during message transmission.

7. Security analysis 
In this section, we provide a security analysis of the proposed 
tamper-resistance model by discussing its resilience against 
various attacks listed in Section 6.1.1 . 
7.1. Resilience against application code tampering 
The proposed model provides protection against active data 
and code tampering attacks using software guards to check 
the dynamic integrity of program code. It is resistant against 
modification of agent code at load-time and hot-swapping of 
code during runtime. The guards detect tampering with agent 
code as soon as the code section that was tampered with is 
executed, which means the time of detection is dependent on 
the location (order) of the tamper in the program. In a more 
complex attack scenario, an adversary can tamper with the 
guard code protecting a specific code segment in addition to 
the code segment itself, in a way that will cause the application code tampering to go undetected. The resilience of the 
approach against kind of attack is described below. 
7.2. Resilience against guard code tampering 
For this attack, we consider an adversary who modifies any 
guard code in the agent to prevent the detection of tampering with the application code. If the adversary is able to 
successfully suppress the tamper detection and reporting 
mechanism, the agent will continue execution on the platform until program completion. Modification in the guard 
code during or before runtime will however cause an incorrect hash value for the corresponding guard to be integrated 
into the encryption key, which will result in a ciphertext that 
cannot be decrypted by the mobile platform. In order to get 
a faulty encryption key in case of any tampering with guard 
code, we need to ensure that the derivation process creates a 
secure (collision-resistant) key. Below we prove that the encryption key derived using the proposed tamper-resistance 
model is as secure as the output of the function used for obtaining guard hashes (i.e. SHA-256). 
7.2.1. Proof of security of the derived encryption key 
Let H be a collision-resistant hash function producing uniformly random output in the range (0, 2 256 -1), and T 1 and T 2 
be two plaintexts. Given 
H 1 = H(T 1 ) (6) 
H 2 = H(T 2 ) (7) 
H 3 = H 1 /varoplusH 2 (8) 
We need to prove H 3 is uniformly random in the range (0, 
2 256 -1) as well, where /varoplusis the bitwise XOR operator. Let P x (0) 
be the probability that the least significant bit of H x is 0 and 
P x (1) be the probability that the same bit is 1. Following the 
uniform randomness property, P 1 (0) = 1 / 2 , P 1 (1) = 1 / 2 (9) 
P 2 (0) = 1 / 2 , P 2 (1) = 1 / 2 (10) 
It follows from the properties of the XOR operator that: 
P 3 (0) = 1 / 2 , P 3 (1) = 1 / 2 (11) 
By applying the same arguments for the other bits as well, 
we prove that H 3 is uniformly random in the range (0, 2 256 -1). 
Therefore, the key generation algorithm produces output as 
secure as the SHA-256 secure hashing algorithm. 
7.3. Resilience against communication tampering 
The proposed approach uses an authenticated encryption 
scheme (GCM) for protecting the privacy and integrity of the 
result sent from an autonomous agent to the mobile platform. According to this scheme, an attacker would need to 
have knowledge of the secret shared key of the mobile platform and the autonomous agent in order to be able to impersonate the agent in a message sent to the mobile platform. 
Any message encrypted with a key other than the true shared 
key will not be decryptable by the mobile platform. Therefore, 
the proposed model provides protection against both man-inthe-middle attacks and any kind of tampering with the communication content between the mobile platform and the autonomous agents using authenticated encryption. In order to 
obtain the secret key of the mobile platform and the agent, 
an attacker can attempt to copy the agent, run it in its original 
form elsewhere, and upon discovering the key, encrypt a bogus 
result with the key to send back to the mobile platform. The 
proposed model provides defense against this type of attack 
as follows: In order to prevent replay attacks, where attackers 
can attempt to use execution flow of old agents, the integration of application sections (methods) into the hash calculation is randomized at each execution. In order to prevent dynamic code analysis attacks, i.e. to limit the exposure window 
of the agent, timeouts are set for the validity of the agent, after 
which the mobile platform terminates communication with 
the agent and spawns another agent for that module. Also, 
for an attacker to be able to send a bogus result to the mobile 
platform, the message has to be sent on the same bridge established upon the call, as the mobile platform will only accept 
messages on this bridge as valid. In order to strengthen the security of the result, an ensemble method offloading multiple 
copies of the agent can also be utilized as described below. 
7.4. Sophisticated attacks tampering with the result 
Let us assume the presence of an adversary in the agent's environment with exact knowledge of the operation principles 
of the agent, such that it can derive the secret key of the agent 
by actually running the agent code. Let us further assume that 
the adversary intercepts agent communication with the mobile platform and replaces the computation result with a bogus one encrypted with the correct secret key. Under such a 
sophisticated attack, the mobile platform will not be able to 
detect the presence of tampering with the decryption process. 
To provide resilience against this type of attack, the proposed 
approach can be extended to offload multiple copies of the 
agent to the cloud instead of just one. The correctness of the 
computation result in that case will be judged based on a comparison of the results sent by the different agents. In the absence of collusion attacks, different results would signify tampering with at least one of the agents, in which case the results 
would not be integrated by the mobile platform. Actually, only 
two copies of an agent will be sufficient to judge correctness 
as shown below. 
Proof that two agent copies are sufficient for judgement of correctness in the absence of collusion attacks: 
There are four possible cases when running two copies A 1 
and A 2 of the agent on two different cloud hosts: 
1. The result from A 1 is correct and that from A 2 is faulty. 
2. The result from A 2 is correct and that from A 1 is faulty. 
3. The results from A 1 and A 2 are faulty and different. 
4. The results from both A 1 and A 2 are correct. 
The other possible case, where both results are faulty and 
are the same as each other is discarded here, as that most 
likely requires collusion of the attackers on the two platforms. 
In the first three cases the results will be rejected, as they will 
be different from each other. The only case when the results 
can be integrated with assurance of correctness will be the last 
one. Therefore, two copies of the agent will be sufficient to rule 
a result as untrusted. Note that this approach is complimentary to the main approach rather than competing, as the main 
approach enables early detection of tamper in the case of no 
tampering with guard code and ensures that the results are 
coming from the correct platform (authenticity) and running 
of multiple copies does not have these effects by itself. 
In addition to the tampering attacks against the application and guard code, attackers resident on the cloud host to 
which the agent migrates or the cloud host itself may try to 
tamper with the agent code. The agent code will be secure initially, as the agent originates from the mobile platform. Additionally, the agent will have to execute the correct application 
code and guard code in order to generate the correct encryption key for the result. In order to ensure that the agent code 
has not been modified in transit to the cloud, the tamper resistance process starts with an integrity check of the whole 
agent upon arrival at the cloud host. Therefore, dynamic code 
analysis by malicious parties on the cloud host will be needed 
to modify the agent's behavior to send a bogus response to 
the mobile platform, and the defense approaches described in 
Sections 7.3 and 7.4 can be utilized to mitigate such attacks. 
8. Tamper resistance overhead 
We also performed experiments to evaluate the performance 
overhead of the proposed tamper-resistance mechanism for 
the makespan of the two applications introduced above. 
8.1. Experiments with face recognition 
The experiments with the face recognition application were 
performed for the case of a picture database of 32 subjects Fig. 7 - Comparison of execution times for the face 
recognition application with vs. without tamper-resistance 
mechanism. 
to compare against. An Android emulator was used as the 
mobile platform, and a medium VMI in the west1-a region of 
EC2 was used to host the mobile agents. 62 integrity checkpoints were inserted into the agent code. Each experiment 
was repeated 75 times and we report the average makespans. 
Fig. 7 shows the comparison of the average makespan of the 
face recognition application with code offloading in the case 
of no tamper-resistance mechanism present and the average 
makespan of the same application with the proposed tamperresistance mechanism integrated. 
As seen in the figure, the difference between the average 
makespans for the two cases is always less than 600 ms, which 
is only about 1.8% of the total execution time. The average 
makespan for on-device execution for the same application is 
around 255 sec, therefore AAMCC with tamper resistance still 
performs much better than monolithic execution on a mobile 
device, and the tamper resistance overhead is negligible. The 
size of the agent code in the tamper-resistant case is 5 KB more 
than that in the case with no protection mechanism, which 
causes an additional delay of only 0.001 seconds on the Wi-Fi 
network.

The experiments with the NQueens application were performed using a Motorola Atrix 4G device to host the mobile application, whereas a medium VMI in the west1-a region of EC2

9. Related work 
Significant research efforts have been put into computation 
offloading since the early 1990s. Improvements in virtualization technology, availability of high network bandwidths and 
cloud computing infrastructures increased the feasibility of 
real-time computation offloading from mobile to cloud platforms in the past decade. 
Early work in dynamic mobile-cloud computing models includes CloneCloud ( Chun et al., 2011 ) and MAUI ( Cuervo et al., 
2010 ), both of which partition applications using a framework 
that combines static program analysis with dynamic program 
profiling, and optimizes execution time and energy consumption on the mobile device using an optimization solver. The 
disadvantage of these approaches is that they require a copy 
of the whole application code/virtual machine at the remote 
execution site, which is both a quite strict requirement for 
public cloud machines and makes the application code vulnerable to analysis by malicious parties on the same platform. Yang et al. (2012) propose a partitioning and execution framework specifically for mobile data stream applications. Huang et al. (2012) propose a low-complexity offloading algorithm to minimize energy consumption on a mobile 
device, however they do not provide details of the system architecture. Lin et al. (2014) present a model for energy-aware 
task scheduling on mobile devices, where tasks are assigned 
to cores on the device or a cloud resource based on their 
precedence requirements. Their approach gives precedence 
to minimizing energy consumption rather than total execution time. The ThinkAir ( Kosta et al., 2012 ) framework provides 
better scalability and parallelism features than its predecessors, however it still requires the existence of the complete 
application code on the cloud server, exhibiting the disadvantages of CloneCloud and MAUI. Furthermore, none of these approaches integrates a mechanism for protection against tampering. 
The two main approaches to provide security in MCC are 
(a) ensuring the security of the cloud platform on which the 
mobile code will execute for all users of that platform and (b) 
ensuring the security of the mobile code and data sent to the 
cloud platform for execution, without relying on the trustworthiness of the cloud platform. 
One approach taken by previous research ( Brenner et al., 
2011; Sander and Tschudin, 1998 ) in mobile code security and 
secret program execution is homomorphic encryption, which 
operates with encrypted functions obtained by homomorphic 
transformations. However this approach was only shown to 
be usable for polynomial and rational functions, which prevents it from providing a general solution to the problem of 
secret execution in the cloud. Another popular approach for 
preventing malicious analysis of mobile code is obfuscation. A 
code obfuscator is a tool which repeatedly applies semanticspreserving code transformations to a program ( Heffner and 
Collberg, 2004 ). The obfuscator tries to make a program as incomprehensible as possible in order to protect it from being 
reverse-engineered or to protect a secret stored in the program from being discovered. While obfuscation makes a program more difficult to analyze, it comes at a cost: The obfuscated programs usually have greater complexity than their 
original, optimized counterparts, which causes decreased 
performance. 
Zhang et al. (2009) propose an authentication and secure 
communication framework for elastic applications, which 
consist of weblets (program partitions) running on mobile device and cloud nodes concurrently. Their proposed method 
leverages elasticity managers on the cloud and the mobile 
device to establish a shared secret between weblets, to provide authentication between the weblets. They also propose 
approaches to authorize weblets running on the cloud to access sensitive data such as those provided by other web services. While this is one of the few proposals considering secure mobile-cloud computing, the authors do not mention any 
implementation details or performance results. 
Zhou and Huang (2012) propose a framework that offloads 
computationally-intensive encryption and decryption operations on a mobile device to the cloud by extending the ciphertext policy attribute-based encryption scheme, so that no 
information about the data contents and security keys is revealed. The main problem with this scheme is that the ciphertext grows linearly with an increasing number of attributes, 
which hurts real-time performance. Xiao and Gong (2010) propose a collaboration model between mobile and cloud platforms to dynamically generate credentials to protect the mobile user from different types of attacks. The proposed scheme 
assumes that the cloud is a fully trusted component in the 
implementation of the proposed security solution. This assumption is too strong especially for public clouds. Chow 
et al. (2010) propose a policy-based cloud authentication platform for mobile users, which is based on the application of 
hash functions on data by the mobile platform with a clientgenerated key and the transfer of the generated information 
to a data collector in the cloud. The main shortcoming of this 
approach is that the mobile client has to apply a hash function on frequently generated data to achieve privacy, resulting 
in high performance overhead. 
Most of the security solutions for mobile-cloud computing 
listed above focus on the confidentiality of mobile data offloaded to the cloud. Frameworks for protecting the runtime 
integrity of code offloaded to the cloud are still at a mostly 
immature state. Although not tailored for MCC, one state-ofthe-art approach for protecting the integrity of computation is 
the software guards algorithm proposed by Chang and Atallah 
(2002) . This algorithm is based on the partitioning of program 
code into regions, where each region is either user code, a 
checker guard that checks the integrity of a code region/other 
guard code or a responder guard that replaces a tampered 
code region with the original code for that region, i.e. guards 
are responsible for protecting each other in addition to the 
application code. The level of protection provided by this approach depends on the number of guards integrated into the program, where a greater number of guards covering a larger 
part of the code base will provide better protection against attacks. The two main issues with increasing the resilience of 
a software program against tampering attacks with this approach are that integration of an increased number of guards 
into the program code results in increased code size and the 
integration of guard code into the program could result in 
significantly lower performance, especially if the guard code 
dominates the program, making it unfit for real-time MCC. 
There is also an extensive literature on the protection 
of mobile agents from malicious modification on untrusted 
hosts. Roth (2002) provides a list of approaches that enable detection of tampering with the integrity of agent data in multihop agent paths. Although the approaches can be extended 
for detecting tampering with agent code as well, they do not 
allow for early detection, i.e. tampering only becomes evident 
when the agent returns to its original host, limiting their use 
in case of real-time MCC. Roth also mentions possible use of 
trusted processors ( Trusted Platform Module ) to protect agent 
integrity, however the assumption of the existence of such 
architectures on the cloud platforms used is too limiting for 
wide applicability of the model. Vigna et al. (2002) propose an 
intrusion detection system for agents, based on the inspection of audit trails formed during the execution of each mobile agent. Their approach requires an agent server architecture specifically providing the audit functionality and mining 
the data gathered from the audit trails in real-time to detect 
malicious modification may not provide accurate results especially under resource-constrained environments. Karnik and 
Tripathi (2001) describe a security mechanism for the Ajanta 
mobile agent system, allowing for detection of data integrity 
violations by the agent's owner. Their approach is targeted for 
agent data protection and detection takes place upon return 
to the original host server, ruling out an early recovery possibility needed in real-time MCC. Jochen describes ( Jochen 
et al., 2003 ) a tamper detection marking mechanism to integrate units similar to watermarks into agent code to detect 
modifications, which requires analysis of the code by the originating host. 
Various solutions provide software-based attestation using 
trusted hardware such as Trusted Platform Module (TPM). A 
TPM is a hardware component that can facilitate various security operations, such as authentication, hardware-based encryption, digital signing, secure key storage, and attestation of 
installed software ( Parno et al., 2010 ). TrustVisor is a specialized hypervisor that provides secure and isolated execution of 
security-sensitive operations of an application using a trusted 
computing base (TCB) and can also perform remote attestation of the isolated execution ( McCune et al., 2010 ). Existence 
of trusted hardware components would be a strong assumption for MCC. A software-based attestation solution that relies 
on timing measurements for checksum calculation has been 
proposed where the detection occurs because of the tamper's 
negative impact on the measurement ( Kovah et al., 2011 ). 
A lot of recent work has focused on attestation for Internet 
of Things (IoT) devices. General hardware solutions like TPM 
are not applicable for the IoT devices because of limited hardware. SEDA is a remote attestation solution focusing on verifying the integrity of the large network of embedded devices as 
a whole ( Asokan et al., 2015 ). It works by distributing the attescomputers & security 77 (2018) 380-396 395 
tation workflow across the entire network by propagating and 
aggregating the information along a hierarchical tree. However, it requires strong trust guarantees for all devices and only 
provides protection against remote software-based attacks. 
SMART is a hardware-based mechanism to establish a dynamic root of trust for attestation of code in embedded devices 
using a read-only non-volatile memory to store the secret key 
material that cannot be changed after deployment ( Eldefrawy 
et al., 2012 ). Further advancements have been made to derive 
the minimal features required to support attestation for embedded devices ( Francillon et al., 2014 ). However, strong assumptions such as the uninterrupted execution of the solution and the minimalistic approach may not be feasible for 
regular systems because the hardware changes may not be 
directly applicable. C-FLAT is another approach for remote attestation of embedded devices that monitors the execution 
control-flow path of the software by leveraging the hardware 
trusted zones to detect any deviation ( Abera et al., 2016 ). It 
instruments the control-flow instructions and computes the 
cumulative hash of the executed blocks to verify the validity 
of the execution. It is limited to detection of control-flow deviations and may not be able to detect run-time data-based 
attacks. 
While most of the solutions for embedded devices do not 
consider physical attacks on devices, DARPA is a solution that 
addresses the detection of physical attacks ( Ibrahim et al., 
2016 ). It uses a heartbeat-based protocol requiring each device to periodically broadcast heartbeat messages which are 
used during attestation of a network of devices. It relies on 
the assumption that a compromised device goes offline for 
a considerable time in which it does not send the heartbeat 
message which can be used for detection. This can result in 
frequent false positives because of failures in network, devices 
and their software.

10. Conclusion 
In this paper we proposed a mobile-cloud computation 
model based on autonomous agent-based application modules, which only requires the presence of isolated execution 
containers for the offloaded agents in the cloud, as opposed 
to other approaches requiring replication of full application 
code. We presented the design of a prototype of the proposed 
model with Android devices and cloud hosts on Amazon EC2 
and showed through experiments that autonomous agentbased computation offloading is an effective tool for MCC, 
providing application execution times significantly lower than 
on-device execution. We discussed major security issues with 
offloading code and data to a public cloud and proposed a 
tamper-resistance model for MCC based on self-protecting 
agents, using various integrity checkpoints of code introspection to detect and report tamper. The proposed model mitigates the issues of end-to-end security, cloud multitenancy 
vulnerabilities and tampering attacks in MCC by making offloaded code self-aware both with the use of self-integrity and 
self-performance checkpoints. The integration of these checkpoints is performed in a transparent manner from the software development perspective, through utilization of aspectoriented programming. The employment of mobile agents in the model enables automated migration of tasks between 
clouds, decreasing the burden on the mobile platform. The 
agents are autonomous in the sense that they monitor their 
own integrity and performance, according to which they make 
migration decisions without the need for management by 
their originating mobile platform. 
We also discussed details of a state-based programming 
model for MCC, where the agent can continue execution from 
the last integrity-preserving checkpoint after migration to a 
different platform, instead of starting execution from the beginning. The proposed tamper-resistance model was shown 
to incur very low runtime overhead and successfully detect 
load-time and runtime code tampering attacks. The mobilecloud computation model presented in this paper is promising 
to meet high-performance computing and integrity assurance 
needs in real-time mobile-cloud computing.

Pelin Angin is an assistant professor of Computer Engineering at Middle East Technical University, Turkey. She received her BS degree in Computer Engineering at Bilkent University in 2007 and her PhD degree in Computer Science at Purdue University in 2013. Her research interests lie in the fields of mobile-cloud computing, cloud computing security, distributed systems and data mining. Bharat Bhargava is a professor of the Department of Computer Science with a courtesy appointment in the School of Electrical and Computer Engineering at Purdue University. His research focuses on security and privacy issues in distributed systems. He is a Fellow of the Institute of Electrical and Electronics Engineers and of the Institute of Electronics and Telecommunication Engineers. In 1999, he received the IEEE Technical Achievement Award for his decade long contributions to foundations of adaptability in communication and distributed systems. He serves on seven editorial boards of international journals. He also serves the IEEE Computer Society on Technical Achievement Award and Fellow committees. Rohit Ranchal is a researcher and technical lead in the Watson Health business unit at IBM. Dr. Ranchal received his PhD from Purdue University in 2015 where he was affiliated with Computer Science and CERIAS. His research interests include cloud and service computing systems with a focus on resilient, secure architectures, and healthcare solutions.

1-s2.0-S0167404818310629-main.pdf.title: Designing an efficient security framework for detecting intrusions in virtual network of cloud computing
1-s2.0-S0167404818310629-main.pdf.abstract:
Cloud computing has grown for various IT capabilities such as IoTs, Mobile Computing, 
Smart IT, etc. However, due to the dynamic and distributed nature of cloud and vulnerabilities existing in the current implementations of virtualization, several security threats 
and attacks have been reported. To address these issues, there is a need of extending traditional security solutions like firewall, intrusion detection/prevention systems which can 
cope up with high-speed network traffic and dynamic network configuration in the cloud. 
In addition, identifying feasible network traffic features is a major challenge for an accurate 
detection of the attacks. In this paper, we propose a hypervisor level distributed network 
security (HLDNS) framework which is deployed on each processing server of cloud computing. At each server, it monitors the underlying virtual machines (VMs) related network traffic 
to/from the virtual network, internal network and external network for intrusion detection. 
We have extended a binary bat algorithm (BBA) with two new fitness functions for deriving the feasible features from cloud network traffic. The derived features are applied to the 
Random Forest classifier for detecting the intrusions in cloud network traffic and intrusion 
alerts are generated. The intrusion alerts from different servers are correlated to identify the 
distributed attack and to generate new attack signature. For the performance and feasibility analysis, the proposed security framework is tested on the cloud network testbed at NIT 
Goa and using recent UNSW-NB15 and CICIDS-2017 intrusion datasets. We have performed 
a comparative analysis of the proposed security framework in terms of fulfilling the cloud 
network security needs.
1-s2.0-S0167404818310629-main.pdf.intro_text:
1. Introduction 
Cloud computing has grown for various IT applications such 
as IoTs, Mobile Computing, Smart IT, etc. Network virtualization is a key enabler of the growing cloud computing 
technology. It allows to run multiple independent virtual 
networks over the shared infrastructure. As shown in Fig. 1 , 
the cloud has mainly three different networks such as; virtual 
E-mail addresses: rajendrapatil@nitgoa.ac.in (R. Patil), hdudeja70991@gmail.com (H. Dudeja), cnmodi@nitgoa.ac.in (C. Modi). network, internal network and external network. The virtual network allows a communication among VMs on same 
physical server. Through the internal network, different cloud 
components such as management systems, storage systems, 
network servers, etc., can communicate with each other. The 
external network is a primary interface between the front end 
(cloud user) and back end (cloud service provider). All these 
networks make a successful provisioning of cloud services to 
the users. However, the vulnerabilities in the existing network 
85 (2019) 402-422 403 
Fig. 1 -C l o u d network infrastructure. 
technologies, and the distributed and dynamic nature of cloud 
pose various security concerns. For instance, the sharing of 
network infrastructure increases the vulnerabilities in DNS 
servers, DHCP, IP, ARP protocols, etc. In addition, the virtual 
network has many vulnerabilities like vSwitch software bugs, 
poor network isolation between VMs, open ports, insecure 
network channels, and poor design and bugs in network 
protocols ( Chowdhury and Boutaba, 2009 ). The successful 
exploitation of such vulnerabilities leads to many attacks 
like Denial of Service (DoS) attacks, port scanning, sniffing, 
spoofing, network probing, etc. 
In literature ( Modi et al., 2013a; 2013b; Subashini and 
Kavitha, 2011 ), various security issues at different layers of 
cloud computing have been investigated and it is recommended to extend or reinvestigate the traditional security 
solution for securing cloud computing. Recently, many network attacks such as IP spoofing, Ransomware, Cryptomining, 
Data breach, DoS/DDoS attacks have targeted the private as 
well as public cloud services. The Microsoft Security Intelligence Report states that the number of account sign-ins 
attempted from malicious IP addresses are increased by 44% 
during first quarter of 2016 to first quarter of 2017.1 The cloud 
services such as Microsoft Azure are frequently targeted to 
compromise VMs and other services. It was observed that over 
two-thirds of the attacks on Azure services in the first quarter 
2017 are performed from China. In May 2017, the worse ran- 
1 https://www.microsoft.com/security/blog/2017/08/17/ 
microsoft- security- intelligence- report- volume- 22- is- nowavailable/ . somware attack "WannaCry" was performed on the National 
Health Service (NHS) in UK 2 . It infected over 0.3 million computer systems within just four days at NHS by scanning the 
connected LANs and WANs. Skybox Security Incorporation 
states that poor configuration is a key source of security risk 
in cloud networks.3 In February 2018, Tesla's Amazon Web 
Services (AWS) cloud system was maliciously accessed via 
a vulnerable Kubernetes console through malicious cryptomining.4 In addition, many cyber threats are evolving, and 
cloud servers are the major target. In May 2017, a data breach 
on OneLogin affected 1.4 billion records on cloud servers.5 In 
July 2017, Equifax credit reporting agency in U.S. detected and 
blocked suspicious network activities associated with a consumer web portal. The security vulnerability (CVE-2017-5638) 
in Apache Struts was exploited to perform such malicious 
activity and over 145.5 Million accounts were hacked.6 
Recently, many DoS/DDoS attacks are reported. In January 
2016, BBC experienced the biggest DDoS attack which took 
the BBC's global website down 7 . In February 2018, the code 
2 https://www.nao.org.uk/report/investigation-wannacrycyber- attack- and- the- nhs/ . 
3 https://blog.skyboxsecurity.com/cloud _ network _ threat _ 
trends/ . 
4 https://www.theinquirer.net/inquirer/news/3027077/ 
hackers-hijack-teslas-unsecured-aws-account-to-minecryptocurrency . 
5 https://www.identityforce.com/blog/2017- data- breaches . 
6 https://www.nytimes.com/2017/09/07/business/ 
equifax-cyberattack.html . 
7 https://www.bbc.com/news/technology-35204915 . 
hosting GitHub website was hit with the largest DDoS attack 8 . 
In general, public cloud services are potentially targeted 
through DDoS attacks. It is observed that the magnitude of 
such attacks is increased with time and technologies. In last 
decade, the DDoS attack rate is increased from 70 GBps to 
1.35 TBps. 
One of the traditional solutions to the above security problems is to deploy a firewall in the virtual network. However, 
the firewall works at a network boundary, and thus, it cannot 
detect the insider attacks ( Modi et al., 2013b ). Although the 
network intrusion detection system (NIDS) as another solution works well in the traditional networks, its deployment 
in the cloud is a major challenge ( Modi and Acha, 2017 ). If 
NIDS is deployed at each VM, it can detect intrusions for that 
VM only, and thus, it is required to deploy multiple instances 
of NIDS. However, managing multiple NIDSs is a complex 
process. NIDS on each physical server can help to inspect 
network traffic of all the VMs through the vSwitch. However, 
it should have a fast detection capability to handle high network traffic. NIDS on the physical switch can help to inspect 
the traffic destined for all the physical servers. However, VM 
to VM traffic cannot be monitored and thus, insider attacks 
cannot be detected. In addition, single NIDS may fail to detect 
the coordinated or distributed attacks in the cloud due to the 
distributed and shared nature of the cloud. Here, it is required 
to deploy NIDS on each physical server and alerts from each 
server should be correlated with the reduced computational 
and communication overhead. 
To address the above problems, we propose a hypervisor level distributed network security (HLDNS) framework 
which is deployed on each physical server of the cloud. It 
monitors the VM related traffic originated to and from the 
virtual network, internal network and external network. 
For intrusion detection, it incorporates the feasible feature 
selection and machine learning technique. We have extended 
the bio-inspired binary bat algorithm (BBA) ( Mirjalili et al., 
2014 ) by incorporating two new fitness functions for deriving 
the feasible features from cloud network traffic since network 
features play a major role in accurate detection of the intrusions. The fitness function can be the accuracy of a classifier 
or a correlation technique deriving a relationship between 
feature and a class label. We have considered both accuracy 
and feature-class label relationship parameters individually 
through Feature Similarity-based Fitness Function (FSFF) and 
Classifier Accuracy based Fitness Function (CAFF) respectively 
to derive the optimal features. The results of FSFF and CAFF 
are combined to get the final set of features. The derived 
features from cloud network traffic are applied to Random 
Forest ( Breiman, 2001 ) classifier for detecting the intrusions 
and generating the alerts. For the performance evaluation 
and feasibility analysis, the proposed HLDNS framework is 
tested on cloud testbed at NIT Goa by performing different 
network attacks and using recent UNSW-NB15 ( Moustafa 
and Slay, 2015 ) and CICIDS-2017 ( Sharafaldin et al., 2018 ) 
intrusion datasets. The performance results are compared 
8 https://thehackernews.com/2018/03/biggest- ddos- attackgithub.html . with the existing research works and validated the fulfillment 
of network security requirements in the cloud. 
The rest of the paper is organized as follows: Section 2 investigates the existing approaches for cloud network security. 
A detailed discussion of the proposed security framework 
is given in Section 3 . In Section 4 , performance evaluation 
and feasibility analysis of the proposed security framework 
is given, while Section 5 concludes our research work with 
references at the end.
1-s2.0-S0167404818310629-main.pdf.conclusion_text:
5. Conclusions and future work 
Network security is a major concern for wide adoption of 
the cloud computing. There are several network vulnerabilities that pose different security risks. We have designed an 
efficient security framework that monitors the VM network 
traffic. It uses both the signature based and anomaly detection techniques, and thus capable of detecting known as well 
as unknown attacks. In addition, signature based detection is 
applied prior to anomaly detection, which helps in reducing 
overall computation cost as anomaly detection has to inspect 
the network traffic only for unknown attacks. For anomaly detection, we have applied different feature selection techniques 
with Random Forest classifier. The feasible features of the 
cloud network traffic are derived using an extended BBA with 
two new fitness functions. This helps in improving the detection accuracy of Random Forest. We have tested the proposed 
HLDNS framework on cloud testbed at NIT Goa by performing 
different attacks in real-time and using recent intrusion 
detection datasets such as UNSW-NB15 and CICIDS-2017. The 
experimental results are very encouraging to make cloud, a 
secured platform for delivering future IT-based services. 
In future, the proposed HLDNS framework can be further 
extended to detect network attacks (such as virus, worms, 
SQL Injection etc.) relevant to the packet data section. For 
this, it is required to derive the features relevant to the data 
section. However, the encrypted data over the network is a 
major challenge to detect such attacks. The proposed HLDNS 
framework can be extended for intrusion prevention by integrating it with a suitable hypervisor level firewall. In addition, 
the proposed fitness functions with BBA can be tested for 
detecting the system level attacks. 
Declaration of competing interest 
This is to state that all authors have participated in conception 
and design, analysis of the experimental results and interpretation, drafting the manuscript and revising it critically as per 
the reviewers comments; and approval of the final version for 
the possible publication in the Journal of Computers & Security, Elsevier. This manuscript has not been submitted to, nor 
is under review at, another journal or other publishing venue. 
The authors have no affiliation with any organization with 
a direct or indirect financial interest in the subject matter 
discussed in the manuscript. 
Acknowledgment 
This work is a part of the project titled-Designing out-of-VM 
Monitoring based Virtual Machine Introspection Framework for Securing Virtual Environment of Cloud Computing [ECR/2017/001221], with research funding support from Science and Engineering Research Board (SERB), Department of 
Science and Technology, Government of India. 
Supplementary material 
Supplementary material associated with this article can be 
found, in the online version, at doi: 10.1016/j.cose.2019.05.016 .

2. Existing approaches to intrusion detection 
in cloud 
An intrusion can be any malicious activity attempting to 
affect confidentiality, integrity, and availability of computing 
resources and services. An intrusion detection system (IDS) 
monitors the network traffic or system activities for detecting any malicious activity ( Rowland, 2002 ). NIDS in cloud 
monitors the network traffic to detect any malicious activity 
affecting the security of cloud resources ( Vieira et al., 2010 ). 
There have been several works till date for detecting the 
network intrusions in cloud. 
A hierarchical IDS architecture ( Ficco et al., 2013 ) installs 
probes at each VM to collect attack information from VM. The 
collected information is forwarded to the security engines at 
the physical server where data correlation is performed to 
detect the distributed attacks. 
Singh et al. (2016) have proposed a collaborative IDS framework that uses Snort to detect the known attacks and the 
fusion of decision tree classifier and support vector machine 
is used to detect unknown attacks. As shown in Fig. 2 , NIDS 
is installed in the virtual bridge, from where it monitors 
network traffic and generates low-level intrusion alerts. The 
correlation unit transforms the low-level intrusion alerts into 
high-level intrusions. 
Chiba et al. (2016) have installed a cooperative NIDS 
framework at front end of cloud and each back end physical 
machine to detect internal and external attacks. All NIDS 
sensors work in a cooperative way to detect the coordinated 
attacks through sharing the alerts to each other. It uses Snort 
to detect known attacks and back propagation neural network 
to detect unknown attacks. 
A collaborative NIDS ( Al Haddad et al., 2016 ) uses Snort as 
a signature-based detection to detect known attacks and support vector machine for detecting network anomaly. The IDS 
sensors deployed at the hypervisor operate in a collaborative 
way to detect the coordinated attacks in the cloud. 
A hierarchical IDS ( Arjunan and Modi, 2017 ) detects network attacks and distributed attacks. The IDS component at 
the hypervisor monitors multiple VMs and detects the virtual 
as well as physical network attacks. The correlation module at 
the centralized server detects the distributed attack through 
alert correlation. 
Mishra et al. (2017) have proposed a malicious network 
packet detection model that monitors VMs at the network 
and virtualization level. It is implemented at the hypervisor 
level and thus, may fail to handle the high network traffic of 
all the VMs running on the corresponding hypervisor. 
85 (2019) 402-422 405 
Fig. 2 - Collaborative IDS framework ( Singh et al., 2016 ). 
An enhanced intrusion detection and prevention system 
( Balamurugan and Saravanan, 2017 ) uses a cloud controller 
(CC), trust authority (TA) and virtual machine management 
(VMM). Here, CC scrutinizes the packet and a hybrid classification model at VMM detects the intruder's packets in the 
cloud using a combination of normalized K-means clustering 
algorithm and a recurrent neural network. In addition, TA 
uses one-time signature (OTS) for the secured data access. 
This framework fails in detecting the VM to VM attacks. 
An anomaly-based IDS ( Rezvani, 2018 ) uses network and 
system-level information for detecting the intrusions. It 
deploys IDS sensors at each VM to capture the network flows 
as well as the system level information. At hypervisor, it uses 
different machine learning techniques for intrusion detection. 
Protocol specific multi-threaded network intrusion detection system (PM-NIDS) ( Patil et al., 2018 ) detects only 
DoS/DDoS attacks in the cloud environment. To handle large 
scale network traffic, the incoming packets are separated 
according to protocol and queued for further processing. Each 
queue is handled by a thread which extracts the relevant 
features and applies protocol specific classifiers to detect 
the intrusions. However, there is a need of extracting other 
attacks relevant features. 
In literature, many NIDSs have used different machine 
learning techniques for anomaly detection. Moustafa and 
Slay (2016) have created UNSW-NB15 intrusion dataset with 
new attack patterns and modern normal traffic for the evaluation of cloud IDS. They have used five different classifiers 
namely; Decision Tree, Naive Bayes, Artificial Neural Network, 
Logistic Regression, and Expectation-Maximization clustering in NIDS. In addition, other techniques such as Genetic 
Algorithm-Logistic Regression approach ( Khammassi and 
Krichen, 2017 ), Genetic Algorithm-Support Vector Machine ( Gharaee and Hosseinvand, 2016 ), k-Nearest Neighbor algorithm ( Dahiya and Srivastava, 2018 ), Ramp Loss K-Support 
Vector classification-regression algorithm ( Bamakan et al., 
2017 ), Cascade of Ensemble ANN ( Baig et al., 2017 ), Gaussian 
Mixture Model and Lower-Upper inter Quartile Range Threshold ( Moustafa et al., 2017 ) and Multi-Layer Feed-Forward ANN 
using Back Propagation and Stochastic Gradient method 
( Al-Zewairi et al., 2017 ) have been investigated for NIDS. 
From literature, we have observed that most of the approaches ( Ficco et al., 2013; Singh et al., 2016 ) are not resistant 
to compromise. The cooperative NIDSs ( Al Haddad et al., 
2016; Chiba et al., 2016 ) have the increased communication 
overhead for exchanging the alerts among all the sensors. 
Some approaches ( Balamurugan and Saravanan, 2017; Mishra 
et al., 2017; Rezvani, 2018 ) are specific to the cloud server and 
thus, they cannot detect the distributed attacks over multiple 
servers. There is a need of correlating the alerts from multiple servers. The cloud IDS frameworks ( Arjunan and Modi, 
2017; Balamurugan and Saravanan, 2017; Mishra et al., 2017; 
Rezvani, 2018 ) are deployed at vSwitch and thus, they are not 
capable to handle the high network traffic of all the VMs due 
to high computational cost. The multiple detection units and 
decision making components in hierarchical IDS ( Arjunan 
and Modi, 2017 ) need to be protected from any unauthorized 
access. Many of the approaches ( Al Haddad et al., 2016; 
Arjunan and Modi, 2017; Balamurugan and Saravanan, 2017; 
Chiba et al., 2016; Ficco et al., 2013; Rezvani, 2018; Singh et al., 
2016 ) need to be evaluated against recent attacks to test their 
feasibility in future cloud-based technologies. There is a need 
of extending or investigating the intrusion detection system 
to fulfill the following requirements of Cloud IDS. 
Handling high network traffic . As the cloud is a large scale 
system, the complexity of managing NIDS at each VM is very 
high. It is feasible to deploy NIDS at the virtual switch or at 
the hypervisor. However, such deployment lacks in handling 
high network traffic of all the VMs and thus, the packet drop 
rate is high. 
Fast detection . Due to the heavy network traffic, the detection of network intrusions should be fast. There is a need of 
identifying optimal features for reducing the computational 
cost. 
Detecting a variety of attacks . The signature-based security system identifies known attacks. However, the security 
requirements of each VM are different in the cloud. Hence 
the signature database needs to be updated periodically. The 
anomaly detection system can detect unknown attacks whenever there is a deviation from normal behavior. However, it 
faces the problem of high false alerts. NIDS should be capable 
of detecting a variety of attacks as well as novel attacks. 
Minimum communication overhead . The distributed intrusion 
detection system faces the problem of high communication 
overhead due to the frequent exchange of alerts among 
multiple sensors. Hence, there is a need of reducing communication overhead while detecting the distributed attacks. 
Resistance to compromise . NIDSs or detection sensors are 
vulnerable to different network attacks. In addition, in the 
case of the centralized correlation of alerts, there is a problem 
of single point of failure. Thus, it is required to secure NIDS 
components from possible compromises. 
High accuracy and low false alerts . The frequent false alert 
create complexity in final decision making about the intrusion. Thus, NIDS should have high accuracy with low false 
alerts. 
3. Proposed security framework 
3.1. Objective 
The objective is to design an efficient security framework that 
can detect network intrusions in the cloud, while fulfilling 
the network security needs such as; handling high network 
traffic, analyzing the VM specific traffic, minimizing communication and computation overhead with high accuracy and 
low false alerts. In addition, it should be scalable automatically with dynamically changing scenario of the VMs without 
human intervention. The objective of the proposed security 
framework can be formally defined as follows: 
Let C = { S 1 , S 2 , . . . , S m } be a cloud system of m servers. 
At server S i , consider N number of network connections 
including N normal as normal and N intrusive as intrusive are 
attempted through virtual, internal and external networks in 
particular time period t (e.g. 5 s). Here, N normal ≤N, N intrusive ≤N 
and N = N normal + N intrusive . An instance of the proposed 
framework F deployed at server S i can be described as a 
tuple ( I, D, P, A, L ), where I is a network interface from where 
packets are captured, D is a signature-based detection which 
checks the packets for known attack, P is a network traffic 
profile generation system, A is an anomaly detection system 
which classifies the network traffic profile as either normal or intrusive, and L is an alert processing system which 
generates intrusion alert, respectively. An instance of the 
proposed framework F captures the packets, identifies the N number of connections and builds network traffic profile 
with k features. For each network traffic profile, the proposed 
framework extracts the optimal features j ( ≤k ) and classifies 
the network traffic profiles as either intrusive or normal with 
high accuracy and low computational cost. 
3.2. Design of the proposed security framework 
The deployment of the proposed security framework in the 
virtual environment of cloud is shown in Fig. 3 . The virtual 
environment includes physical servers, hypervisor, control 
VM (CVM) and running VMs. All the physical servers are 
connected through a physical switch, forming an internal 
network. Each physical server is virtualized using Type I 
hypervisor ( Barham et al., 2003 ). The user VMs and CVM are 
connected through vSwitch, which forms a virtual network. 
The proposed HLDNS framework is deployed on CVM at each 
physical server. It monitors network traffic of the VMs to/from 
the virtual network, internal network and external network. 
Thus, it helps in detecting the virtual network layer as well 
as external attacks to/from the VMs. The components of the 
proposed framework are flexibly configured over the cloud so 
that they can share intrusion alerts with the correlation unit 
(CU). In Type 2 hypervisor, the HLDNS framework should be 
deployed on the hypervisor. 
The proposed HLDNS framework resides at the control VM 
and always looks for the joining of new VM on the underlying 
physical server. Once a VM joins, the proposed framework 
identifies its IP address and virtual network interface; and 
starts capturing the network packets. In addition, it has 
low computational and communication cost and thus, it 
is scalable with the dynamically changing scenario of VMs 
without human intervention. As shown in Fig. 4 , the proposed 
HLDNS framework has mainly five modules namely; packet 
capturing, signature-based detection, network traffic profile 
generation, anomaly detection, and alert generation and 
processing. These modules work serially. In Table 1 , we have 
mentioned the cloud network security specific input and 
output for each module. 
3.2.1. Packet capturing from virtual switch 
The HLDNS framework deployed at CVM monitors the virtual switch continuously and captures the network packets 
to/from underlying VMs. To capture the network packets, it 
uses a Libpcap library ( McCanne and Jacobson, 1993 ) which 
provides in-built functions for user-level packet capturing 
and network sniffing. The captured packets are applied to 
signature-based detection. 
3.2.2. Signature based detection 
We use Snort ( Roesch, 1999 ), a signature-based detection as 
a primary defense against known attacks. Here, the captured 
packets are applied to Snort which matches the packet contents with the known attack signatures for any correlation. 
If any match is found, then the corresponding packet is 
considered as an intrusion and an alert is generated. The 
normal packets notified by Snort are passed to the network 
traffic profile generation module. 
As shown in Fig. 5 , the packet decoder performs initial analysis of the packet. The preprocessor performs the

3.2.3. Network traffic profile generation 
As shown in Fig. 6 , the captured packets are processed 
using multi-threaded models such as; protocol-specific 
multi-threading model (PMM) and active connection based 
multi-threading model (ACMM). PMM ( Patil et al., 2018 ) takes 
the captured packet and passes to ACMM as per the network 
protocol. It distributes network packets among ACMM threads 
for further processing. ACMM is used to handle multiple active 
connections specific to each protocol. It maintains two lists 
namely; active connections list and completed connection 
list. The window size of the completed connection list is w (e.g. 
5 s). PMM takes the captured packet, identifies its protocol and 
passes it to the ACMM. ACMM inspects the captured packet 
relevant connection, identifies the features and assigns new 
ACMM thread or existing ACMM thread based on packet flag. 
The ACMM thread inspects the basic features of the given 
packet and updates the temporary network profile of an ac- tive connection. Then it adds that packet to the packet list of 
corresponding active connection and waits for it to complete. 
Once it gets a packet indicating the completed connection, 
total 19 features are generated from the temporary network 
profile and current packet. In addition, each thread generates 
7 statistical features from the completed connection list. 
Thus, total 26 features form a network traffic profile. Here, we 
have considered the features selected by the extended BBA 
over the UNSW-NB15 dataset ( Moustafa and Slay, 2015 ). 
Bat algorithm (BA) is a heuristic based algorithm for analyzing the echolocation behavior of bats to perform global 
optimization. As per literature ( Yang, 2010; 2013 ), it has been 
observed that BA is much superior to other bio-inspired 
algorithms in terms of accuracy and efficiency. As it uses 
a combination of the existing bio-inspired algorithms. In 
addition, BA offers an automatic zooming into a region and 
parameter control. Thus, BA gives the promising solution 
for the global optimization problem and BBA attempts to 
find the optimal solution for the feature selection problem. 
In the proposed HLDNS framework, the network feature 
selection problem is an optimization problem that has n cube as a search space, where n stands for the number 
of features. The optimal solution is chosen among the 2 n

In BBA, the artificial bats can navigate in binary search

The position of a bat can be changed with the probability of

to 1 and vice versa. The transfer functions force bats to move 
in a binary space. According to Rashedi et al. (2010) , the transfer function should be selected in such a way that it should 
map the process of search in a continuous search space to

In bio-inspired algorithms, a fitness function is an objective function that quantifies the optimality of a solution. 
The objective function can be the accuracy of a classifier or 
a correlation technique that derives the relationship between 
feature and a class label ( Nakamura et al., 2012 ). In literature 
( Chakraborty and Kawamura, 2018; Enache et al., 2015; Taha 
et al., 2013; Yang et al., 2017 ), researchers have used accuracy 
as a basic parameter in the fitness function. Such fitness 
function is accuracy-centric and lacking in providing the optimal feature set. Thus, we have extended the BBA though two 
new fitness functions. The objective of these fitness functions 
is to find the optimal feature set. In the proposed work, we 
define optimality through two types of fitness functions. First 
fitness function considers the correlation of each feature with 
the class label and returns the feature subset. Another type 
of fitness function returns the feature subset that maximizes 
the accuracy through certain classifier. The proposed "Feature 
Similarity-based Fitness Function (FSFF)" finds the optimal 
feature set based on the similarity of a feature with class label, while "Classifier Accuracy based Fitness Function (CAFF)"
uses the accuracy of the Random Forest classifier to return 
optimal feature set.

the position vector p and dataset as input to generate a new 
dataset with optimal features according to the p . The working 
of the FSFF is shown below: 
FSFF applies Gini Index ( Breiman et al., 1984 ), Similarity 
( Mitra et al., 2002 ) and Correlation ( Yu and Liu, 2003 ) on 
network traffic features of the new dataset. Here, Gini Index 
and Similarity find the similarity of all features with the class 
label, whereas Correlation finds the correlation among all 
the features. The feature subset at particular bat position is

Correlation . The well known measure of dependency between two random features is the correlation coefficient ( Yu 
and Liu, 2003 ). The correlation coefficient Corr ( x, y ) between 
features x and y is computed using the following equation: 
Corr (x, y ) = cov (x, y ) 
σx *σy (22) 
Where, cov ( x, y ) is the covariance between features x and y ; 
and σx and σy are standard deviations of x and y respectively. 
The value of Corr ( x, y ) is in [-1, 1]. Corr (x, y ) = 0 indicates that 
x and y are completely independent, whereas Corr (x, y ) = 1 or 
-1 indicates that x and y are highly correlated (positively and 
negatively, respectively). 
For selecting optimal features, the fitness value needs to 
be maximized. The selected features should have a high Gini 
Index and high similarity with the class, while correlation 
among the features should be less.

the position vector p and dataset as input to generate a new 
dataset with the optimal features according to p . The working 
of the CAFF as follows: 
CAFF splits the newly created dataset into the training 
dataset Tr and testing dataset Ts . As per our observation and 
analysis, Random Forest ( Breiman, 2001; Zhang et al., 2008 ) 
classifier gives better accuracy with low computational cost. 
Thus, we have considered Random Forest classifier in CAFF 
and its accuracy is considered as a fitness value. The Random 
Forest classifier is trained over Tr and tested on Ts . Here, training and testing datasets may be different among the bats, 
since each one of them may encode different set of features.

We have considered UNSW-NB15 ( Moustafa and Slay, 2015 ) 
dataset. This dataset includes 44 features. However, all 44 features are not dependent on the intrusions, and thus reducing 
the detection accuracy. We have applied an extended BBA 
with two new fitness functions (FSFF+CAFF) to this dataset 
and found total 26 features which can help in improving the 
detection accuracy. The description of the selected features 
is given in Table 2 . These features are extracted from the 
network traffic to generate network traffic profile which is 
then applied for anomaly detection. 3.2.4. Anomaly detection 
It identifies the possibility of an attack by analyzing the 
applied network traffic profile with the previously observed 
network traffic behavior. For this, we have used Random 
Forest ( Breiman, 2001; Zhang et al., 2008 ) classifier. The classifier is trained (in offline) using previously observed network 
traffic profiles of normal and known intrusive connections. 
This trained model is used to classify (in real-time) future 
network connections into normal or intrusive. It predicts 
network attacks in real-time and notifies to alert generation 
and processing module. 
Random Forest classifier ( Breiman, 2001; Zhang et al., 2008 ) 
creates multiple classification trees from the given dataset for 
classifying the unknown data. Each tree in the forest receives 
the unknown input vector for classification. Then, for the 
given unknown input, each tree votes for a particular class 
based on its training. Finally, the forest decides the class with 
the maximum number of votes. It can handle large datasets 
with many features and provides an estimation of important 
variables in classification which makes it suitable for cloud 
IDS. 
During the training, the number of estimators in Random 
Forest is finalized based on out-of-bag (OOB) error. Here, 
Random Forest classifier is trained over 10 iterations by considering the estimators multiple of the selected features. At 
each iteration, OOB error is calculated and the number of estimators is finalized when minimum OOB error is found. Let X = 
{ x 1 , x 2 , . . . , x n } is a feature set of n features. Tr and Ts are the 
training and testing datasets. It takes X, Tr, Ts and e as inputs. 
The number of estimators e is finalized using Eq. (23) . Here n
