3.2.5. Alert generation and processing 
It takes the decision about an intrusion from signature based 
detection and anomaly detection and generates an alert in 
the form of connection parameters like protocol, source IP, 
source port, destination IP, destination port and intrusion 
type. In addition, it processes the alerts to generate the attack 
signature. For this, it checks the frequency of an alert with the 
predefined threshold ( Th ) for a particular time (e.g. 2 seconds). 
If the frequency of an alert is crossing the threshold, then an 
alert is passed to CU for generating a Snort signature. The 
CU receives such alerts from all instances of the proposed 
HLDNS framework deployed at different servers in the cloud 
and calculates the alert majority factor ( AMF ) using Eq. (24) to 
make a decision about the distributed attack. 
AMF = Number of framework instances support same alert 
Total framework instances in the cloud network 
(24) 
If AMF > S Th (standard threshold, e.g. 50%) for an alert, 
then the correlation unit generates the Snort signature 
and updates it to the signature database of all instances of 
HLDNS. Later, this helps in early detection of such attacks 
using signature based detection. 4. Experimental results and analysis 
For the performance validation and feasibility analysis, we 
have tested the proposed HLDNS framework on cloud testbed 
at NIT Goa as well as using recent intrusion datasets. 
4.1. Experimental setup for HLDNS framework validation 
As shown in Fig. 7 , the cloud testbed consists of three physical 
servers where each server is virtualized using VMware Esxi 
hypervisor.9 A vCenter server 10 manages the physical servers 
and VMs. The cloud testbed has six VMs with different 
operating systems namely; Windows 7, Ubuntu 14.04 and 
Windows 10. These VMs can communicate with each other 
through the virtual network interface (vSwitch) that shares 
the physical network with VMs. The details about network 
configuration and underlying VMs on each server are given 
in Table 3 . We have deployed HLDNS framework on a CVM at 
each server. For performing various attacks on VMs, we have 
used different attacking tools which flood malicious traffic. 
From the vSwitch, HLDNS framework monitors the virtual 
network packets for detecting the intrusions. 
For the comparative analysis and performance evaluation, 
we have considered different evaluation parameters such as;

4.2. Experimental results and analysis in real-time 
validation 
We have conducted different experiments in real-time and 
offline. In real-time, different attacks are performed on VMs 
from the co-hosted VM, internal network and external network. These attacks are detected at CVM using the HLDNS 
framework and alerts are generated. The HLDNS is trained 
(in offline) using normal and attack traffic. We have set VM3 
as a victim and VM4 as a co-hosted attacker; VM1 and VM6 
as internal attackers; and E1 as an external network attacker 
(please refer Table 4 ). 
4.2.1. Experiments in real-time validation 
We have performed different attacks using different tools 
such as Hping3,11 UDP Unicorn,12 NetTool,13 HTTP Flooder 14 
etc. In addition, we have accessed benign services running 
on VM3. As a result, total 86,316 records containing 20,584 
normal profiles and 65,732 intrusion profiles are generated. 
We have used these profiles for training the Random Forest 
classifier. As shown in Table 5 , four test cases are considered 
based on the position of an attacker. For each test case, we 
have applied Random Forest classifier with three feature sets 
derived using the feature selection techniques. 
4.2.2. Results in real-time validation 
The detection results (in real-time simulation) are depicted 
through the graphs. For each test case, we have applied different feature sets (derived using feature selection techniques)

flooder . to anomaly detection. The proposed HLDNS framework with 
BBA+(FSFF+CAFF) as a feature selection technique and Random Forest as an anomaly detection technique has good 
performance in detecting the intrusions with an affordable 
computational cost. 
As shown in Fig. 8 , it detects more than 97% intrusive 
connections in real-time simulation. Here, TPR represents 
that more than 97% intrusion profiles are identified as intrusions. It is observed that more than 99% intrusive profiles 
are detected in case-1, case-2, and case-3. However, in case-4, 
only 97% intrusive profiles are detected. This is due to the 
presence of more number of unknown attacks. FPR indicates 
that the percentage of normal profiles are wrongly identified 
as an intrusion. As shown in Fig. 9 , the proposed HLDNS 
framework has very low false positives ( < 0.5%). 
As shown in Fig. 10 , true negative rate is higher ( > 98%) for 
the proposed HLDNS framework. This represents that more 
than 98% normal profiles (in test experiments) are identified 
as normal for the applied cases. The proposed HLDNS framework has very few false positives ( < 1%), as shown in Fig. 11 . 
It indicates that the HLDNS framework has good capability 
in detecting the intrusions. As shown in Figs. 12 and 13 , the 
proposed HLDNS framework achieves high accuracy ( > 99%) 
in intrusion detection with an affordable computational cost. 
On average, it requires 5.2 s (approx.) to process a network 
connection profile. Alternatively, it can monitor approximately 0.192 million network connection profiles per second. 
This makes the proposed HLDNS framework feasible for 
securing the cloud network. 
4.3. Experimental results and analysis in offline 
validation 
For offline validation of the proposed HLDNS framework, we 
have used UNSW-NB15 ( Moustafa and Slay, 2015 ) and CICIDS- 
2017 ( Sharafaldin et al., 2018 ) intrusion datasets. UNSW-NB15 
dataset is created at Cyber Range Lab of the Australian Centre 
for Cyber Security. It includes modern network traffic with

into a training dataset with 175,341 records and a testing

type of attacks such as; Reconnaissance, Backdoor, DoS, 
Exploits, Analysis, Fuzzers, Worms, Shellcode and Generic. 
CICIDS-2017 dataset is a public dataset for intrusion detection 
tasks from the University of New Brunswick's (UNB) Canadian 
Institute for Cybersecurity (CIC). CICIDS-2017 dataset includes 
3.1 million records. It contains a variety of activities generated by a twelve-machine network over five days. It includes 
different attacks such as; DoS Hulk, Port Scan, DDoS, DoS 
GoldenEye, FTPPatator, SSHPatator, DoS Slow Loris, DoS Slow 
HTTP Test, Botnet, Brute Force, XSS, Infiltration, SQL Injection 
and Heartbleed. In addition, benign traffic was artificially 
generated to simulate user behaviors, while attacks were 
performed manually. 
4.3.1. Experiments in offline validation 
For the network traffic feature selection, we have applied 
an extended BBA with two novel fitness functions. In each 
iteration, the updated position vector p of particular bat and 
training-testing datasets are passed to fitness functions. Here, 
we have considered UNSW-NB15 and CICIDS-2017 datasets 
for the feature selection. Each iteration returns a max fitness 
value and a subset of features that maximizes the fitness 
value over the passed dataset. It has been observed that BBA 
gives the optimal features with maximum fitness value (e.g. 96.94% accuracy on UNSW-NB15 dataset and 99.96% accuracy 
on the CICIDS-2017 dataset using CAFF) with 50 iterations. For 
the UNSW-NB15 dataset, FSFF has returned 18 features and 
CAFF has returned 23 features. For CICIDS-2017 dataset, FSFF 
has returned 31 features and CAFF has returned 33 features. 
The optimal features obtained through these two fitness 
functions are given in Table 6 . 
For the feasibility analysis of the proposed HLDNS framework, UNSW-NB15 and CICIDS-2017 datasets are applied to 
Random Forest classifier ( Zhang et al., 2008 ). The number of 
estimators in Random Forest classifier is finalized based on 
OOB error. Random Forest classifier is trained using training 
dataset over 10 iterations by considering the estimators 
multiple of the selected number of features. 
4.3.2. Results in offline validation 
To validate the results, we have performed 10-fold crossvalidation of Random Forest classifier. Table 7 shows different 
experiments performed in offline validation of the proposed 
HLDNS framework. Here, we have considered both partial and 
full datasets of UNSW-NB15 and four days (Tuesday to Friday) 
network traffic in the CICIDS-2017 dataset. 
The detection results in offline validation of the proposed 
HLDNS framework are depicted through the graphs. The attacks presented in the UNSW-NB15 and CICIDS-2017 datasets 
are accurately detected by the proposed HLDNS framework 
with more than 97% accuracy.

feature selection plays a crucial role in the accurate detection 
of intrusions. In addition, the protocol specific multi-threaded 
feature extraction model extracts the optimal features identified by an extended BBA technique and thus, it helps in 
fast detection of intrusions with an affordable computational 
cost. The proposed HLDNS framework can detect a variety 
of attacks for securing the virtual environment of cloud 
computing. It provides high accuracy with low false alerts in 
detecting attacks reported in UNSW-NB15 and CICIDS-2017 
datasets. The alert processing module handles the alerts on 
a specific intrusion event and sends only high-frequency 
alert to CU. Thus, alert correlation generates the minimum communication overhead. The proposed HLDNS framework is 
resistance to compromise since it is deployed at CVM where 
root privileges are required to access the framework, which 
seems difficult. In addition, the correlation unit is separated 
from the cloud network to avoid any malicious access from 
insiders. From the experimental results in real-time validation, it is observed that the proposed HLDNS framework can 
detect intrusions in real-time with high accuracy and low 
false positive rate.

5. Conclusions and future work 
Network security is a major concern for wide adoption of 
the cloud computing. There are several network vulnerabilities that pose different security risks. We have designed an 
efficient security framework that monitors the VM network 
traffic. It uses both the signature based and anomaly detection techniques, and thus capable of detecting known as well 
as unknown attacks. In addition, signature based detection is 
applied prior to anomaly detection, which helps in reducing 
overall computation cost as anomaly detection has to inspect 
the network traffic only for unknown attacks. For anomaly detection, we have applied different feature selection techniques 
with Random Forest classifier. The feasible features of the 
cloud network traffic are derived using an extended BBA with 
two new fitness functions. This helps in improving the detection accuracy of Random Forest. We have tested the proposed 
HLDNS framework on cloud testbed at NIT Goa by performing 
different attacks in real-time and using recent intrusion 
detection datasets such as UNSW-NB15 and CICIDS-2017. The 
experimental results are very encouraging to make cloud, a 
secured platform for delivering future IT-based services. 
In future, the proposed HLDNS framework can be further 
extended to detect network attacks (such as virus, worms, 
SQL Injection etc.) relevant to the packet data section. For 
this, it is required to derive the features relevant to the data 
section. However, the encrypted data over the network is a 
major challenge to detect such attacks. The proposed HLDNS 
framework can be extended for intrusion prevention by integrating it with a suitable hypervisor level firewall. In addition, 
the proposed fitness functions with BBA can be tested for 
detecting the system level attacks.

He has received B.E. degree in computer science and engineering from Shivaji University, Maharashtra, India in 2008, M.Tech. degree in computer science and engineering from National Institute of Technology Surathkal, India in 2014. His research interests include Cloud Computing, Virtualization Security, Network Security. He has significant contribution in the field of cloud computing security. Ms. Harsha Dudeja is presently working as a Research Fellow at National Institute of Technology Goa under the DST, SERB, GOI funded research project titled "Designing out of VM Monitoring based Virtual Machine Introspection Framework for securing virtual environment of cloud computing" (ECR/2017/001221). She has received B.Tech and M.Tech degrees in the field of computer science and engineering from Rajasthan Technical University, India in the year 2013 and 2017, respectively. Her research interests include Cloud Computing security, Data Mining, Machine Learning and Security. Dr. Modi Chirag Navinchandra obtained his Ph.D. (2010-2014) and M.Tech (2008-2010) in Computer Engineering from National Institute of Technology Surat (NIT Surat), India and did his B.E, in Computer Engineering from Sardar Patel University, India. He is currently working as an assistant professor in the department of computer science and engineering at National Institute of Technology Goa, India. His research interest includes Information Security and Privacy, Cryptography, Cloud Security, Network Security, Intrusion Detection and prevention and Blockchain and DLT. He has published many papers in reputed journals and international conference proceedings, with good number of citations. He has received the Young scientist award in specialization of Cloud Computing (2015) from VIFRA, Chennai, India. 1-s2.0-S0167404821000845-main.pdf.title: Optimized extreme learning machine for detecting DDoS attacks in cloud computing
1-s2.0-S0167404821000845-main.pdf.abstract:
Distributed denial of service (DDoS) attack is a serious security threat to cloud computing 
that affects the availability of cloud services. Therefore, defending against these attacks becomes imperative. In this paper, we present a DDoS attack detection system based on an improved Self-adaptive evolutionary extreme learning machine (SaE-ELM). SaE-ELM model is 
improved by incorporating two more features. Firstly, it can adapt the best suitable crossover 
operator. Secondly, it can automatically determine the appropriate number of hidden layer 
neurons. These features improve the learning and classification capabilities of the model. 
The proposed system is evaluated using four datasets namely, NSL-KDD, ISCX IDS 2012, 
UNSW-NB15, and CICIDS 2017. It achieves the detection accuracy of 86.80%, 98.90%, 89.17%, 
and 99.99% with NSL-KDD, ISCX IDS 2012, UNSW-NB15, and CICIDS 2017 datasets, respectively. The experiments show that the performance of the proposed attack detection system 
is better than the system based on original SaE-ELM and state-of-the-art techniques. However, it shows a longer training time than SaE-ELM based system.
1-s2.0-S0167404821000845-main.pdf.intro_text:
1. Introduction 
Cloud computing helps users/organizations in reducing infrastructure costs by providing various resources online. 
These resources are provided in the form of services. The 
users/organizations have to pay only for the duration of service usage according to the pay-as-you-use policy. The availability of these services is of utmost importance, otherwise, 
users/organizations have to bear a great financial and/or reputation loss. 
The attackers can use DDoS attacks to make these cloud 
services unavailable to legitimate users. In this attack, the attackers put a very high load on the services provided by a victim server on the public network. This results in the consumption of all the bandwidth of the victim and it becomes unavailable. A network called the botnet of several compromised 
hosts on the Internet is used in this type of attack for sending 
are used to make this attack more destructive. In reflectionbased attacks, the bots do not directly send traffic to the victim. Instead, the traffic is sent to public servers (called reflectors) in the form of requests with source IP spoofed as the 
victim. In response to these requests, the servers send huge 
traffic to the victim resulting in exhaustion of the bandwidth. 
The size of the received responses by the victim is very large as 
compared to the requests sent by bots, this process is called 
amplification. The impact of this type of attack is measured 
with an amplification factor, which is defined as the ratio of 
the size of response and size of the request. An example of this 
type of attack is DNS amplification attack ( Anagnostopoulos 
et al., 2013; Rossow, 2014 ). In this, the bots send queries to 
open recursive DNS resolvers. In these requests, the source IP 
address is spoofed as the victim IP address, and special arguments are passed to receive a large response. In response to 
these queries, DNS resolvers send huge traffic to the victim's 
2 computers & security 105 (2021) 102260 
IP address. This results in bandwidth exhaustion at the victim 
site that ultimately makes it unavailable. 
Several machine learning-based solutions to detect DDoS 
attacks in cloud computing have been proposed. The big 
challenge in machine learning-based solutions is the detection of these attacks with high accuracy. ELM belongs to the 
category of Artificial Neural Networks (ANN) having a single hidden layer only, which is called SLFN (single hidden 
layer feed-forward neural network). In this model, the connection weights between input and hidden layers (referred to 
as input-hidden link weights) along with hidden biases are 
initialized with random values. The Moore-Penrose inverse 
Rakha (2004) is used to calculate the connection weights between hidden and output layers (referred to as hidden-output 
link weights). Therefore, it can be trained in a single step and 
does not require iterative training. Due to the random initialization of weights and biases, sometimes, it may not provide 
optimal training accuracy, resulting in low testing accuracy. 
Also, the number of neurons in the hidden layer needs to be 
more than other SLFN, which results in a higher testing time 
for unknown samples. To overcome these problems, optimization of input-hidden link weights and hidden biases for ELM 
are proposed in the literature. Various evolutionary optimization algorithms are used for optimizing these parameters. In 
Zhu et al. (2005) , differential evolution is used and the proposed model is called evolutionary extreme learning machine 
(E-ELM). In Cao et al. (2012) , an improved version of E-ELM 
called self-adaptive evolutionary extreme learning machine 
(SaE-ELM) is proposed. In Han et al. (2013) ; Xu and Shu (2006) , 
particle swarm optimization (PSO), and in Eshtay et al., 2018, 
2020 , competitive swarm optimization are suggested for optimization. In Mohapatra et al. (2015) , cuckoo search, and in 
de Oliveira and Ludermir (2012) , fuzzy fish swarm optimization are suggested for optimizing ELM parameters. 
SaE-ELM has incorporated many new features for finding the optimal values of ELM parameters (input-hidden link 
weights and hidden biases) more efficiently. It can automatically choose the best suitable mutation strategy for an individual of the population during each generation. The scaling factor used in mutation is randomly generated from a 
pre-specified range for each individual. The crossover rate is 
updated automatically during the entire evolution process. 
However, only a single crossover operator (uniform) is used 
in this model. The search process for finding optimal values 
of ELM parameters can further be augmented by incorporating multiple crossover operators. Because the optimal choice 
of crossover operators at different stages of the optimization 
process is different as against a single one for the complete 
process ( Aleti and Moser, 2016; Smith and Fogarty, 1997 ). This 
is due to the distinct exploration capabilities of operators. Several works have suggested the use of multiple operators instead of a single one for performance improvement in various 
types of applications ( Acan et al., 2003; António, 2009; Herrera et al., 2005; Yoon and Moon, 2002 ). This motivates us to 
use multiple crossover operators to further improve the performance of SaE-ELM. We have modified the original SaE-ELM 
model to select the best suitable crossover operator for an individual from the available pool in a generation, automatically. 
It results in better optimization of ELM parameters. Which 
ultimately improves the training and testing accuracy of the model. Furthermore, the automatic determination of the appropriate number of hidden layer neurons is also incorporated 
using the technique proposed in Eshtay et al., 2020 , which 
eliminates the need for manual setting. The modified model 
is then used to detect DDoS attacks in cloud computing. The 
key contributions in this paper are listed below. 
• An improved SaE-ELM model is developed that can adapt 
mutation strategy, crossover rate, and crossover operator. 
It is also capable of automatically determining the appropriate number of hidden layer neurons. 
• The developed model is used to construct a DDoS attack 
detection system for cloud computing. 
• Several experiments have been performed with four stateof-the-art datasets, i.e., NSL KDD, ISCX IDS 2012, UNSW- 
NB15, and CICIDS 2017 for performance evaluation of the 
proposed system including the comparison with the systems based on E-ELM, SaE-ELM, and other widely-used machine learning models. 
The rest of the paper is organized as follows. In Section 2 , 
some recent key contributions are discussed. A brief overview 
of ELM and SaE-ELM is discussed in Section 3 . In Section 4 , our 
proposed model (SaE-ELM-Ca) is explained. The DDoS attack 
detection system based on our proposed model is discussed in 
Section 5 . The results of experiments and their discussions are 
given in Section 6 . Finally, in Section 7 , conclusions are made.
1-s2.0-S0167404821000845-main.pdf.conclusion_text:
7. Conclusions 
Cloud computing provides various types of resources in the 
form of services over the Internet. The availability of cloud services is crucial to the smooth functioning of this technology. 
Attackers can use DDoS attacks to disrupt the availability of 
cloud services. In the present work, a machine learning-based 
DDoS attack detection system for cloud computing is proposed. First, an improved version of self-adaptive evolutionary 
extreme learning machine (SaE-ELM) called self-adaptive evolutionary extreme learning machine with crossover adaptation (SaE-ELM-Ca) is developed. The developed model is capable of adapting the best suitable mutation strategy, crossover 
rate, and crossover operator. It can also determine the appropriate number of hidden neurons automatically. These 
features improve the learning capability of the model. This 
model is then used to construct the DDoS attack detection 
system. The performance of our proposed system is evaluated using state-of-the-art datasets NSL-KDD, ISCX IDS 2012, 
UNSW-NB15, and CICIDS 2017. It achieves the detection accuracy of 86.80%, 73.00%, 98.90%, 89.17%, and 99.99% with KD- 
DTest+, KDDTest-21, ISCX IDS 2012, UNSW-NB15, and CICIDS 
2017 datasets, respectively. Performance comparison of our 
proposed system with systems based on original SaE-ELM and 
other machine learning models like ANN, DT, and SVM shows 
that it performs better than these systems. 
Declaration of Competing Interest 
The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper. 
CRediT authorship contribution statement 
Gopal Singh Kushwah: Conceptualization, Methodology, 
Software, Validation, Investigation, Writing - original draft. Virender Ranga: Resources, Supervision, Project administration, Writing - review & editing.

2. Related work 
In Rathore and Park (2018) , the authors proposed a method 
based on the combination of extreme learning machine and 
semi-supervised fuzzy c-means algorithm. The ELM is trained 
using the training dataset and the membership degree of 
samples in the unlabeled dataset is computed using semisupervised c-means. The samples with a membership value 
higher than a confidence threshold are further classified using ELM. In ELM classification, the samples having classified 
with higher confidence than the confidence threshold of ELM 
are added to the training dataset. This process continues until all samples of the unlabeled dataset are assigned a label. 
In Kalliola et al. (2018) , the authors proposed a method based 
on network flow statistics. During the training phase, various 
flow statistics related to an individual IP or subnet IP are stored 
as vertices on a graph. Based on this graph, the normal behavior of the network is modeled using a one-class ELM. The 
graph updates whenever changes occur in traffic flow. For detecting attacks, the current behavior of the network is compared with its normal behavior. A major deviation from normal behavior is identified as an attack. 
In Li et al. (2019) , the authors proposed a technique for 
DDoS attacks in the Internet of things (IoT) environment. Joint 
entropy features from the traffic data are extracted based on 
Tasllis entropy. These features are representative of specific 
characteristics of DDoS attacks in the IoT environment. The 
joint entropies of source IP and destination IP pair, destination IP and destination port number pair and, destination IP, 
the number of bytes in the packet, protocol, and source IP are 
used. Based on these features, the ELM classifier is trained and 
then used for attack detection. In Kushwah and Ali (2019) , the 
authors proposed a solution for the cloud computing environment. The attack detector is placed on the router that connects the cloud network to the Internet. It uses simple ELM 
as a classifier. In Kushwah and Ranga (2020) , the authors proposed an improvement to the work suggested in Kushwah and 
Ali (2019) . In this, an ensemble of ELMs is used instead of a 
single ELM. All ELMs are trained with the complete training 
dataset. During the detection phase, the outputs of all ELMs 
are combined using majority voting to identify attacks. 
In Roopak et al. (2020) , the authors proposed a method for 
attack detection in IoT. Firstly, a multi-objective optimizationbased feature selection technique is proposed. It is based on 
a combination of the Non-dominated sorting genetic algorithm (NSGA-II) and the Jumping Gene. The conflicting objectives are, maximizing accuracy, recall, precision, and relevance along with minimizing redundancy and the number of 
features. An ELM classifier is used for attack classification. In 
Gong et al. (2020) , the authors proposed a solution for hybrid 
DDoS attacks in SDN. In this work, a framework for managing and evaluating trust among the OpenFlow switches is proposed that can update the trust value of OpenFlow switches in 
real-time. In this framework, the trusted OpenFlow switches 
are equipped with two modules network monitoring and trust 
evaluation. Based on the monitored parameters and trust 
value, ELM is used to classify attacks from normal traffic. 
3. Background 
3.1. Extreme learning machine 
ELM is an artificial neural network with fast training capability. It is a network of processing elements called neurons that 
are grouped into three different layers input, hidden, and output. The neurons of one layer are connected to neurons of the 
other layer using links that are assigned some weights. In the 
training process, input-hidden link weights along with hidden 
biases are assigned randomly in the range [1,1], and hiddenoutput link weights are calculated analytically using Moore-
Penrose inverse. This makes the training process very fast as 
there is no iterative tuning used. This is in contrast to Backpropagation ANN (BP-ANN) where all weights are randomly 
initialized in the range [1,1] and then tuned iteratively. 
An ELM is shown in Fig. 1 . The number of neurons in input, hidden and output layers are m, n and p , respectively. The 
input-hidden link weight a ij connects i th input neuron to j th 
hidden neuron. Similarly, link with weight b ij connects i th hidden neuron to j th output neuron. Hidden layer biases are represented as h i , for i = 1 to n . The set of all input-hidden link 
weights and hidden-output link weights are represented by 
matrices αand β, respectively. The i th sample is represented 
as /Omega1i = [/Omega1i 1 , /Omega1i 2 , /Omega1i 3 . . . , /Omega1im ] T ∈ R m . Where, m is the number of

3.2. Self-adaptive evolutionary extreme learning machine 
(SaE-ELM) 
ELM can be trained very quickly as it does not require iterative 
training. But due to the random initialization of input-hidden 
link weights and hidden biases, sometimes, it may not provide optimal training accuracy resulting in low testing accuracy. Also, the number of neurons in the hidden layer needs 
to be more than other SLFN, which results in the higher testing time of unknown samples. To overcome these problems, 
SaE-ELM has been proposed. 
In SaE-ELM, the input-hidden link weights along with hidden biases are optimized using self-adaptive differential evolution ( Qin and Suganthan, 2005 ). Whereas, hidden-output 
link weights are calculated using Moore-Penrose inverse. Selfadaptive differential evolution is a population-based algorithm where each member of the population represents a solution. These solutions are evolved generation by generation 
in search of the optimal solution. In the case of SaE-ELM, each 
solution is encoded as a vector of input-hidden link weights 
along with hidden layer biases, as shown in Fig. 2 . 
SaE-ELM is trained by repeating the following steps initialization, hidden-output link weights calculation, mutation, 
crossover, and selection, generation by generation until the 
desired training accuracy is achieved or the maximum number of generations is reached.

3.2.1. Initialization 
In this step, a population (with size ρ) of encoded solution vectors is initialized during each generation. Each individual of population is called target vector and represented

are randomly initialized with values in the range [1,1]. For all

generation ( ν-1 ). 
3.2.2. Hidden-output link weights and objective function calculation

4. Proposed model (SaE-ELM-Ca) 
In SaE-ELM, the best mutation strategy for a target vector is 
selected automatically based on the success rate of that strategy in past generations. However, only a single crossover operator (uniform) is used during the complete evolution process. In many studies, it is found that the use of different 
crossover operators during different stages of the evolution 
process can improve the search process of the optimal solution. Therefore, to further improve the performance of SaE- 
ELM, in this research work, we use a pool of crossover operators instead of a single one. The best suitable crossover operator for generating a particular trial vector is selected automatically based on the success rate of that operator in past generations. Automatic determination of the appropriate number 
of hidden neurons is also incorporated in the modified model. 
The proposed model is called self-adaptive evolutionary extreme learning machine with crossover adaptation (SaE-ELM- 
Ca). The various features of the proposed model are described 
below. 
4.1. Crossover operator adaptation 
For this purpose, a vector called crossover operator selector (CS) and the probabilities of different crossover operators 
along with crossover rate vector (CR) are used. The length of CS 
is equal to the population size ( ρ), and its elements are initialized with random values in the range [0,1]. We have used four 
crossover operators, i.e., whole arithmetic, uniform, ring, and 
heuristic. Their probabilities during νth generation are represented as q s,ν, for s = 1 to 4. Initially, all the operators have equal 
probabilities of being selected so their probabilities are set to 
0.25. The selected crossover operator for generating i th trial 
vector in νth generation

4.1.2. Uniform crossover 
In this crossover, all genes of the parents are treated independently ( Pavai and Geetha, 2016 ). Each gene of offspring is chosen either from the first parent or the second, which depends 
on two random numbers and the crossover rate. If the first random number is less than or equal to the crossover rate, or the 
second random number is equal to the index of the gene, then 
the gene is chosen from the first parent, otherwise, it is chosen from the second parent. As shown in Fig. 4 , the offspring θi,ν= [ θ1

It is shown in Fig. 5 . In this crossover, the chromosomes of the

the cutpoint, one in the clockwise direction and the other in

4.2. Automatic determination of hidden neurons 
The number of hidden layer neurons needed in SaE-ELM is 
less than ELM. But there is a need to manually set the numcomputers & security 105 (2021) 102260 7

ber of neurons. To overcome this problem we incorporated automatic determination of the appropriate number of hidden 
layer neurons in our proposed model. This model is capable 
of automatically choosing the appropriate number of hidden 
neurons from a range, for each solution vector in a generation. The technique proposed in Eshtay et al., 2020 is utilized 
for this purpose. We take an upper bound on the number of 
hidden neurons ( n max ), and the number of neurons ( n ) for a 
particular solution vector is chosen automatically from n max . 
Fig 7 shows the encoding of a solution vector suggested in our 
proposed model. The first part shows the input-hidden link 
weights and the second part shows hidden biases. The third 
part represents flags for neuron selection. All the elements of 
the solution vector are continuous in the range [1,1], therefore, these can not be used as flags, directly. We have used 
the transfer function to map these values into either 0 or 1 
as suggested in Eshtay et al., 2020 . A zero value indicates that 
the corresponding neuron is deactivated while one value indicates an active neuron. 
5. DDoS attack detection system based on the 
proposed model 
Fig. 8 shows the proposed DDoS attack detection system using SaE-ELM-Ca model. The attack detector is installed near 
the router that connects the cloud network to the Internet. In 
the present work, we assume a single connection to the Internet. The case of multiple connections can be handled similarly by installing a separate detector for each connection. The 
attack detector has two components preprocessor and a classifier. The working of the proposed attack detection system is 
depicted in a flow chart shown in Fig. 9 . 
5.1. Preprocessor 
This module captures the incoming traffic to the cloud from 
the Internet. Then it converts the captured traffic into groups 
of samples. These groups are applied to the classifier for attack 
detection. The first step is to extract the useful features that 
are used for classifying samples as either attack or normal. In 
the proposed system, nine features are used, more information about used features is given in Table 1 . The next step is 
dealing with the symbolic features of data. One-hot encoding 
is used to convert symbolic features into discrete features. In 
this, one feature value is represented as a vector of binary values. Suppose there is a feature 'protocol_name' that can have Table 1 -F e a t u r e s used in proposed system. 
S.N. Feature name Type 
1 Flags Symbolic 
2 Flow duration Continuous 
3 Flow inter-arrival time Continuous 
4 Number of bytes from destination to source Continuous 
5 Number of bytes from source to destination Continuous 
6 Number of packets from destination to source Continuous 
7 Number of packets from source to destination Continuous 
8 Protocol name Symbolic 
9 Service name Symbolic 
three values, ' tcp', 'udp', or 'icmp'. In this case, 'tcp' is represented as (1,0,0), 'udp' as (0,1,0), and 'icmp' as (0,0,1). After 
that, normalization is performed, which scales the feature values in the range [0,1]. Finally, groups of samples for captured 
traffic during each period t are constructed. Here, each sample 
of the group is in the form /Omega1i = [/Omega1i 1 , /Omega1i 2 , /Omega1i 3 . . . , /Omega1im ] . 
5.2. Classifier 
The classifier used in the proposed attack detection system is 
our proposed SaE-ELM-Ca model. It takes the groups of samples prepared by the preprocessor as input and classifies each 
sample of the groups as either normal or attack. It is a supervised model and requires training with labeled samples before 
being used for attack detection. 
5.2.1. Training 
For training the classifier, a training dataset is used. It is a collection of χlabelled samples of the form [/Omega1tr 
i , τi ] . Here, /Omega1tr 
i = 
[/Omega1tr

im ] is the set of features for i th training sample and τi = [ τi 1 , τi 2 , τi 3 , . . . , τip ] is the label of that sample with 
p classes. The format of vector τi is such that only one of its 
element can have value 1 and others must have value 0. The 
value 1 represents the membership of a sample to the corresponding class. For example, in binary classification, the value 
of p is set to 2. Now, τi becomes either τi = [0, 1] or τi = [1, 0]. τi = [0, 
1] represents normal class, whereas τi = [1, 0] represents attack 
class. 
The training process is used to find optimal values for 
input-hidden link weights matrix ( α) along with hidden biases using self-adaptive differential evolution and calculate 
the corresponding hidden-output link weights matrix ( β) using Moore-Penrose inverse. In the beginning, a population of 
Fig. 8 -T h e proposed DDoS attack detection system. 
target vectors is initialized. For each vector, RMSE and corresponding hidden-output link weights matrix is calculated. 
Then mutation and crossover are performed, and trial vectors are generated. The next generation population is generated by selecting individuals from target vectors and trial 
vectors, based on RMSE and the norm of hidden-output link 
weights matrix. This process continues until the termination 
criteria meet. At the termination, the best solution vector in 
the population represents the optimal values of input-hidden 
link weights and hidden layer biases. At this point, the classifier is trained. The training of the proposed attack detection 
system is represented by Algorithm 1 . 
5.2.2. Attack detection 
During attack detection, the samples prepared by the preprocessor of the form /Omega1i = [/Omega1i 1 , /Omega1i 2 , /Omega1i 3 . . . , /Omega1im ] are applied in 
the groups to the trained classifier and output φi is calculated. 
The output of the model classifies each sample as either at- tack or normal. If φi = [0, 1] then the sample belongs to normal 
class and if φi = [1, 0] then sample belongs to attack class. If all 
the samples of the applied groups are normal then it shows 
normal operation. But if some samples are classified as an attack then it shows the attack has been launched in the cloud. 
In this case, the cloud manager is notified about the attack. 
The attack detection process of the system is represented by 
Algorithm 2 .

6.1.1. NSL-KDD dataset 
It has a training subset named KDDTrain+.txt and two test 
subsets KDDTest+.txt and KDDTest-21.txt. The set KDDTest- 
21.txt is formed by removing the records that can be easily 
classified by 21 common classifiers from KDDTest+.txt. All the 
attacks in this dataset are grouped into four classes, DOS, R2L, 
U2R, and Probe. The total number of samples in KDDTrain+, 
KDDTest+, and KDDTest-21 are 1,25,973, 22,544, and 11,850, respectively. Table 2 gives details about the number of samples 
with their class type. There are 41 features in samples of this 
dataset. 
6.1.2. ISCX IDS 2012 dataset 
This dataset consists of traffic collected in seven days, from 
Friday to Thursday. The traffic of Friday contains only normal 
samples and the remaining days' traffic contains both normal 
and attack samples. There are four types of attacks present 
in the dataset, HTTP DoS, DDoS, Bruteforce, and Infiltrating. 
This dataset contains a total number of 2,450,324 samples. Out 
of these 2,450,324 samples, 2,381,414 samples are normal and 
68,910 are attack. The dataset contains only 2% attack samples and the remaining 98% are normal samples. Due to this 
imbalance, the performance of the system can get affected. To 
avoid this, we have used a subset with 70, 096 normal samples 
along with all 68,910 attack samples in experiments. This subset is further divided into two subsets of sizes 70% and 30% for 
training and testing, respectively. The details about the number of samples are given in Table 3 . The samples of this dataset 
contain 19 features. 
6.1.3. UNSW-NB15 dataset 
The total number of samples in this dataset is 2,540,044. A subset of this dataset with 257,673 samples is configured as training and testing datasets. The training set contains 175,341 
samples and the testing set contains 82,332 samples. The 
dataset contains nine types of attacks namely, Analysis, Backdoor, DoS, Exploits, Fuzzers, Generic, Reconnaissance, Shellcode, and Worms. More details about the dataset are given in 
Table 3 . There are 48 features in samples of this dataset. 
6.1.4. CIC-IDS2017 dataset 
This dataset contains five day's traffic from Monday to Friday. Monday's traffic contains only normal samples and the 
remaining days' traffic contains normal and attacks samples. 
There are eight types of attacks present in the dataset, Botnet, 
Bruteforce, DDoS, DoS, Heartbleed, Infiltration, Portscan, and Web. The total number of samples in this dataset is 2,491,689, 
including 2,273,097 normal samples and 218,592 attacks samples. The complete dataset is divided into training and testing 
sets in the ratio of 70:30, resulting in two subsets of 1,744,184 
and 747,505 samples, respectively. The details of the different 
types of classes are given in Table 3 . There are 78 features in

6.3. Results 
6.3.1. Training performance 
Before measuring the testing performance of our proposed 
system, training is performed. The system is trained using different values of various parameters and the training accuracy 
is measured. The hidden neuron upper bound ( n max ) is varied 
from 20 to 100. The initial population size ( ρ) is varied from 
10 to 18. The number of generations is varied from 50 to 250. 
The mutation learning period ( lp m ) and crossover learning period ( lp c ) are taken as constant with a value of 10. First, the 
number of generations is varied while taking neuron upper 
bound and initial population size as constant with a value of 
10. Fig. 10 shows the training accuracy with the number of generations for all datasets. It can be observed that from 50 to 250 
generations, the accuracy increases with the number of generations. But after 250 generations, there is no improvement 
in the training accuracy. Next, the neuron upper bound and 
initial population size both are varied simultaneously while 
keeping the number of generations constant with a value of 
250. Fig. 11 (a)-(d) show the training accuracy with the different values of neuron upper bound and initial population size 
for all datasets. From these figures, it can be observed that by 
increasing the initial population size the accuracy does not 
improve much. While accuracy improves significantly when

6.3.4. Discussions 
The performance of the proposed DDoS attack detection system is compared with the systems based on widely used machine learning models like ANN, Decision tree, and Support 
vector machine (SVM). The ANN used is a three-layer model 
with 100 neurons in the hidden layer. For decision tree and 
SVM, the functions 'fitctree' and 'fitcsvm' of MATLAB are used 
with default parameters. This comparison is shown in Table 6 . 
It can be observed that the proposed system shows better 
performance than these three systems. For NSL KDD, ISCX 
IDS 2012, and UNSW-NB 15 datasets, the proposed system 
shows a considerable improvement. However, for CICIDS 2017, the improvement is small. Performance comparison of the 
proposed attack detection system with our previous works 
( Kushwah and Ali, 2019; Kushwah and Ranga, 2020 ) and stateof-the-art techniques are depicted in Table 7 . The work suggested in Kushwah and Ali (2019) used a partial dataset of 
NSL KDD. Similarly, in the work suggested in Kushwah and 
Ranga (2020) also, partial datasets of NSL KDD and ISCX IDS 
2012 are used. Therefore, our proposed system is also evaluated with the same partial datasets in addition to the complete datasets. The evaluation of our proposed system with 
the same partial datasets shows performance improvement 
over our previous works. Table 7 also depicts that the perfor- 
20 computers & security 105 (2021) 102260 
mance of the proposed system is better or comparable to the 
state-of-the-art techniques. 
After all the experiments, we find that the proposed system can detect attacks with high values of accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, precision, and F-score which is the core of 
any newly proposed DDoS attack detection system. It is also 
observed that the proposed system performs better than the 
system based on the original SaE-ELM in terms of state-ofthe-art metrics. It shows that the use of different crossover 
operators during different stages of the evolution process for 
the search of the optimal solution works well as compared 
to a single operator. The comparison of the proposed system with some widely used machine learning model-based 
systems and state-of-the-art techniques has also shown its 
usefulness. The downside of the proposed system is a longer 
training time than the system based on SaE-ELM. This aspect 
can be bearable as the training process of the proposed system 
is not very frequent. Therefore, we can say that our proposed 
system is very useful in detecting DDoS and other types of attacks in cloud computing environments.

7. Conclusions 
Cloud computing provides various types of resources in the 
form of services over the Internet. The availability of cloud services is crucial to the smooth functioning of this technology. 
Attackers can use DDoS attacks to disrupt the availability of 
cloud services. In the present work, a machine learning-based 
DDoS attack detection system for cloud computing is proposed. First, an improved version of self-adaptive evolutionary 
extreme learning machine (SaE-ELM) called self-adaptive evolutionary extreme learning machine with crossover adaptation (SaE-ELM-Ca) is developed. The developed model is capable of adapting the best suitable mutation strategy, crossover 
rate, and crossover operator. It can also determine the appropriate number of hidden neurons automatically. These 
features improve the learning capability of the model. This 
model is then used to construct the DDoS attack detection 
system. The performance of our proposed system is evaluated using state-of-the-art datasets NSL-KDD, ISCX IDS 2012, 
UNSW-NB15, and CICIDS 2017. It achieves the detection accuracy of 86.80%, 73.00%, 98.90%, 89.17%, and 99.99% with KD- 
DTest+, KDDTest-21, ISCX IDS 2012, UNSW-NB15, and CICIDS 
2017 datasets, respectively. Performance comparison of our 
proposed system with systems based on original SaE-ELM and 
other machine learning models like ANN, DT, and SVM shows 
that it performs better than these systems.

He has worked for 3 years as an Assistant professor in IPS College of Technology and Management Gwalior, India. At present, he is pursuing Ph.D. in Computer Engineering from NIT Kurukshetra, India. His research interest covers denial of service attack defense, cloud computing, and machine learning. Dr. Virender Ranga has received his Ph.D. degree in 2016 from Computer Engineering Department of National Institute of Technology, Kurukshetra, Haryana, India followed by M.Tech. and B.Tech. degrees. He has published more than 70 research papers in various SCI/SCIE/SCOPUS Journals and reputed International Conferences in the area of Computer Communications. Presently, he is Assistant Professor in the Computer Engineering Department, National Institute of Technology Kurukshetra since 2008. He has been conferred by Young Faculty Award in 2016 for his excellent contributions in the field of Computer Communications. He has been acted as member of TPC in various International conferences of repute. He is a member of editorial board of various reputed journals like Journal of Applied Computer Science & Artificial Intelligence, International Journal of Advances in Computer Science and Information Technology (IJACSIT), Circulation in Computer Science (CCS), International Journal of Bio Based and Modern Engineering (IJBBME) and International Journal of Wireless Networks and Broadband Technologies. He has been acted as Guest Editor for a special issue published in International Journal of Sensors, Wireless Communications and Control (Bentham Science Publication). He is an active reviewer of many reputed journals of IEEE Transactions, IEEE Communications Surveys & Tutorials, Springer, Elsevier, Talyor & Francis, Wiley and InderScience. His research area includes Cloud Security, Wireless Sensor & Adhoc Networks Security, IoT Security, FANET Security, SDN Security, IoRT Security etc.

1-s2.0-S0167404821003977-main.pdf.title: Cloud computing security: A survey of service-based models
1-s2.0-S0167404821003977-main.pdf.abstract:
Cloud computing has recently attracted significant attention due to its economical and high-quality services. In the last decade, cloud services have inevitably entangled with businesses' and individuals' daily 
lives through products and services. On-demand, pay-per-use characteristics encourage corporations to 
outsource part of their businesses to accelerate their services and multiply value. The latest market tendency toward migration to cloud environments, started in 2019, indicates a flourishing trend in the next 
few years. Despite the numerous benefits of the cloud computing model for businesses or individuals, 
security issues still have been stated as the top cloud challenge in 2020. Although various factors affect 
security, technologies enabling cloud computing such as virtualization and multitenancy, in addition to 
on-demand characteristics, initiate new security entrances for malevolent activities. In this study, we surveyed service-based cloud computing security issues to establish the current state of the field. The main 
contribution of this paper is to analyze the state of cloud security in the last decade and provide a unified 
taxonomy of security issues over the three-layer model, i.e., IaaS, PaaS, and SaaS.

1-s2.0-S0167404821003977-main.pdf.intro_text:
1. Introduction 
Cloud computing has received notable attention, providing flexibility, scalability, reliability, sustainability , and affordability ( Varghese 
and Buyya, 2017; Vasiljeva et al., 2017 ). The pillar concept of the 
cloud, pay-per-use, has attracted not only individuals but businesses to benefit from the new approach to make profits ( Becker 
et al., 2017; Bohn et al., 2011; Weinman, 2018 ). According to 
a survey conducted in 2020 among 750 global cloud professionals ( Flexera, 2020 ), due to the COVID-19 impact, organizations will spend 47% more on cloud services in 2021 alone. Top 
growing cloud service consumers, i.e., IoT, machine learning/AI, 
data warehouse, and serverless markets will grow 47 . 2% on average ( Bahrami and Singhal, 2015 ). Although tech giants such as 
Google, Microsoft , and IBM compete to provide the best solutions 
to users, the field still requires more research on security solutions ( Kaur et al., 2018; Kumar and Goyal, 2019; Zissis and Lekkas, 
2012 ). 
Despite the obvious benefits of cloud computing, the complexity of the model and shared technologies have given rise to security concerns ( Flexera, 2020; Rajaraman, 2014 ). The diversity of

client would encounter security vulnerabilities caused by a different combination of a cloud configuration ( Ghobaei-Arani et al., 
2018 ). The National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST) 
has introduced the service-based model as a standard for cloud 
computing. This model includes Infrastructure-as-a-Service (IaaS), 
Platform-as-a-Service (PaaS) , and Software-as-a-Service (SaaS) defining all IT sharable resources such as software, hardware, or network ( Huang et al., 2015; Modi et al., 2013 ). 
The Multitenancy, Elasticity , and Deployment model raises

allows Cloud Service Providers (CSP)s to share resources among 
numerous customers. Through this feature, several users coexist in 
a single instance of a physical device at the same time, increasing the probability of Virtual Machine (VM) or Hypervisor (HV) 
attacks. Elasticity provides a scaling up/down capability for increasing/decreasing resources ( Diaby and Rad, 2017; e Rubab et al.,

The administration of layers defines the other important factor in the security of servicebased cloud computing. Non-uniform management in a layer creates multiple vulnerability entry points and exposes the system to 
more threats ( Bohn et al., 2011; Kumar and Goyal, 2019 ). 
In this study, we focus on the service-based cloud computing 
security concerns to analyze the current state of the field and classify them into a service-based taxonomy. The main contributions 
of this paper can be noted as follows. 
1) Recent state-of-the-art service-based cloud vulnerabilities are 
presented. 
2) A taxonomy of service-based cloud vulnerabilities and countermeasures is proposed. 
3) Research challenges and future research directions are explored. 
4) A classification of vulnerabilities and countermeasures is established. 
5) Generic security issues in the service-based model are identified and enumerated.

7. Conclusion 
Our research studied the last decade of service-based cloud 
computing security issues through a comprehensive analysis of 
high-quality published papers. This study aimed to provide a summary of the current research status and establish a taxonomy that 
maps vulnerabilities to proper countermeasures. To this end, security vulnerabilities were categorized into four classes, IaaS, PaaS, 
SaaS, and generic. The first three classes discuss common issues in 
F. Khoda Parast, C. Sindhav, S. Nikam et al. Computers & Security 114 (2022) 102580 
each layer, while the generic category address vulnerabilities possible in all layers. Although the security concerns have more varieties, we tried to summarize the most discussed topics in the literature. 
According to the research results, DoS/DDoS, shared technology, and session hijacking were among the most addressed issues. 
As has been presented, DoS/DDoS attacks were the most frequent 
concern in the literature. A series of vulnerabilities are common 
network security issues that arise due to the medium of the cloud, 
such as DoS and MiTC. While others, e.g., multitenancy, are cloudspecific groups. As studies show, common issues would be a concern for more study regardless of purposes, service model, or architecture. The complexity of cloud architectures, in addition to 
service diversity and user configurations, could initiate new security threats. Currently, the variety of cloud services has increased 
to address any form of user requirements. Although it provides 
more flexibility, new security holes might be introduced for malicious activities. In the literature, most vulnerabilities are discussed 
in the general configuration or the traditional structure; however, 
both providers and customers need to be more conscious of risks 
and challenges associated with individual decided compositions.

1. Introduction 
Cloud computing has received notable attention, providing flexibility, scalability, reliability, sustainability , and affordability ( Varghese 
and Buyya, 2017; Vasiljeva et al., 2017 ). The pillar concept of the 
cloud, pay-per-use, has attracted not only individuals but businesses to benefit from the new approach to make profits ( Becker 
et al., 2017; Bohn et al., 2011; Weinman, 2018 ). According to 
a survey conducted in 2020 among 750 global cloud professionals ( Flexera, 2020 ), due to the COVID-19 impact, organizations will spend 47% more on cloud services in 2021 alone. Top 
growing cloud service consumers, i.e., IoT, machine learning/AI, 
data warehouse, and serverless markets will grow 47 . 2% on average ( Bahrami and Singhal, 2015 ). Although tech giants such as 
Google, Microsoft , and IBM compete to provide the best solutions 
to users, the field still requires more research on security solutions ( Kaur et al., 2018; Kumar and Goyal, 2019; Zissis and Lekkas, 
2012 ). 
Despite the obvious benefits of cloud computing, the complexity of the model and shared technologies have given rise to security concerns ( Flexera, 2020; Rajaraman, 2014 ). The diversity of

Kent), shakak@unb.ca (S. Hakak). involved elements in the cloud paradigm, i.e., network, architecture, APIs, and hardware, increases the intricacy of security issues ( Pancholi and Patel, 2016 ). As a result, a cloud provider or 
client would encounter security vulnerabilities caused by a different combination of a cloud configuration ( Ghobaei-Arani et al., 
2018 ). The National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST) 
has introduced the service-based model as a standard for cloud 
computing. This model includes Infrastructure-as-a-Service (IaaS), 
Platform-as-a-Service (PaaS) , and Software-as-a-Service (SaaS) defining all IT sharable resources such as software, hardware, or network ( Huang et al., 2015; Modi et al., 2013 ). 
The Multitenancy, Elasticity , and Deployment model raises 
important security implications Hwang et al. (2016) . Multitenancy 
allows Cloud Service Providers (CSP)s to share resources among 
numerous customers. Through this feature, several users coexist in 
a single instance of a physical device at the same time, increasing the probability of Virtual Machine (VM) or Hypervisor (HV) 
attacks. Elasticity provides a scaling up/down capability for increasing/decreasing resources ( Diaby and Rad, 2017; e Rubab et al., 
2020 ). Once a user requires fewer resources, those could be allocated to another customer as needed. In such cases, the previous user's data might still exist in the allocated location, which 
opens up security issues ( Almorsy et al., 2016; George Amalarethinam and Rajakumari, 2019 ). In addition to the cloud computing 
enabling technology, the availability of resources creates a perfect 
environment for intruders to apply attacks to other systems. Athttps://doi.org/10.1016/j.cose.2021.102580 
F. Khoda Parast, C. Sindhav, S. Nikam et al. Computers & Security 114 (2022) 102580 
tackers have the opportunity to execute multiple penetration tests 
targeting known vulnerabilities to find VMs' security holes via lowpriced services ( Zhang et al., 2014 ). The administration of layers defines the other important factor in the security of servicebased cloud computing. Non-uniform management in a layer creates multiple vulnerability entry points and exposes the system to 
more threats ( Bohn et al., 2011; Kumar and Goyal, 2019 ). 
In this study, we focus on the service-based cloud computing 
security concerns to analyze the current state of the field and classify them into a service-based taxonomy. The main contributions 
of this paper can be noted as follows. 
1) Recent state-of-the-art service-based cloud vulnerabilities are 
presented. 
2) A taxonomy of service-based cloud vulnerabilities and countermeasures is proposed. 
3) Research challenges and future research directions are explored. 
4) A classification of vulnerabilities and countermeasures is established. 
5) Generic security issues in the service-based model are identified and enumerated. 
The rest of the article is organised as follows: Section 2 describes background concepts of the field. The status of the current 
research is presented in Section 3 . Current research challenges and 
future research directions are discussed in Sections 4 and 5 . The 
article presents the future and conclusions in Sections 6 and 7 .

2. Cloud computing overview 
NIST addresses cloud security concerns according to three main 
categories, service-based models, deployment models , and characteristics ( Bohn et al., 2011 ). Regarding the focus of this study, an 
overview of the service-based model technologies and concepts is 
discussed in this section. 
2.1. Cloud computing enabling technologies 
The existence of cloud computing has been possible only in 
the presence of essential concepts such as virtualization, multitenancy , and Service Oriented Architectures (SOA) . These techniques 
implement resource sharing among users from a physical instance ( Sengupta et al., 2011; Verma and Kaushal, 2011 ). 
2.1.1. Virtualization 
Virtualization defines an abstract approach to create a computer, enabling resource partitioning in the cloud environment. 
Sharing resources becomes feasible with the help of a VM , via a 
file generally known as an image, which either can be produced 
by users or achieved from external sources ( Barrowclough and Asif, 
2018; Tabrizchi and Rafsanjani, 2020 ). In practice, any sharable 
IT resources could be virtualized to provide multi-user access to 
one resource instance. Desktop, network, storage, data, application, CPU, and cloud virtualization are the most adopted forms of 
virtualization. Cloud virtualization embodies IaaS, PaaS, and SaaS 
models, which implies resource virtualization ( IBM Cloud Education, 2021; Malik et al., 2018; Rashid and Chaturvedi, 2019 ). Fig. 1 
presents an abstract model of the service-based cloud computing environment. In this model, physical resources are allocated to 
numerous users of different layers with the help of a hypervisor 
through virtualization. 
2.1.2. Hypervisor 
A hypervisor (HV) or Virtual Machine Monitor (VMM) in the 
cloud environment behaves relatively similar to the OS in a traditional system. As a software layer between physical hardware and 
Fig. 1. Abstraction of multitenancy by virtualization in service-based cloud environments. 
VMs, it coordinates the various VMs and assures them of receiving requested resources ( Asvija et al., 2019; Barrowclough and Asif, 
2018 ). Having numerous VMs altogether at the same time on one 
machine becomes possible with the HV technology. The most accepted HV classification has been defined as the two-type model, 
the bare-metal and hosted types. The former type operates directly 
on the bare hardware, e.g., Xen, and ESX, while the second type 
runs as an application on the host OS, e.g., KVM, QEMU, and VirtualBox. As a result of direct resource communication, the latency of 
the latter type decreases remarkably. While the high-performance 
capability makes bare-metal HVs a great option in a cloud environment, the root privileges turn them into an excellent target for 
security attacks ( Bauman et al., 2015 ). CSPs such as Amazon AWS 1 
offer various types of virtualizations such as paravirtual (PV) and 
hardware VM (HVM) that would be mapped into the hosted and 
bare-metal HV. Fig. 1 depicts a bare metal HV in which the HV directly translates user VM commands to the hardware and there is 
no need for a host OS. 
2.1.3. Multitenancy 
Multitenancy defines a software architecture helping several 
customers access one instance of software simultaneously. In this 
technique, multiple VMs located in a server utilize the same 
use the same physical entities to service end-users. A Service- 
Oriented Architecture (SOA) utilizes several mediatory technologies, 
e.g., HTTP and Simple Object Access Protocol (SOAP) , to provide the 
promised services to multiple customers. Fig. 1 depicts a possible 
resource virtualization leading to the pictured multitenancy scenario. In multitenancy, physical instances such as CPU and memory are divided into sharable elements, through virtualization, and 
allocated to different clients. The simultaneous access to one instance would degrade the shared resource performance on the one 
hand but maximize resource usage on the other hand. In theory, 
1 https://docs.aws.amazon.com 
F. Khoda Parast, C. Sindhav, S. Nikam et al. Computers & Security 114 (2022) 102580 
an isolated user-space would prevent security issues such as data 
leakage; however, that is not the case in real-world scenarios, and 
more vulnerabilities could be introduced to the cloud paradigm as 
a result of this technology ( Kumar and Goyal, 2019; Tabrizchi and 
Rafsanjani, 2020 ). 
2.1.4. Service oriented architecture 
Service-Oriented Architecture (SOA) defines a reusable software 
development methodology in which components are loosely coupled to enhance interoperability and reusability. Undependability of services improves development agility and makes the SOA 
pattern a proper fit for new computation environments such as 
service-based cloud computing. In this model, target functionalities are provided through service interfaces. Services are typically 
defined through Web Service Definition Language (WSDL) standards and exhibited through SOAP or Representational State Transfer (REST) network protocols. This model of software development 
involves numerous benefits. A user needs the minimum amount of 
information to utilize the interface due to the loosely coupled components. The language of the provider could be different from the 
consumer, which increases undependability ( IBM Cloud Education, 
2021; Wang et al., 2014 ). 
2.2. Service-based cloud computing 
Providing economical high-quality services to users defines the 
main goal of the cloud computing paradigm. These services can 
be any sharable IT resources such as hardware, software, or network ( Huang et al., 2015; Modi et al., 2013 ). IaaS, PaaS, and SaaS 
define three famous service-based cloud models that make the 
cost-effectiveness, availability, and scalability of these services popular for mid-size to large businesses ( IBM Cloud Education, 2021 ), 
(see Fig. 2 ). 
2.2.1. Infrastructure-as-a-Service 
IaaS defines all computational resources in a virtual environment such as networking, data storage, servers, virtualization, and 
OS to facilitate remote services for clients. A user then can access 
the presented services through APIs via the internet. A company 
can rent all required IT resources to build a software ecosystem 
on a pay-per-use subscription basis. Amazon EC2 (Elastic Compute 
Cloud) represents an example of IaaS providers. In this environment, users have a higher level of flexibility in terms of having 
numerous VMs simultaneously. In IaaS, a user can deploy a private 
or public image, which is a template to configure a VM. Private images are configured by users while public images are published by 
an external source such as a company or an open-source organization. The architecture of IaaS might be different from that depicted 
in Fig. 2 based on a client desired model. In the hosted HV the 
VM OS operates on the host OS, which means the CSP manages 
the host OS, and the user governs the VM OS. The bare metal HV 
type, on the other hand, can be directly executed on the hardware, 
which eliminates the need for the host OS ( IBM Cloud Education, 
2021; Shaikh and Meshram, 2020; Vaquero et al., 2011 ). 
2.2.2. Platform-as-a-Service 
PaaS incorporates a cloud-based development environment 
with all required resources through the web medium. Normally, 
programming languages, IDEs, databases, web servers, and OS are 
accessible through shared resources so that a developer can produce a program free from the lower layer dependencies ( Bach- 
Nutman, 2020; Pham et al., 2017 ). In this model, services are accessible through a Graphical User Interface (GUI) via the internet. 
As shown in Fig. 2 , all IaaS layers plus middleware and runtime constitute the PaaS concept. Amazon web services and Windows Azure 
are two examples of the PaaS model ( Singh et al., 2019; Toraskar and Borse, 2018 ). A software development team would find the 
required technologies for all software lifecycles, e.g., design, implementation, test, version control, and continuous integration and 
delivery in the PaaS model. 
2.2.3. Software-as-a-Service 
As depicted in Fig. 2 , SaaS is a combination of all IaaS and 
PaaS layers with the addition of data and application panels that 
supply on-demand application services such as email, word processors, and design applications to the end-users. In this model, 
a client utilizes an application located in a remote cloud environment through a single instance of the application allowing several 
customers to execute the software simultaneously. In this model, 
all software stack and hardware components are provided and 
managed by CSP, and a user utilizes the ready-to-use application 
by an annual/monthly payment. The subscription model is beneficial for both providers and users. Clients pay less than a licensing model, and providers would have more clients as the software is more affordable. Google, Microsoft and Amazon are pioneers in providing such services, e.g., Google Drive, Microsoft 365, 
and Amazon AWS ( Cook, 2018; Li et al., 2010; Loukis et al., 2019 ). 
2.3. Cloud computing management 
Management specifies the other important concept in servicebased cloud computing. In a common classification schema, a 
cloud computing architecture is divided into nine layers, networking, storage, servers, virtualization, OS, middleware, runtime, data , 
and application . Management of layers might be granted to a user 
or CSP according to the service model ( Varghese and Buyya, 2017 ). 
In IaaS, the management of networking, storage, server, virtualization, and OS are assigned to the CSP and a customer manages 
middleware, runtime, data, and application. In PaaS, a user only 
controls the data and application layer, and the CSP oversees the 
other layers. In SaaS, all layers of the cloud are administrated by 
the vendors and the consumer has limited administrative authority over an application ( Manvi and Shyam, 2014 ). Fig. 2 illustrates 
a general resource management scenario without any assumptions 
about the service-based cloud model. It is worth noting that in 
real-world scenarios, the management of layers might differ according to the users' desired configurations. As discussed in virtualization (Section 2.1.1 )and HV ( 2.1.2 , regarding the type, the architecture and as a result, the management of layers might change, 
respectively ( Malik et al., 2018; Rashid and Chaturvedi, 2019 ). 
2.4. Deployment model 
The deployment model defines the access exclusivity of the 
shared resources. A public cloud provides services to any user 
through the internet, whereas the private cloud computing model 
grants exclusive resource access to an organization. In this model, 
the administration could be either operated by the CSP or a customer. Likewise, the infrastructure could be located in the CSP 
location or out-sourced to a third-party private host ( Kim and 
Vouk, 2014 ). A community model presents cloud services to a group 
of customers with common concerns, such as security. The administration and resource access are similar to the private model; 
however, a consumer could access other organizations' services 
through the organization. A hybrid model describes a combination 
of two or more deployment models ( Bohn et al., 2011 ). 
3. Recent advances in cloud computing security 
Researchers have studied cloud computing security issues from 
various viewpoints; however, virtualization, multitenancy, data security , and general vulnerabilities are the most discussed topics in the

literature. Table 1 summarizes the current status of surveys in the 
community. In this study, we propose a security taxonomy based 
on the aforementioned security concerns. In this taxonomy, vulnerabilities are generalized into two primary classes: cloud-specific 
and cloud-generic . The former discusses service-based specific security concerns, i.e., IaaS, PaaS , and SaaS . The latter addresses common security issues regardless of the layer. Fig. 3 illustrates the 
proposed taxonomy. 
3.1. Cloud-Specific 
The cloud computing paradigm becomes possible only in the 
presence of enabling technologies and concepts, such as virtualization and multitenancy. The service-based models, IaaS, PaaS, and SaaS, apply various techniques to provide services to the target 
customers. However, each technology might introduce a new security vulnerability to the cloud ecosystem. In this section, the most 
addressed security vulnerabilities and countermeasures in the literature are presented. 
3.1.1. IaaS 
The virtualized physical hardware is presented as a service to 
customers in the IaaS model. In this layer, the most common security issues are established around the virtualization concept. VM 
image, virtual network, HV, and hardware define top vulnerabilities 
in this layer ( Bouayad et al., 2012 ). Table 2 , presents a summary 
of vulnerabilities over the IaaS layer in addition to an associated 
countermeasure. 
F. Khoda Parast, C. Sindhav, S. Nikam et al. Computers & Security 114 (2022) 102580 
Fig. 3. Cloud computing security taxonomy. 
Virtualization 
VM images are files including important information such as 
VM configurations and logs, a well-known target of attacks. Image alteration by code injection and information theft encompass 
some examples of image template vulnerabilities ( Almorsy et al., 
2016 ). Gonzales et al. (2017) analyzed four IaaS architectures with 
different security configurations. The architecture with a series of 
encryption, access control, signature policy, and isolation is introduced as the most robust model. In this model, although VM images are protected through encryption mechanisms, still VM vulnerabilities such as VM CPU timing, side-channel attack, VM attack 
through the HV, disk injection to live VM, etc., threaten the system. A Bayesian network approach has been proposed to mitigate 
the mentioned vulnerabilities. In this approach, a network of elements located in a trusted zone is produced and the probability 
of an attack is calculated from the network paths ( Gonzales et al., 
2017 ). 
Zhang et al. (2014) analyzed Amazon Machine Images (AMIs) 
security vulnerabilities on the AWS EC2. The established model calculates the risk-gain value of a vulnerability through tactical-game 
modelling in the system. In this model, an earlier reaction to an 
attack acquires more reward. The study revealed that more than 
50% of VM vulnerabilities are related to the Ubuntu OS that makes 
the attack scenario easier for intruders. Having a misconfigured VM 
image increases the chance of a DoS attack. Maintaining all instances up-to-date is recommended as a feasible countermeasure, 
which defines a difficult obligation in practice. However, patching 
public VM images, maintaining running instances, giving patching 
priority to prevalent vulnerabilities, and shuffling cloud infrastructure smartly are introduced as more applicable solutions for mitigating VM image vulnerabilities. 
Huang et al. (2015) elaborated on IaaS security analysis from 
a stakeholder perspective. Malicious activities are categorized into 
CSP and user attacks. The CSP can monitor and manipulate storage, VM images, HV, and Service Level Agreements (SLAs). In contrast, the user could cause cache-based or general leakage channels. Users of the public cloud services should trust the CSP to 
protect their data from other clients. This approach has led to new 
threats on confidentiality, integrity, and data availability that can 
be caused by malicious CSP or other clients. Contractual security is 
a new security property of customers specific to the cloud business model that is of interest to attackers. Dedicating a VM is proposed 
as a solution for cross-VM leakage and cloud-side encryption issues 
to protect the message confidentiality in this layer ( Anwar et al., 
2017 ). 
VM escape denotes a vulnerability allowing an attacker in a VM 
to bypass the hypervisor to interact with the host OS to obtain root 
privilege. HyperSafe, Trusted Cloud Computing Platform (TCCP) and 
Trusted Virtual Datacenter (TVD) are introduced as three countermeasures of the VM escape problem. HyperSafe prevents hypervisor bypassing by preventing write-protected memory changes. 
TCCP provides an isolated execution environment. In this model, 
there exists a Trusted Third Party (TTP) that maintains a Trusted 
Coordinator (TC) and a Trusted Virtual Machine Monitor (TVMM). 
In the TVD approach, virtual machines are divided into groups 
with a common interest. Then, the intragroup communications are 
protected through secure channels ( Hashizume et al., 2013 ). 
The HV incorporates the multitenancy concept in the shared 
environment. The high privilege ability the HV it vulnerable as 
a target for intruders. If attackers penetrate an HV, they can execute any type of attack such as kernel structure manipulation 
and rootkit. Trusted Platform Module (TPM) is a hardware security solution that utilizes hardware capabilities to insure the security of the components. The technology applies the BIOS signature mechanism for secure boot time. Modern hardware processors are equipped with a crypto chip insuring secure boot time 
that prevents HV tampering. The processor can verify the software boot-time information through a series of assessments on the 
chip ( Gonzales et al., 2017 ). 
Mazhar et.al. ( Ali et al., 2015 ) outlined the security concerns 
mainly related to the third-party service providers. The primary security issues related to third-party CSPs are the points that emerge 
due to virtualization, multitenancy, and shared resource pools. Although the research in this context essentially focuses on the communication and architectural perspectives, the virtual network demands more attention. Even though virtual devices were presented 
to secure the virtual network, a comprehensive, well-planned design is required to regulate or monitor the traffic to prevent information leakage. Shared technologies such as virtualization, HV, and 
VMs have generated new security gates for adversaries. Rewriting 
the packets could be a solution for VM security matters, maintaining a balance between privacy and monitoring. The tamper-
