Hardware 
Cryptography mechanisms are applied to increase the security 
level of data in the transit and storage processes. Despite the 
mechanism in place, data should be decrypted for process purposes at some point. The multitenancy feature of the cloud facilitates access to storage mediums such as disk, memory, and cache. 
Intruders located in a shared host with a victim can access the 
plain value of the key, or any form of confidential data in the storage mediums. Cache-based side-channel attacks, a family of cross- 
VM side-channel vulnerabilities, represent a form of these concerns. Another complaint with cloud-based services is the access 
limitation of upper layer users to the lower layers. Intel is working 
on the Software Guard Extension (SGX) technology that provides a 
protected memory area to run an application called an enclave. In 
the secure enclaves, even privileged software such as the OS has 
no right to access the protected area ( Coppolino et al., 2017 ). 
3.1.2. PaaS 
In this layer, all required services are provided to customers for 
deployment purposes via an SOA model. Resource sharing via multitenancy and an SOA increase the risk of numerous security issues Bouayad et al. (2012) . Table 3 , presents a summary of vulner- abilities over the PaaS layer in addition to an associated countermeasure. 
SOA 
Resource sharing raises serious issues when there exists a 
conflict of interest between customers. A possible scenario is 
the colocation of two competitors in a single host. The Chinese Wall Model mitigates the accidental or intentional access to 
shared resources by dividing users into conflict of interest groups 
and allocating physical resources accordingly ( Hay et al., 2011 ). 
Arora et al. (2012) propose a combination of policies, monitoring, 
and restrictions as the solution for multitenancy and virtualization. 
SLA represents one of the policies determining the advantages and 
liabilities of each participant. Secure Configuration Policy (SCP) describes another policy that guarantees a secure configuration in the 
hardware/software layer or SLA configuration. 
Freet et al. (2015) investigate the digital forensic security challenges in the service-based cloud environment. In this paradigm, 
processing power, data storage, and other shared resources rely on 
the IaaS layer. In a common shared resource environment, the data 
packets of the VM traverse in all possible ways via a host machine. 
Although each VM is separated from other VMs on an actual device, any undermined VM can assault another VM in the organization. Additionally, any misconfiguration of an HV results in a DoS 
attack as it permits one VM to utilize all framework assets against 
other VMs in a shared environment. Any changes in the configuration and settings by a malicious user in the PaaS layer can influence the whole cloud architecture. As PaaS has a service-oriented 
architecture, the primary security challenges are XML-related, DoS, 
injection, and MiTC attacks in this layer.

Rodero-Merino et al. (2012) discuss two popular programming 
language technologies in the PaaS paradigm, i.e., Java, and.Net, 
in terms of multitenancy subjects. According to experimental results, Java and.NET do not offer a fully secured hosting setting. 
More specifically, a detailed analysis of the security features over 
the Enterprise Java Bean (EJB) and Open Service Gateway Initiative 
(OSGi) were conducted to evaluate the security of the most prominent Java containers in the cloud domain. In this study, isolation, 
resource accounting , and safe thread termination are proposed as 
remedies for the multitenancy technology vulnerabilities. Java supports isolation through JVM technologies, i.e., EJB containers and 
servlets, whereas the.Net platform facilitates the isolation through 
Common Language Runtime (CLR) profiling. Google App Engine 
(GAE), a Java-based cloud engine, applies another approach for isolation. In a GAE, each entity resides in an isolated VM and has restricted access to resources. 
Vulnerable Host/Object 
In a shared environment, customers' objects are threatened by 
multiple elements, i.e., other tenants, hosts, and external attackers. The combined Trusted Computing Base (TCB) is normally applied as a solution for vulnerable objects and lack of interoperability for intra-API communications ( Verma and Adhikari, 2020 ). 
A model including four practices is proposed to address vulnerable objects: Transport Layer Security (TLS), Sticky Access Control Policy (SACP), Policy Enforcement Points (PEPs) , and Undeniable Logging 
Protocol (ULP). constitute the model. The well-known network protocol, TLS, delivers secure communication through a cryptography 
mechanism. The SACP and PEP deploy a fine-grained object-based 
access control and ULP assures the authenticity of the logging system ( Sandikkaya and Harmanci, 2012 ). Interoperability defines the 
fundamental concept enabling cloud paradigm, APIs, and platforms 
to communicate together but is recognized as a PaaS vulnerability. 
A TCB provides a solution for vulnerable hosts and lack of interoperability. An encryption layer can be added to protect against exposed objects in the proposed method ( McKay and Cooper, 2018 ). 
3.1.3. SaaS 
Built on top of two layers, SaaS inherits security issues of lower 
layers. In addition, dependence on web APIs makes the model vulnerable to web technology security issues ( Bouayad et al., 2012 ). 
Table 4 presents a summary of vulnerabilities over the SaaS layer 
in addition to the associated countermeasures. 
Application 
In the OWASP project, web technology vulnerabilities have been 
studied and the top ten are introduced. Broken authentication, injection, XML External Entities (XXE), broken access control, sensitive data exposure, security misconfiguration, insecure deserialization, cross-site scripting (XSS), and insufficient logging and monitoring define part of web API concerns ( Li, 2020 ). 
Chouhan et al. (2015) classify SaaS security issues into three 
main categories, data, application, and deployment. Data security 
includes security of data in storage, transit, backup, recovery, integrity, and access control. The delivery of SaaS services strongly depends on web technologies and concepts. Software design flow, 
user interface technologies, web services, and malware define application security points. Design and implementation of a web application include front-end and back-end languages, libraries, and 
dependencies such as HTML, JavaScript, PHP, Java, Python, SOAP, 
etc. Normally, a design might not cover all security aspects and 
introduces subsequent vulnerabilities into the system. 
Grobauer et al. (2011) reviewed the security subjects of the core 
cloud computing technologies and their characteristics. Vulnerabilities are divided into the following categories: core technologies, 
essential cloud characteristics, prevalent security concerns, defects 
in known security controls, and architectural components. The authentication topic has been introduced as the primary vulnerability of the cloud system that compromises user data. As the SaaS 
layer communicates directly with the end-users through web APIs, 
the layer is vulnerable to web technology security issues such as 
authorization, access control, and session hijacking. Each cloud environment must have strong mechanisms and protocols to control 
identity, authentication, authorization, and auditing. The cryptographic algorithms are counted as a remedy for a majority of security issues. A secure channel through cryptography highly alleviates 
the hijacking threat. 
Data security is an important issue in all layers of the 
cloud; however, SaaS users totally rely on CSP to protect any 
breaches of credential information either in transit or in storage. Hashizume et al. (2013) discussed three countermeasures 
to address the data breach problem, Fragmentation-Redundancy- 
Scattering (FRS), digital signatures, and homomorphic encryption. 
FRS splits primary data into parts with little meaningful information and propagates the parts across the whole system in a redundant way. In the digital signature approach, the RSA algorithm is 
applied to verify data authenticity after transit through the network. Homomorphic algorithms are applied to messages that are 
manipulated in an encrypted format. Depending on the goal of a 
system, one or a combination of the aforementioned approaches 
would be a solution for protection against a data breach. 
Data vulnerabilities in this layer can be mitigated by protocols 
such as Secure Socket Layer (SSL) and TLS. The protocols create a 
secure channel between a client and server to establish end-toend 
secure communication. HTTP examination is another solution to 
enhance data issues. To this end, a web application scanner examines the HTTP requests and responses regularly to provide log files 
via read- only APIs through a central log server ( Freet et al., 2015 ). 
3.2. Cloud-generic vulnerabilities 
Cloud computing embodies network technologies that comprise 
inherited security issues such as TCP/IP communication vulnerabilities ( Khalaf et al., 2019 ). In this model, a layer would have specific and generic security concerns. The former arises due to applied technology in a layer such as virtualization in IaaS; the latter 
can be either for the network or common cloud-based issues in all

3.2.1. DoS/DDoS 
In Denial of Service (DoS) or Distributed Denial of Service 
(DDoS) attacks, the attacker exploits a TCP vulnerability to cause resource starvation for a legitimate user. In the cloud paradigm, 
VMs are exposed to DoS attacks due to the insufficient bandwidth 
under-provisioning issue. A series of papers propose solutions to 
mitigate this attack ( Arora et al., 2012; Jabir et al., 2016 ). 
SYN cookie analysis and connection limitation outline a series 
of solutions to prevent DoS attacks. In SYN flow analysis, a se- 
F. Khoda Parast, C. Sindhav, S. Nikam et al. Computers & Security 114 (2022) 102580 
quence of synchronization messages are communicated between 
client and server. In the SYN flood attack, the attacker initiates 
the handshaking for synchronization, but never completes it entirely. To prevent this type of DoS attack, the server never waits 
for an acknowledgement. The handshaking process will be completed once the SYN-ACK is correctly received. By this approach, 
the server is never occupied with the incomplete synchronization 
mechanism required for a TCP connection. The other DoS attack is 
applied by holding resources busy through open connections. This 
type of attack is normally prevented by limiting connections from 
a client with the same IP address ( Coppolino et al., 2017 ). 
DoS/DDoS attacks can be detected by thermal behaviour analysis. MetaMORP(h)OSy is a formal massive object-based profiler 
for the real-time behaviour modelling of a software system. The 
profiler can be extended to examine thermal requirements and 
behaviours. Formal models are adopted to determine strange actions, verify functional and non-functional properties at the early 
stage, and implement monitoring at run-time. MetaMORP(h)OSY 
produces an observer at runtime for evaluating thermal properties. 
The observer monitors central interfaces and thermal regions on a 
remote system to find any differences between expected and normal thermal behaviour; the monitor evaluates new process parameters in small time fractions ( Amato et al., 2018 ). 
3.2.2. MiTC 
Man-in-The-Cloud (MiTC) attack determines the second important vulnerability in the generic category. In this threat, the intruder commences by collecting information from the web to target a victim. Once detecting vulnerabilities, such as open ports 
or unprotected servers, the attacker performs a malicious activity. Therefore, a series of operations is recommended to mitigate 
the probability of MiTC ( Arora et al., 2012 ). Probes should be prevented through strongly configured firewalls and IDSs and critical 
data should be hidden. Closing non-essential ports and preventing 
routing bypass through any mechanism are strongly suggested. In 
addition, the current recommendations are beneficial in DoS attack 
prevention ( Jabir et al., 2016 ). 
3.2.3. API 
API denotes the key concept of the cloud ecosystem for communication. Although this feature facilitates a convenient mechanism 
for information transmission it raises security concerns. A lack of 
interoperability could lead to a serious issue if a well-defined policy is not already in place ( Sandikkaya and Harmanci, 2012 ). To 
prevent malicious activity, such as eavesdropping or alteration, the 
customer can benefit from a central server log system that captures all activities. Then, the log file is stored signed and encrypted 
to prevent alteration ( Freet et al., 2015 ). 
Isharufe et al. (2020) address PaaS security issues due to cloud 
features, i.e., on-demand self-service, broad network access, resource 
pooling, rapid elasticity , and measured service . On-demand selfservice capability is provided through an API to customers. As recommended, the CSP should utilize a Multi-factor Authentication 
(MFA) mechanism to ensure the identity of the user and the confidential data should be transferred through a secure system such as 
the latest version of Security Assertion Markup Language (SAML). 
Zissis and Lekkas (2012) analyzed authentication and authorization as two important processes in an information system. Authentication verifies user identity versus authorization defines the level 
of access to either hardware or software resources. Shibboleth is 
open-source middleware software that applies SAML standards to 
guarantee proper authentication and authorization. Shibboleth implies a Single Sign-On (SSO) standard that relies on third-party mediation ( Zissis and Lekkas, 2012 ). 3.2.4. Malicious insider 
A malicious insider in a cloud computing environment could be 
a CSP employee who misuses his/her access privileges in a nefarious way. Discretionary Access Control (DAC) and Mandatory Access Control (MAC) introduce two approaches for preventing such 
vulnerabilities ( Sengupta et al., 2011 ). Both methods are applied to 
the OS to restrict access through strict permission policies. MAC is 
stated as the proper access restriction mechanism for the cloud environment due to the higher level of security. AppArmor in Linux 
and TrustedBSD in the Mac OS are examples of the MAC approach ( Coppolino et al., 2017 ). 
Kamongi et al. (2013) proposed a framework for evaluating 
the security vulnerabilities of the Cloud Computing System (CCS) 
named VULCAN , a comprehensive security assessment via a Natural Language Processing (NLP) method and ontology reasoning . 
The framework benefits from the Ontology Vulnerability Database 
(OVDB) and National Vulnerabilities Database (NVD) repositories to 
identify known security issues or new patterns. The framework indexes all possible vulnerabilities based on the script of NVD and 
OVDB. Then, a cloud-based system should be tested for any security concerns. The VULCAN framework functions similar to a classifier in which all vulnerabilities are categorized and labelled into 
groups. When a vulnerability is discovered, the framework will 
mark that new instance as one of the known categories based on 
the vulnerability features. Gonzales et al. (2017) applied a Bayesian 
network to find the malicious insider paths. The primary purpose 
of finding the attack path is to understand the vulnerability level of 
the information system to derive probabilistic standards of enterprise network security. The proposed approach has been extended 
to the CCSs by constructing an acyclic directed graph through the 
attack paths. This approach attempts to consider the contributions 
of specific CCS security features in reducing the vulnerabilities of 
elements in a CCS to reduce the overall security profile of an IaaS 
cloud. 
3.2.5. Hijacking 
Account or service hijacking is the theft of user credential data 
allowing further malicious activities. Proper identity and access 
management policies help in mitigating the issue. Dynamic credentials are a recommended countermeasure for hijacking. This 
method changes the secret values based on predefined parameters 
such as user location or received packages ( Hashizume et al., 2013 ). 
Khan and Al-Yasiri (2016) present current and future privacy and security arguments by interviewing cloud developers, 
providers, and IT managers. A vulnerability might originate from 
a misconfiguration or improper action in various layers or stages. 
Finding the source of the issue helps in detecting and preventing hijacking. The result of the study shows that weak credentials 
and improper authorization validations are typical causes that lead 
to an account or service hijacking. Inappropriate data handling in 
transit, processing and storage by an untrusted third party could 
cause data leakage. Insecure third-party APIs increase the DoS attack probability. By cross-site scripting or SQL injection, attackers can manipulate user data. An unprotected virtual machine increases the virtual network sniffing/spoofing attack probability. 
In account or service hijacking, the attacker steals credential 
information so that they can exploit the system. As the cloud 
paradigm opens new entry points for the intruders, hijacking can 
be operated from either layer. Two-factor authentication, Provable 
Data Possession (PDP), Network-based Intrusion Detection Systems 
(NIDS), and cryptography algorithms are a group of solutions to 
mitigate the account or session hijacking threats ( Selvamani and 
Jayanthi, 2015 ). In two-factor authentication, normally a secondary 
device is involved to validate the user authenticity. Whereas the 
PDP, defines a public key-based method to verify the manipulated 
F. Khoda Parast, C. Sindhav, S. Nikam et al. Computers & Security 114 (2022) 102580 
data by a server, NIDS apply the erasure codes method for intrusion detection ( Chawki et al., 2018 ). 
4. Challenges 
The cloud computing paradigm provides numerous benefits 
for businesses and individuals. However, applied technologies and 
complex architectures raise challenges that need to be addressed. 
Regarding the scope of the current study, we will discuss IaaS, 
PaaS, SaaS, and generic security challenges. 
4.1. IaaSs security challenges 
According to the literature, lower layers' vulnerabilities have the 
most destructive effect on the whole system. That means, if a security issue initiates in the IaaS layer, that would propagate to the 
upper layers and endanger the whole system. Due to the access 
restrictions in lower layers, the customer has few chances to apply 
an appropriate security countermeasure ( Hashizume et al., 2013 ). 
Virtualization and multitenancy are the top security concerns in 
this layer. The virtualization in the IaaS environment would cause 
associated security issues such as DoS and cross-VM side-channel 
attacks due to the bandwidth under-provisioning issues or colocation VM escape ( Chawki et al., 2018 ). 
VM images in an IaaS model include potential vulnerabilities. 
In this environment, the user can use a public or private image to 
configure VMs. Public images are published with outer providers 
such as individuals, open-source communities, or IT companies. In 
Amazon EC2 alone, a user can apply more than 60 0 0 public images. Regarding the result of a current study, on public images have 
the potential of backdoors as providers might forget to remove 
keys or other critical information properly ( Zhang et al., 2014 ). 
Stability of network configurations create a conventional attack surface for intruders. In the IaaS layer, normally the range of 
IP addresses is more predictable and stable compared to the traditional computing model. In addition, non-cloud machines might 
utilize firewalls or another protective mechanism. The heterogeneous environment of the cloud makes it easier for an intruder to 
exploit security holes ( Zhang et al., 2014 ). 
4.2. PaaS security challenges 
Lock-in defines a PaaS concern. The PaaS paradigm provides a 
development environment for a software developer to enjoy extensible software and hardware resources for developing a product. As there is no unified standard for all vendors, the lock-in 
issue threatens PaaS customers. Lock-in happens once the customer requires services that are not available in the primary vendor environment Kritikos et al. (2017) . To this end, the customer 
should migrate to another provider or host facility. However, the 
migration to other vendor servers is highly inefficient in terms 
of budget or time for the customer. Therefore, there would be a 
lock-in condition for PaaS customers who should decide between 
staying in a vendor with limitations or accept the cost of migration Arora et al. (2012) . 
The SOA model enables PaaS customers to deploy their application or software in a shared environment. Although the model provides numerous benefits, it limits access to the lower layer, making it difficult for a PaaS customer to apply security tools. Therefore, the primary configuration makes the system vulnerable to 
MiTC, DoS, injection, and XML-related attacks. However, the PaaS 
API should necessarily include high security standards for service 
delivery to the upper layer customers Freet et al. (2015) . 4.3. SaaS security challenges 
Multitenancy facilitates a cheaper service either for a provider 
or end-user. In some cases, users should only pay-as-you-go, while 
in others they might receive free services such as Google Docs. 
This concept supports the coexistence of numerous users in a single instance of software/hardware at the same time. In this environment, data management could become a challenging affair. 
Preserving data locality, integrity, confidentiality, segregation, and 
backup could become difficult to manage as several users will be 
using the same system. 
Web API defines the SaaS delivery model to final customers 
that exposes the method to various web technology security flaws. 
Therefore, lack of a proper policy could lead to severe security 
or privacy problems such as access to user data in a common 
area in the presence of multitenancy and data leakage in a shared 
database system ( Vaquero et al., 2011 ). 
Control limitation explains another main challenge since a 
user has no control over the application, OS, and middleware in 
the SaaS cloud. All services are controlled by the CSP and users can 
access only the rented application from the CSP. This limitation intuitively means that a customer has no access to the log files or 
any tools to monitor, alter, apply or improve security policies. 
4.4. Cloud-Generic security challenges 
Patching software regularly reduces security concern at all 
layer. Despite the provided taxonomy, we realize that the late 
patching process of software was mentioned as a potential vulnerability. By releasing on-time patching, software providers reduce 
the risk of various security issues. On the other side, users that ignore new updates would face the same consequences ( Zhang et al., 
2014 ). 
Cost-effectiveness of a cloud environment is not only a beneficial concept for users but also intruders. Having inexpensive machines makes the penetration test easier for attackers. 
Intruders can benefit from this concept to exploit cloud customers ( Zhang et al., 2014 ). 
Lack of interoperability explains a potential security issue 
of APIs. Interoperability means the ability to communicate between different com ponents or platforms of a system in a compatible way that normally is operated through an API. There are 
various scenarios underlying this ability such as migration from 
one CSP to another, or upgrading services in a CSP or customer 
side ( Machado et al., 2009 ). 
Internet protocol is associated with known security flaws such 
as DoS, DDoS, MiTC, and account or service hijacking. All aforementioned vulnerabilities can be operated from all layers. However, according to the PaaS architecture model, intruders tend 
to operate the attack scenario in this layer more than others ( Bouayad et al., 2012 ). 
Malicious insiders is a known security issue that needs more 
attention. According to Bouayad et al. (2012) , more than 70% of 
attacks are related to the company's human resources. Although 
products such as SGX can help us to make some progress in this 
direction, it still demands greater effort s ( Coppolino et al., 2017 ). 
5. Discussion 
In the literature review DoS/DDoS, session hijacking and shared 
technologies are the top three discussed security concerns in the 
cloud computing environment ( Agarwal et al., 2020; Yan et al., 
2014 ). According to the research results, scalability is one of the 
core cloud characteristics, such that a user can request more resources based on a given workload. That means, on one hand, a 
cloud system provides scalability to users, on the other hand, it

to user data. Utilizing a malware, the attacker alters the token to 
access the required info. Having a successful implementation, the 
attacker has data access from any machine. Bouayad et al. (2012) Freet et al. (2015) Isharufe et al. (2020) 
Chawki et al. (2018) 
Malicious insiders In this attack, one of the CSP employees accesses confidential data 
and utilizes collected information in a malicious way. This attack 
defines one of the most dangerous vulnerabilities. Coppolino et al. (2017) Krishna et al. (2016) Chawki et al. (2018) 
Data breach The possibility of data leakage increases in the cloud environment. 
Shared technologies and multitenancy are some examples of cloud 
technologies that raise the data breach probability. Coppolino et al. (2017) Khan and Al-Yasiri (2016) 
supports inexpensive resources for intruders and attackers to target other systems ( Coppolino et al., 2017; Deshmukh and Devadkar, 
2015 ). The second most discussed issue is the shared technology 
that makes the cloud so fascinating and is also a point of criticality in terms of security ( Coppolino et al., 2017 ). In a shared environment, if attackers succeed in compromising the HV, they can 
take control over the host system due to the HV root privilege. 
Apart from this, a session hijacking vulnerability is also in the top 
three issues in the cloud where a legitimate host can lose control 
over its own system, allowing intruders to compromise security requirements such as confidentiality, availability, and integrity of the 
deployed services in the host system. Table 6 depicts top security 
concerns in the literature. The results indicate that the lower layer 
vulnerability is more important and malicious insiders are one of 
the unexplored issues in the cloud system. The main contribution 
of this study is to provide a detailed understanding of the security 
vulnerabilities in the service-based cloud model. Moreover, previous studies have discussed countermeasures or vulnerabilities separately, which is fairly difficult to identify the solution for a particular vulnerability. That is why our result describes a classified 
table that has been produced to provide a pair of security countermeasure information. 
6. Future research directions 
Based on our literature search, most studies analyzed IaaS and 
SaaS security issues and there is limited research on PaaS vulnerabilities. Unfortunately, those limited number of studies poorly address countermeasures or a framework to solve security flaws. It 
intuitively implies either the model is less popular among others, 
or it has fewer known vulnerabilities in the community. However, 
according to the current software development trend, study of the 
security concerns of this layer requires more effort. 
6.1. Transparent policy compliance 
The cloud computing model suffers from a lack of coherent 
policies in terms of security and service compatibility. Vendors 
have no integrated instructions either for security policies or service delivery constraints. As discussed, the lock-in issue for PaaS customers arises due to the mentioned limitations of the model. 
Working on a universal security schema in the cloud platform is 
an essential future direction that necessarily improves the service 
quality and security status ( Kritikos et al., 2017 ). 
6.2. Cloud-based hardware security concerns 
Well-known companies, such as Intel, are working on 
hardware-level protection concepts such as the aforementioned 
technology, SGX; however, these technologies are still experimental and need more investigation. In addition, most of them 
are deployable in the traditional computing model. Cloud-based 
hardware is still developing and requires more research and 
enhancement effort s ( Coppolino et al., 2017 ). 
6.3. Exploiting vulnerabilities in virtualization technologies 
The other important finding of this study is that the literature is focused on the vulnerabilities of virtualization technologies. That is obviously due to the importance of the concept in 
the service-based cloud computing model. Although virtualization 
stands among well-studied vulnerabilities, in current research exploration, it was difficult to find a study investigating all possible 
scenarios. Virtualization is mainly classified into four categories as 
discussed. However, comprehensive surveys of virtualization vulnerabilities over class types were scarce in the literature. In the 
future, investigating the different security aspects of one vulnerability regarding various scenarios would add value in this context. 
The results of such investigations would help both CSPs and users 
to employ best practices.

7. Conclusion 
Our research studied the last decade of service-based cloud 
computing security issues through a comprehensive analysis of 
high-quality published papers. This study aimed to provide a summary of the current research status and establish a taxonomy that 
maps vulnerabilities to proper countermeasures. To this end, security vulnerabilities were categorized into four classes, IaaS, PaaS, 
SaaS, and generic. The first three classes discuss common issues in 
F. Khoda Parast, C. Sindhav, S. Nikam et al. Computers & Security 114 (2022) 102580 
each layer, while the generic category address vulnerabilities possible in all layers. Although the security concerns have more varieties, we tried to summarize the most discussed topics in the literature. 
According to the research results, DoS/DDoS, shared technology, and session hijacking were among the most addressed issues. 
As has been presented, DoS/DDoS attacks were the most frequent 
concern in the literature. A series of vulnerabilities are common 
network security issues that arise due to the medium of the cloud, 
such as DoS and MiTC. While others, e.g., multitenancy, are cloudspecific groups. As studies show, common issues would be a concern for more study regardless of purposes, service model, or architecture. The complexity of cloud architectures, in addition to 
service diversity and user configurations, could initiate new security threats. Currently, the variety of cloud services has increased 
to address any form of user requirements. Although it provides 
more flexibility, new security holes might be introduced for malicious activities. In the literature, most vulnerabilities are discussed 
in the general configuration or the traditional structure; however, 
both providers and customers need to be more conscious of risks 
and challenges associated with individual decided compositions.

1-s2.0-S0167404822004114-main.pdf.title: Security analysis and improvement of a public auditing scheme for secure data storage in fog-to-cloud computing
1-s2.0-S0167404822004114-main.pdf.abstract:
Human society has drastically changed as a result of the widespread application of Internet of Things (IoT) 
technology, particularly in various industrial fields. In spite of the advantages brought by IoT services, 
the underlying security challenges cannot be underestimated. Cloud-based IoT data collection poses a 
number of challenges, including how to ensure its integrity. In order to address this issue, Tian et al. 
have proposed a fog-to-cloud computing-based public auditing scheme in IoT scenarios, which provides 
the data-privacy preserving mechanism and tag transformation strategy. However, in this paper, we show 
that Tian et al.'s scheme fails to achieve soundness, an essential security property, by giving two attacks. 
In the first attack, the malicious cloud server can delete all the data and then deceive the Third Party 
Auditor (TPA) into believing that the data is well-preserved. In the second attack, the malicious cloud 
server can modify the outsourced data and then deceive TPA into believing that all the data is kept intact. 
We further provide a simple but effective solution for Tian et al.'s scheme to resist the above-mentioned 
attacks. Security analysis and performance evaluation are also given to demonstrate the robustness and 
high efficiency of the improved scheme.
1-s2.0-S0167404822004114-main.pdf.intro_text:
1. Introduction 
Internet of Things (IoT) consists of automatic sensors embedded 
in various physical devices. By utilizing and analyzing the device 
data collected from widespread distributed sensors, IoT technology 
makes it possible for people to interact with the objects around 
us and provides us a more convenient life. While enjoying the 
convenience brought by IoT technology, what follows is the challenge of transforming and processing an unprecedented amount 
of data generated constantly by geographically distributed IoT devices. Considering the advantage of tremendous computation and 
storage resources, cloud computing has been applied into sorts of 
IoT scenarios, such as data analysis, data mining and data storage, 
as a promising technology solution. However, due to the inherently 
high centralized feature of cloud computing itself, it is inevitable 
that all the outsourced data need to be centrally transmitted to the 
remote center server via the network. As a result, while responding to multiple service requests simultaneously, regional network 
E-mail addresses: wfzhang@swjtu.edu.cn (W. Zhang), jiaoheng_x@163.com 
(H. Jiao), yzq0622@my.swjtu.edu.cn (Z. Yan), xmwang@swjtu.edu.cn (X. Wang), 
mkhurram@KSU.EDU.SA (M.K. Khan) . congestion occurs from time to time, which leads to slow response 
of service requests and low-quality service experience. 
Therefore, fog computing, as an important complement of 
cloud computing, is recently attracting considerable interest of researchers. With computing resources moving from distant cloud 
server to fog nodes located at the edge of data source, fog computing is capable of processing data in real time and responding 
service request with low time-delay under special application scenarios, (such as Driverless car, Augmented reality, etc.). In a typical 
fog-to-cloud computing architecture, all the original data generated 
by kinds of distributed sensors will firstly be sent to the local fog 
nodes for data pre-processing, and then outsourced to the remote 
cloud server for further analysis and storage. 
In spite of all aspects of promising prospects, fog-to-cloud 
computing is not free of security challenges. How to ensure the 
integrity of outsourced data stored in the untrusted servers is a 
fundamental issue. For chasing financial benefit or protecting reputation itself, cloud servers may try to convince users that all the 
outsourced data is remained intact, while parts of data possibly 
has been tampered or corrupted. At the very worst case, cloud 
server can even delete the data user rarely accessed for saving its 
storage resource. To guarantee the integrity of data in the cloud, 
there is a vast amount of literatures on Data Integrity Checking. 
W. Zhang, H. Jiao, Z. Yan et al. Computers & Security 125 (2023) 103019 
Ateniese et al. (2007) proposed a data integrity checking protocol in untrusted remote cloud environment based on the notion of 
provable data possession (PDP). With the help of using homomorphic verifiable tag (HVT), the protocol enables data owners perform 
data integrity checking without downloading their original data, 
which greatly decreases the communication overhead. At the same 
time, Juels and Kaliski (2007) explored the proofs of retrievability (POR) scheme to achieve data integrity checking for large files 
in cloud. This method makes use of error correction and random 
permutation so that data owner can detect the changes of stored 
files. However, these schemes can only support static files for integrity verifying. When encountering the situation that the stored 
files in cloud are completely dynamic, adaptive changes are needed 
for these schemes. To support data dynamics, various approaches 
are presented ( Chen et al., 2020; He et al., 2021; Jiang et al., 
2016; Shen et al., 2017; Sookhak et al., 2018; Wang et al., 2011 ; 
Shacham and Waters, 2013 ). Many practical data structures applied 
in computer storage field have been reused to design data integrity 
checking protocol, such as divide and conquer tables, Merkel hash 
tree and doubly linked info table. Moreover, focusing on dynamic 
sensitive data sharing scenario, a mass of data integrity checking protocols with privacy preserving and group user revocation 
have been proposed by researchers. To protect user identity privacy 
from cloud server, Li et al. (2019) adopted the core idea of Fuzzy 
signature to design their scheme. Shen et al. (2019) made use of 
the idea in sanitizable signature to hide sensitive information from 
other group members. By introducing a third party, named sanitizer, into system, the sensitive information and its verification 
tags can be converted into uniform format. In Sun et al. (2020) ; 
Tian et al. (2019) ; Wang et al. (2014) ; Zhao et al. (2019) , authors 
also took different methods to achieve user information privacy 
preserving, like random mask encryption, ring signature and so on. 
Moreover, to handle the problem of user revocation in dynamic 
group under cloud data sharing scenario, Wang et al. (2015) proposed a novel scheme, named Panda. By using the proxy resignatures, Panda greatly reduces the communication and computation overhead on the user side. From another perspective, 
Zhang et al. (2020) raised an efficient solution to realize user revocation by updating the private keys of non-revoked users. Compared to Panda, the computation overhead in the whole system has 
been further reduced. 
Nevertheless, all the aforementioned schemes are not useable 
in practical IoT scenarios. The main limitation of those protocols 
is two-fold. firstly, unlike data generated by certain users in cloud 
environment, the data sources are various sensors in the wireless network. Then, difference in the strategy of tagging between 
fog-to-cloud computing paradigm and cloud computing paradigm 
produces. Secondly, the fog nodes have not been taken into consideration during the whole stage of data processing in former 
protocols, while under real IoT scenarios fog nodes play an irreplaceable role, such as caching necessary local content, aiding in 
pre-processing for real data stream. Therefore, it is imperative and 
urgent to design a suitable data integrity checking protocol in fogto-cloud computing for real IoT scenarios. 
Tian et al. (2019) first proposed a public auditing scheme for 
fog-to-cloud computing, which both achieves the tag transforming 
strategy between the mobile sinks (seen as data sources) and fog 
nodes and perfect data privacy-preserving against TPA. To tackle 
the problem that authenticatable tag of metadata generated by 
different mobile sinks with different keys, they designed a novel 
tag transforming strategy, which converts tags produced by mobile 
sinks to the ones produced by fog nodes. Specially, fog nodes will 
resign the semi-finished authenticatable tag received from mobile 
sinks and then forward it to cloud sever together with its personal 
authenticatable tags. On the other hand, a zero-knowledge proof 
mechanism is introduced during the verification phase. Based on
1-s2.0-S0167404822004114-main.pdf.conclusion_text:
6. Conclusion 
In this paper, we perform a cryptanalysis on a tailor-made public auditing scheme in a fog-to-cloud computing environment proposed by Tian et al. and present an improvement on its security. 
By revealing two potential attacks, we point out the fact that Tian 
et al.'s scheme fails to achieve soundness as it claimed. Specifically, both data forging attack and data deleting attack launched 
by malicious cloud sever can convince data owner that all the data 
is stored correctly and intactly while any data corruption accident 
cannot be detected by TPA. Therefore, the attacks above make the 
scheme of Tian et al. (2019) inadequate to meet the security requirements of public auditing schemes. We then designed an applicable authenticatable tag as the countermeasure to those attacks 
while keeping original properties of the basic scheme. Finally, security analysis and performance evaluation are conducted to show 
the practicality of our improved scheme. 
CRediT author statement 
Wenfang Zhang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing original draft, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Resources, Software, Supervision. Heng Jiao: Solution design & verification, Data curation, Writing - original draft. Zhuoqun Yan: 
Investigation, Formal analysis, Validation, Visualization. Xiaomin 
Wang: Writing - review & editing, Funding acquisition. Muhammad Khurram Khan: Writing - review & editing. 
W. Zhang, H. Jiao, Z. Yan et al. Computers & Security 125 (2023) 103019 
Declaration of Competing Interest 
The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to 
influence the work reported in this paper. 
Data Availability 
Data will be made available on request. 
Acknowledgments 
This work was supported in part by the National Natural Science Foundation of China under Grant 61872302 ; in part by the 
Key program for Sichuan Science and Technology under Grants 
2019YFH0097 and 2019YFG0502.

In this paper, we investigate the details of Tian et al. (2019) and 
analyze its security. Moreover, we point out two potential attacks, and then corresponding countermeasures are given to 
prevent those two attacks. Our contributions can be summed 
up as follows.We show that the cryptographical construction in 
Tian et al. (2019) fails to achieve the fundamental security property in data integrity checking schemes. We demonstrate that their 
scheme is vulnerable to a malicious server which is capable of 
launching two grave attacks, i.e., data replacing attack and data 
deleting attack. In the first attack, while part of challenge data 
blocks is corrupted, a malicious server can use some other wellmaintained data blocks to forge a valid proof, and then pass the 
verification. In the latter attack, a malicious server can even delete 
all the outsourced data and only store the parameters that are 
needed. Furthermore, those two attacks launched by a malicious 
server will not be detected by TPA or other auditors, and data 
owner may always believe that data remains intact in cloud until 
it is retrieved. We then present an improved protocol to tackle the 
vulnerability discussed above and then prove that our new protocol is secure against above-mentioned attacks. 
2. Review of Tian et al.'s protocol 
In this section, we provide a comprehensive over of the Tian 
et al.'s protocol including the system model. 
2.1. System model 
As shown in Fig. 1 , there are five entities in this protocol: IoT 
devices, mobile sinks, fog nodes, cloud server and third-party auditor (TPA). IoT devices are seen as the sensor data producers, which 
produce data and send them to the mobile sinks. Mobile sinks, 
responsible for collecting data, are considered as the actual data 
sources, which will also generate semi authenticatable tags for received data before forwarding them to fog nodes. Fog nodes play 
two roles. They first verify the correctness of the received data before further processing and then generate the final authenticatable 
tag on the basis of semi authenticatable tag. All the data with final authenticatable tag will be transmitted to the cloud servers, 
which provide storage and computing services in a scalable way. 
TPA plays a role in checking the integrity of the outsourced data 
regularly on behalf of the data owner.

3. Security analysis of Tian et al.'s protocol 
In this section, we first demonstrate the weakness in scheme 
of Tian et al. Then, we present two potential attacks that pose a 
serious threat to the security feature of soundness owing to an 
improper-designed tag generation strategy. In the first attack, while 
parts of challenged blocks are corrupted, a malicious cloud server 
can forge a valid authenticatable tag of any fog node without its 
private key and then generate a forgeable response obtained from 
some tamper data blocks to pass the verification. In the second attack, a malicious cloud server only stores some parameters but not 
the outsourced data and then convinces the TPA that all the data 
is preserved intact. Consequently, Tian et al. (2019) cannot satisfy 
the security requirements of public auditing schemes any longer. 
In the following, we explain the weakness and the two attacks in 
detail. 
W. Zhang, H. Jiao, Z. Yan et al. Computers & Security 125 (2023) 103019 
3.1. Weakness 
In Tian et al. (2019) , the authenticatable tag σq,j is generated 
as σq,j = (u H 1 (id q,j ) ·g m q,j ) fsk v . However, due to the improper construction of authenticatable tag, i.e., a specific generator gwhich 
has been used to generated public keys of all the entities (mobile sinks and fog nodes) is reused to generate authenticatable tag. 
We found that a malicious sever can obtain useful knowledge from 
the authenticatable tag to reconstruct any valid authenticatable tag 
without corresponding private key. By manipulating this forged authenticatable tag, the cloud server can forge a valid response and 
launches its strikes. 
We show how a malicious cloud server completes its preparatory work before its attack as follows:

4. An improved protocol 
In this section, we intend to revamp Tian et al. (2019) which 
suffers security attacks. We modify the way of generating data 
blocks authenticatable tag to avert degeneration of the effect of 
data encryption, such that the adversary sever can no longer obtain 
βv by leveraging authenticatable tag. Then we demonstrate how 
the protocol with improved authenticatable tag resists the attacks. 
4.1. Improved protocol details 
Intuitively, Tian et al. (2019) can be amended in a variety of 
way to achieve a standard level of security. On the one hand, considering that both attacks are executed successfully with the help 
of βv , one way to toughen security soundness is to modify the way 
of generating data blocks authenticatable tag, which prevents any 
adversary server from getting useful knowledge from authenticatable tag to forge valid response. On the other hand, those attacks 
can also be avoided if we change the sequencing of basic protocol 
execution, i.e., let TPA derive the response /Theta1from /Phi1i , /Omega1i and /Psi1
instead the cloud server. Then TPA executes the Verify algorithm 
to verify the correctness of proof from the cloud server. 
In the latter case, however, changing the sequencing of protocol 
execution may contradict with the original idea in basic protocol. 
The cloud server is required to transfer a great deal of proof information and TPA has to consume a lot of computing resources, 
which increases the complexity of the whole system. Therefore, we 
give a solution to the weakness by modifying the way of generating data blocks authenticatable tag while both the communication 
costs and computational overhead stay the same as the initial protocol. We describe the details related to concrete improvement in 
the following. 
As the major differences between the new protocol and the initial protocol are the TagGenMS, TagGenF and Verify algorithms, 
other algorithms are omitted because they are the same as the initial protocol.

4.2. Security analysis 
In this subsection, we prove that our improved scheme is secure against data replacing attack and data deleting attack, and the 
Theorem 1 (Resistance of data replacing attack and data deleting 
attack) . The improved scheme using revised authenticatable tag 
can resist data replacing attack and data deleting attack. 
Proof. As discussed above, the malicious sever executes its strikes 
successfully in Tian et al.'s protocol with the help of βv derived 
from publicly known authenticatable tag, where 
βv = (β/prime 
v ) (H 1 (id q,j )) -1 =u H 1 (id q,j ) ·fsk v ·(H 1 (id q,j )) -1 =u fsk v . 
In our improved protocol, the authenticatable tag is generated as 
σq,j = ( H 2 (id q,j ) ·u m q,j ) fsk v . Considering that the malicious sever 
wants to forge a valid response, it first attempts to recover βv from 
σq,j by computing βv = u fsk v = σq,j ·H 2 (id q,j ) -fsk v ·u -m q,j . However, fsk v is the private key only possessed by each fog node,

which means that it is impossible for the malicious sever to 
recover βv from σq,j . In other words, the malicious sever cannot launch its strikes anymore in our improved protocol without 
βv . /square

5. Performance evaluation 
In this section, we first give a numerical computational costs 
analysis of the improved scheme, and then evaluate the performance of the improved scheme by extensive experiments. 
5.1. Computational costs 
We list all the necessary notations used to describe operations 
of our improved scheme in Table 1 . We analyze the computational 
cost mainly involved in tag generation phase and proof verification 
phase. To produce an authenticatable tag for a block, a fog node 
needs to perform (2 ·E + 1 ·M + 1 ·H 2 ) operations. It sometimes 
also has to compute extra signatures that are used by cloud sever 
to check the integrity of received data blocks, but we don't count 
this extra computation overhead into the total computational cost, 
as this step is actually intended for achieving higher security in 
cloud system. To verify the correctness of the proof, TPA first computes H v ,which brings (c ·E + c ·H 2 ) operations. And then TPA conducts (t ·E + t ·P + 1 ·H 3 ) operations to verify the final equation. 
We compare the computational cost in our revised scheme with 
the basic Tian et al.'s scheme ( Tian et al., 2019 ) and the scheme Table 1

(hereinafter, Panda) in Wang et al. (2015) . The protocol named 
Panda in Wang et al. (2015) considers a data sharing scenario in 
which all the data blocks are created, modified by multiple users. 
The nature of the way of auditing data integrity by aggregating 
challenged blocks belonging to different users in Panda is the same

costs of abovementioned schemes. 
5.2. Experimental results 
In this subsection, we evaluate the performance of our improved scheme and give a performance comparison with the other 
two schemes involved in Table 2 . All the experiments are conducted on a personal computer with Intel I7-3770 3.4 GHz processor, 8 GB memory and Ubuntu operating system. In such an ideal 
environment, we assume that the communication delay between 
parties does not exist and the error rate of data transmission between parties can be ignored. The Charm-Crypto Library is used in 
the experiments, and the security parameter is set to be 160 bits. 
All experiment results are the mean of 15 trials. 
We simulate the time cost of tag generation over the three 
schemes under two different scenarios: with different number of 
data blocks for various data streams and with different sizes of a 
data block. 
In the first case, by varying the number of data blocks from 
10 0 0 to 50,0 0 0, where the size of each data block is 4 KB, we simulate the time cost of generating authenticatable tag, and Fig. 2 
represents the experimental results. As shown in Fig. 2 , the time 
for processing tag in our improved scheme and Panda is a bit lower 
than that in basic Tian et al. scheme. For example, for a file with 
30,0 0 0 data blocks, the time cost in our improved scheme and 
Panda is about 1203 s and 1207 s, while the fog node in basic Tian

scenarios than the basic Tian et al. scheme, such as Augmented Reality, Self-driving, etc. 
In our experiments, we select different number of challenged 
data blocks corresponding to different detection probability to 
measure the time cost for proof verification. For instance, to 
achieve 99 % detection probability in auditing task with a file that 
has 1 % data error rate, we need to challenge C data blocks each 
time, in which C satisfies 1 -(1 -0 . 01) c ≥0 . 99 . When we set the 
detection probability 99 % , the number of challenged data blocks 
should be 460. When we set the detection probability 90 % , the 
number of challenged data blocks should be 230 and so forth. The 
experimental results of time cost at TPA side for detection probability varying from 60 % to 99 % are shown in Fig. 4 . We can learn 
that for the detection probability 99 % , the time cost of TPA in our 
improved scheme and Tian et al.'s scheme is 18.56 s and 17.94 s, 
respectively, while the relevant time cost in Panda is 25.3 s. It 
means that the performance of our improved scheme is improved 
by 26.6 % . The reason is that the size of proof in Panda is far larger 
than that in other two schemes, which leads to extra exponentiation operations. To put it another way, the communication cost 
in other two schemes is also significantly reduced compared with 
Panda. 
Remarks . Based on Table 2, Figs. 3 and 4 , while toughening the 
security of Tian et al. (2019) , our improved scheme achieves rela- Fig. 4. Time cost for proof verification. 
tively better efficiency. In other words, our new scheme not only 
repairs the weakness in Tian et al. (2019) and resists the potential 
attacks, but also keeps the original properties of the basic scheme.

6. Conclusion 
In this paper, we perform a cryptanalysis on a tailor-made public auditing scheme in a fog-to-cloud computing environment proposed by Tian et al. and present an improvement on its security. 
By revealing two potential attacks, we point out the fact that Tian 
et al.'s scheme fails to achieve soundness as it claimed. Specifically, both data forging attack and data deleting attack launched 
by malicious cloud sever can convince data owner that all the data 
is stored correctly and intactly while any data corruption accident 
cannot be detected by TPA. Therefore, the attacks above make the 
scheme of Tian et al. (2019) inadequate to meet the security requirements of public auditing schemes. We then designed an applicable authenticatable tag as the countermeasure to those attacks 
while keeping original properties of the basic scheme. Finally, security analysis and performance evaluation are conducted to show 
the practicality of our improved scheme.

1-s2.0-S0167404824000749-main.pdf.title: Crayfish optimization algorithm for improving cyber security in cloud computing
1-s2.0-S0167404824000749-main.pdf.abstract:
The decentralized and distributed architecture of cloud computing promotes adoption and growth in various 
societal domains, including education, government, information technology, business, entertainment. Cloud 
computing (CC) makes a broad range of information technologies available. Security and privacy are key 
challenges in storing big data in the cloud. To overcome this challenge, a Self-Attention Conditional Generative 
Adversarial Network Optimised with Crayfish Optimization Algorithm for Improving Cyber Security in Cloud 
Computing (CybS-CC-SACGAN -COA) is proposed in this paper to enhance the safety of CC environment. The 
input data is amassed from NSL-KDD database. After that, the data is fed to pre-processing segment. The segment 
of pre-processing eliminates Cloud data termination and missing value replacement using Reformed Phase 
Conserving Vibrant Range Compression filter. The results of pre-processing serves for feature selection. The 
optimal features are selected by means of Manta Ray Foraging Optimization Algorithm (MRFOA). Cloud data is 
categorized as normal and abnormal data, like Denial-of-Service (DoS), Probe, Remote to local attack (R2L), User 
to root attack (U2R) by the help of SACGAN. Crayfish Optimization Algorithm (COA) is proposed to optimize the 
SACGAN classifier that classifies anomaly data precisely. The proposed CSCC-SACGAN -COA technique is 
activated in Python under some metrics. The proposed CSCC-SACGAN -COA approach has attained higher 
detection accuracy, lower computation time, higher AUC, higher scalability and lower detection error rate 
compared to the existing methods.
keywords: Manta ray foraging optimization algorithm and
self-attention conditional generative adversa rial network
1-s2.0-S0167404824000749-main.pdf.intro_text:
1.Introduction 
The term "cloud computing" symbolizes internet-based computing 
that uses virtual shared servers and workstations to supply platforms, 
software, infrastructure, and resources. This type of computing is 
constantly growing (Mayuranathan et al., 2021 ). Cloud computing is 
susceptible to attacks, because of its open structure. The success of cloud 
computing depends on privacy and security (Rajan and Naganathan, 
2022 ; Lou et al., 2021 ). Traditional security protocols cannot address 
this issue. Attacks like the Sybil attack and the Denial of Service (DoS) 
are inevitable (SobinSoniya and Maria Celestin Vigila, 2022 ). The 
detection of hostile network activity is among the most important and 
longstanding cyber security issues. The complexity of security-related 
issues is rising due to the spread of networks and various technologies being used (Kholidy, 2021 ; SobinSoniya and Maria Celestin Vigila, 
2022 ; Srilatha and Shyam, 2021 ). Furthermore, odd network behavior is 
misconstrued as a symptom of a compromised device or network. From 
disseminated computing Cloud computing is progressed the practice of 
the cloud increases and the number of clients shares single to the cloud 
resources by using consistent inaccessibility technologies (Prabhakaran 
and Kulandasamy, 2021 ; Mehanovi ˘c et al., 2021 ; Guezzaz et al., al.). It 
causes an improvement of traffic network malevolent occurrences 
becoming more extensive and difficult (Vu et al., 2022 ). Cloud 
computing contains many benefits such as; scalability of instance, quick 
elasticity, dignified services the most important key feature are the assistances of cost savings to enterprises and also using on-demand services in virtual manner through internet (Selvapandian and Santhosh, 
2021 ; Krishnaveni et al., 2021 ). It is difficult to predict the assets in 
u{�~zkw! s{yo|kr o>!ÐÐÐ1ow�o �to~1m{y2w{m k�o2m{�o!
2cloud and also the Communication between them, hence quantity of 
intrusions increasing gradually (Rajagopal et al., 2021 ). Nowadays, reports of statistical shows excellent number of securities violates occur in 
cloud computing of virtual network layer (Mondal and Goswami, 2021 ; 
Ghosh et al., 2021 ). Intrusion detection scheme (IDS) is the widely used 
part of computer system safety processes for safeguarding cloud environs from cyber-attacks (El Kafhali et al., 2022 ; Tissir et al., 2021 ; 
Hassaoui et al., 2024 ; Hassaoui et al., 2023 ). To keep track of the 
development of computer-related crimes, intrusion detection systems 
(IDS) employ a variety of reaction mechanisms to find weaknesses, spot 
illicit activity, and take preventative action (Turcato et al., 2021 ). Cyber 
security systems (CSS) in platform of cloud tools and techniques are 
available to identifying attacks on cloud setup (Qureshi et al., 2021 ; 
Ogwara et al., 2021 ; Samunnisa et al., 2023 ). Due to heavy traffic and its 
changes of behavior traditional CSS is turn out ineffective and ineffi cient. To form a good security solution CSS use firewall (Dehghani et al., 
2022 ). 
Modern and sophisticated technologies are essential to estimate the 
massive volume of data created from the interactions among the devices 
of cloud computing systems because of the complexity and size of the 
distributed infrastructure of cloud computing networks. Real-time 
analysis of the complex data generated is necessary for the develop ment of robust intrusion detection systems. Therefore, deep learning 
techniques are promising for the analysis of large data, consisting of 
traffic flows in cloud networks for indicators of intrusions, even if they 
have not been detected before. These are motivated to do this research. 
An anomaly-based network intrusion detection scheme is proposed 
that can track and examine the network traffics flow targeting cloud 
environments. The nature of these traffics should be reported to the 
network administrator so that any obtrusive network connections can be 
dropped and blocked. SACGAN is considered as a classifier of the 
network connections. The most pertinent network features are chosen 
using the binary-based MRFOA, and the SACGAN control parameters are 
adjusted using the standard-based COA. The proposed system is assessed 
using network data from the benchmark NSL-KDD dataset. The outcomes shows that the proposed method is identified the intrusive 
network connections with maximal accuracy with less false alarm rates 
(FARs). 
The primary contributions of this paper are abridged below: 
≡CybS-CC-SACGAN -COA is proposed for protecting CC environment. 
≡The feature selection and categorization method for IDS is explored. 
Proposed a feature selection strategy for intrusion identification 
based on the MRFOA. 
≡The proposed method categorizes network attacks by employing 
SACGAN, which classifies the data into DoS, Probe, R2L, U2R, 
Normal (no attack). 
≡Crayfish Optimization Algorithm (COA) is proposed to optimize the 
SACGAN classifier, which precisely categorized by the CC Intrusion. 
≡The efficacy of the proposed method is analyzed with existing CybS- 
CC-BPNN-ArBCOA, CybS-CC-DKNN, CybS-CC-DNN-GTOA and CybS- 
CC-WDBiLSTM-GWO methods. 
Remaining paper is structured as below: the recent studies are 
depicted in section II, section III describes the proposed method, section 
IV proves the result, section V presents the conclusion.
1-s2.0-S0167404824000749-main.pdf.conclusion_text:
5.Conclusion 
In this paper, CybS-CC-SACGAN -COA is proposed. The relevant 
features were selected for improving the training speed and decreasing 
the computational time of the technique. The proposed CybS-CC-SAC -
GAN-COA approach is executed in Python under performance metrics. 
The proposed CybS-CC-SACGAN -COA approach attains 19.08 %, 29.63 
%, 31.05 %, and 35.45 % higher Precision; 24.16 %, 27.14 %, 18.42 %, 
and 19.03 % higher recall; 21.15 %, 25.29 %, 26.15 %, and 30.46 % 
higher throughput than the existing methods, like CybS-CC-BPNN- 
ArBCOA, CybS-CC-DKNN, CybS-CC-DNN-GTOA and CybS-CC- 
WDBiLSTM-GWO respectively. 
While the proposed anomaly-based NIDS utilizing Crayfish Optimi zation Algorithm and Self-Attention Conditional Generative Adversarial 
Network has demonstrated promising results, it is significant to 
acknowledge some limitations inherent in the current study that is, the 
efficacy of the proposed NIDS is contingent on the quality and repre sentativeness of the training dataset (NSL-KDD). The inherent biases and 
limitations within the dataset may impact the generalizability of the 
model to real-world cloud environments. The proposed IDS assume a 
static network environment during the training and testing phases. The 
impact of dynamic network conditions, such as fluctuations in traffic 
volume and topology changes, on the system 's performance needs 
further investigation. 
Conducting experiments in real-world cloud environments will provide valuable insights into the adaptability and performance of the 
proposed NIDS in practical scenarios. This involves collaboration with 
cloud service providers for data access and validation. Exploring the 
integration of multiple optimization techniques, beyond COA, to optimize the control parameters of SACGAN. This may involve exper imenting with hybrid optimizations, transfer learning techniques etc.

Sahaya Stalin Jose. G: Methodology. G. Sugitha: Supervision. 
Ayshwarya Lakshmi. S: Supervision. Preethi Bangalore Chaluvaraj: 
Supervision. 
Declaration of competing interest 
The authors declare that they have no known competing financial 
interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence 
the work reported in this paper 
Data availability 
No data was used for the research described in the article.

2.Related work 
The recent works related to deep learning based intrusion identifi cation of cloud environment are detailed in this section. 
Wen et al., (Wen, 2022 ) have suggested CC intrusion detection 
technology basis on BP-NN. Initially, intrusion cloud dataset was taken 
from KDD cup dataset and it was given to Cyber security system. In 
Cyber security system, back propagation neural network were exploited 
for categorizing the cloud data as normal or anomalous data. Consequently, the back propagation neural network was enhanced by 
utilizing the artificial bee colony optimization technique. It presents 
better results under F-Score, but it increases the computation time. 
Mayuranathan et al., (Mayuranathan et al., 2022 ) have presented 
efficient optimal security system for intrusion recognization in CC utilizing hybrid deep learning model. Mainly the cloud data was collected 
and transferred to the pre-processing section. The Pre-processing 
segment, improved heap optimization technique were exploited to 
improving the data quality by eliminating unwanted data. The features 
of optimal were fed Kronecker of DNN classify the intruders. It presents 
better results under sensitivity, but it decreases the precision value. 
Balamurugan et al., (Balamurugan et al., 2022 ) have presented 
Network optimization utilizing defender scheme in cloud computing 
safety basis intrusion identification scheme along game theory DNN 
(IDSGT-DNN). Primarily, the intrusion cloud dataset was taken and 
given to Cyber security system. In Cyber security system, DNN was 
exploited for the categorization of cloud data as normal and anomalous 
data. In general, DNN has not rapid any adoption of optimization 
techniques tomeasure appropriate parameters to ensure a certain categorization to computing of cloud intrusion. So, game theory optimiza tion technique was exploited to enhance the DNN. The presented 
method provides better results in terms of accuracy, but it provides low 
area under curve value. 
Geetha and Deepa, (Geetha and Deepa, 2022 ) have presented 
FKPCA-GWO WDBiLSTM classifier IDS in environments of cloud. 
Initially, the intrusion cloud dataset was collected and sent to the Fisher 
kernel based Principal of Component Analysis dimensionality reducing 
algorithm by choosing the optimal features. The chosen optimal features 
were provided to gray wolf optimizer established weight dropped 
BiLSTM classifier to classifying the cloud intruders. It provides better 
result in terms of specificity, but it increases the detection error rate. 
Kumar et al., (Kumar et al., 2022 ) have presented intrusion identi fication with prevention of CC: From perspective of deep learning. 
Initially, intrusion cloud dataset was taken and it was given to CSS. In 
cyber security system, Fuzzy Min Max Neural Networks-Based Scheme 
was utilized for classification of intruders. It gives better results in term 
precision but it decreases the sensitivity. 
Jain et al., (Jain et al., 2022 ) have presented Training fuzzy deep 
neural network along honey badger algorithm (HBA) in CC for intrusion 
identification. The presented study suggest a novel fuzzy deep neural 
network (FDNN) for cloud environments called FDNN -HBA was 
developed in conjunction with the Honey Bader Algorithm to provide 
privacy-preserving intrusion identification. An efficient training method 
was used for intrusion identification and categorization using the FDNN 
model. The presented method provides better sensitivity but increased 
computation time. 
Thilagam and Aruna (Thilagam and Aruna, 2021 ) have presented 
Intrusion detection for network based CC by custom RC-NN with 
optimization. Initially, intrusion of cloud dataset was taken and it given 
to Cyber security system. RCNN with LSTM and the Ant Lion optimi zation algorithm were utilized in cyber security systems to classify cloud 
data as normal and abnormal. The presented method lowers the F-Score 
but yields better area under the curve results. Table 1 depicts the related 
studies comparision. 
3.Proposed methodology 
In this manuscript, the CybS-CC-SACGAN -COA is proposed to 
improve the security on cloud computing. The block diagram of CybS- 
CC-SACGAN -COA is shown in Fig. 1.It comprises data acquisition, preprocessing, feature selection, cloud cyber security system and optimi zation. Description of the detailed proposed methodology is given 
below, S.S. Jose. G et al.

(NSL-KDD dataset ). This database is an improved version of KDD cup

encompasses copious interference that are playacts in the cloud. There 
are four main categories of abnormal and several normal records in the

DoS, Probe, R2L attack, U2R, Normal. There are 41 features from 
various data packet and 1 class label for every data packet. Table 2

Probe is an exhibition that exertions to contract data from a cloud

Nmap, Portsweep, saint and mscan.

R2L is an indicator that endeavours to acquire restricted communi cation with a remote machine. An invader could not locally investiture

4.Results with discussion 
The experimental outcomes about proposed CybS-CC-SAC -
GAN-COA are discussed to enhance security on the cloud environment. 
The experiments are performed in laptop along Intel core i7 CPU, 4GB 
RAM, 64-bit Windows 10 OS. NSL-KDD dataset is taken for carried out 
these experiments. The proposed technique is simulated in Python using 
several performance measures. The outcomes of proposed CybS-CC- 
SACGAN -COA are analyzed with existing methods like CybS-CC-BPNN- 
ArBCOA (Wen, 2022 ), CybS-CC-DKNN (Mayuranathan et al., 2022 ), 
CybS-CC-DNN-GTOA (Balamurugan et al., 2022 ) and 
CybS-CC-WDBiLSTM-GWO (Samunnisa et al., 2023 ) methods. 4.1. Dataset description 
The efficiency of CybS-CC-SACGAN -COA is validated utilizing the 
NSL-KDD dataset. It includes 3,79,679 archives depicts four assaults 
including DoS, Probe, R2L, and U2R attacks, in addition to archives 
showing normal behavior (no attacks). In this, On behalf of training 50 
% data are taken then the remaining 50 % data for testing. 
4.2. Performance metrics 
This is a main task to select the ideal classifier. To analyze the performance, the given confusion matrix is necessary. 
≡True Positive (TP): intrusive properly characterized as intrusive. 
≡True Negative (TN): normal properly characterized as normal. 
≡False Positive (FP): normal wrongly characterized as intrusive. 
≡False Negative (FN): intrusive wrongly characterized as normal.

4.2.9. Detection latency 
Detection latency is a measure of the time it takes for IDS to detect 
and respond to a security incident. It represents the elapsed time from 
the occurrence of a security event to the moment the IDS identifies and 
generates an alert or takes an action. It is given in Eq. (32) 
Detection LatencyTD�TO (32) 
where, TD is the time at which the IDS detects the security incident, TO is 
the time at which the security event actually occurs. 
4.2.10. Throughput 
It measures the rate at which a system can complete a task or deliver 
a service over a specific period of time. In the context of network or 
communication systems, throughput often refers to the amount of data 
transferred successfully between two points in the network within a 
given timeframe. The general equation for throughput is given in Eq. 
(33)

effectiveness. It assesses how well the IDS can expand its capacity to 
accommodate growth without a significant degradation in performance. 
It is given in Eq. (34)

Resource consumption in the context of intrusion detection refers to the amount of computing resources, such as CPU, memory, and network 
bandwidth, utilized by the intrusion detection system (IDS) to perform 
its tasks. Managing resource consumption is crucial to ensure that the 
IDS effectively detects and responds to security threats without negatively impacting the overall performance of the cloud or network environment. It is expressed in Eq. (35)

Scalability. Here, the CybS-CC-SACGAN -COA technique attains 22.98 
%, 20.86 %, 29.34 % and 10.1 % higher Scalability evaluated to the 
existing CybS-CC-BPNN-ArBCOA, CybS-CC-DKNN, CybS-CC-DNN- 
GTOA and CybS-CC-WDBiLSTM-GWO models respectively. 
A count of data that can be transferred successfully over a network in 
a specific time frame is throughput. Fig. 6 shows the throughput analysis. Here, the proposed CybS-CC-SACGAN -COA technique attains 
28.98 %, 21.86 %, 24.34 % and 19.1 % higher throughput analyzed to 
the existing CybS-CC-BPNN-ArBCOA, CybS-CC-DKNN, CybS-CC-DNN- 
GTOA and CybS-CC-WDBiLSTM-GWO models respectively. 
FPR provides insights into the SACGAN 's tendency to generate false 
alarms, specifically for the negative class. A lower FPR indicates that the 
model is better at avoiding false positive errors, which is generally 
desirable in applications where false positives have higher conse quences. Fig. 7 shows the Performance analysis of False Positive Rate. 
Here, the CybS-CC-SACGAN -COA method attains least FPR value over 
the existing techniques. The proposed CybS-CC-SACGAN -COA technique achieves 27.05 %, 29.30 %, 24.70 %, and 21.52 % lower FPR for

R2L Attack; 25.30 %, 24.49 %, 24.09 % and 28.20 % lesser FPR for U2R 
Attack; 21.12 %, 29.98 %, 25.94 % and 22.41 % lesser FPR for Normal 
compared with existing method like CybS-CC-BPNN-ArBCOA, CybS-CC-

4.4. Discussion 
This paper proposes SACGAN optimised with Crayfish Optimization 
Algorithm to optimize SACGAN network algorithm. SACGAN model 
classifies the input data into DoS, Probe, R2L, U2R attacks and normal. 
The proposed method is contrasted with four other existing methodol ogies; they are CybS-CC-BPNN-ArBCOA, CybS-CC-DKNN, CybS-CC- 
DNN-GTOA and CybS-CC-WDBiLSTM-GWO. These models were tested 
on 37,129 samples of the NSL-KDD dataset after being trained on 
1,11,386 instances. Performance of intrusion detection accuracy is the

11primary evaluation. The experimental outcomes depicts that the identification of cloud computing intrusion attack samples, the proposed 
CybS-CC-SACGAN -COA approach has a high detection rate of 99.67 % 
and can successfully separate aberrant data from normal data. 
Furthermore, the average false alarm rate is a low 3.42 %. This proposed 
method has a higher RoC value of 0.9915, which is 2.8 % to 5.8 % higher 
than other models. Finally, the outcomes depict that the proposed 
technique identifying anomaly data effectually in Cloud Computing to 
improve Cyber Security.

5.Conclusion 
In this paper, CybS-CC-SACGAN -COA is proposed. The relevant 
features were selected for improving the training speed and decreasing 
the computational time of the technique. The proposed CybS-CC-SAC -
GAN-COA approach is executed in Python under performance metrics. 
The proposed CybS-CC-SACGAN -COA approach attains 19.08 %, 29.63 
%, 31.05 %, and 35.45 % higher Precision; 24.16 %, 27.14 %, 18.42 %, 
and 19.03 % higher recall; 21.15 %, 25.29 %, 26.15 %, and 30.46 % 
higher throughput than the existing methods, like CybS-CC-BPNN- 
ArBCOA, CybS-CC-DKNN, CybS-CC-DNN-GTOA and CybS-CC- 
WDBiLSTM-GWO respectively. 
While the proposed anomaly-based NIDS utilizing Crayfish Optimi zation Algorithm and Self-Attention Conditional Generative Adversarial 
Network has demonstrated promising results, it is significant to 
acknowledge some limitations inherent in the current study that is, the 
efficacy of the proposed NIDS is contingent on the quality and repre sentativeness of the training dataset (NSL-KDD). The inherent biases and 
limitations within the dataset may impact the generalizability of the 
model to real-world cloud environments. The proposed IDS assume a 
static network environment during the training and testing phases. The 
impact of dynamic network conditions, such as fluctuations in traffic 
volume and topology changes, on the system 's performance needs 
further investigation. 
Conducting experiments in real-world cloud environments will provide valuable insights into the adaptability and performance of the 
proposed NIDS in practical scenarios. This involves collaboration with 
cloud service providers for data access and validation. Exploring the 
integration of multiple optimization techniques, beyond COA, to optimize the control parameters of SACGAN. This may involve exper imenting with hybrid optimizations, transfer learning techniques etc.
